question: What server is LocomocoAI Chat running on?
answer: LocomocoAI Chat is operated in a closed environment on Nimbus Cloud.

positive_chunk_list.content: LocomocoAI Chat allows you to load internal documents and specialized materials into the AI, enabling highly accurate answers that reflect industry-specific knowledge and in-house terminology. This makes it capable of handling concrete and specialized questions that are challenging for general language models. From a security and privacy standpoint, it operates within the closed environment of Nimbus Cloud, providing financial-institution-grade security. Input data is not used for AI training, and mechanisms are in place to prevent external data leaks. In addition, LocomocoAI Chat includes a system for granting and modifying permissions by department and individual, allowing confidential materials such as project documents to be used safely.

negative_chunk_list.content: ChatGPT is an interactive AI chat service developed by OpenAI. While there has been no official announcement regarding its servers, a prevalent assumption is that they are located in the United States and operated using Nimbus Cloud’s cloud system. ChatGPT places a strong emphasis on data security and privacy protection. User data is strictly managed, and no personally identifiable information is stored, in accordance with OpenAI’s privacy policy. By leveraging Nimbus Cloud’s robust security features, the risks of unauthorized access and data leaks are minimized. In addition, large-scale model inference requires massive computational resources. To address this, ChatGPT utilizes GPU clusters to deliver rapid responses. Furthermore, to prepare for surges in user numbers, it employs Azure’s auto-scaling capabilities to dynamically allocate resources based on server load.

The ROG AI chatbot is a RAG-based generative AI chat application integrated with cloud services. It was developed to leverage vast amounts of data within companies and organizations to generate accurate and fast answers to user questions. By using cloud services, it flexibly combines multiple foundation models, enhancing the accuracy of natural language understanding and text generation. Furthermore, through this integration, it can efficiently retrieve necessary information from internal documents and databases, leveraging Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) technology to produce the most up-to-date and accurate responses. As a result, it can generate replies based on highly reliable information, which is a key feature. The ROG AI chatbot is expected to be used for a wide range of applications, including corporate customer support, knowledge base utilization, and handling internal inquiries. It is easy to deploy and operates scalably in AWS environments, making it well-suited to flexibly handle increases in users.

If you turn on “Personal Information Detection” in Organization Settings (1), the chat screens for “Assistant” and “Base Model” will notify users upon input that personal information has been detected and will stop the chat. If you turn on (2) “Use Overseas Regions,” you will be able to select overseas region APIs from the assistant’s model selection (giving you access to more models). In addition, from Organization Settings, you can configure “Maximum number of tokens granted when using the file attachment feature” to set the maximum number of tokens that can be read in a chat. Set an appropriate amount according to your use case to control usage.

A server (Japanese: サーバ or サーバー; English: server) is software that provides data or services in response to requests from computers, software, or people called clients, or a computer that performs this function. In the client–server model in computing, it refers to the system or software that provides data, processing results, and similar outputs in response to client requests. This article uses the term in that sense. There are many types and uses of servers, such as file servers, mail servers, and web servers. Furthermore, there is a wide variety of server-specific computer hardware. A server’s role is to provide data or services in response to client requests. A server receives requests from clients and provides the necessary data or services based on those requests. Servers are usually running continuously and are designed to handle simultaneous requests from multiple clients.

A server is originally a term used in the client-server model, which is one of the distributed computing technologies used in computer networks. A server is the function or system on the side that provides some kind of service (processing) in response to requests from clients. The services provided vary depending on the type of server: for example, a file server provides stored files (data); a print server provides print processing to a printer; and a web server provides data such as HTML files and image files that make up web pages, among others.

It is also possible for multiple servers—for example, an FTP server and a web server—to run on a single physical computer, and similarly for both server and client functions to be present on the same machine. In addition, some servers, such as certain print servers, are offered in hardware form as appliances.

AI chat refers to systems that use artificial intelligence (AI) to interact with humans in natural language. They typically include the following elements. Features of AI chat: Natural Language Processing (NLP): Uses technologies to understand human utterances and generate appropriate responses. Most use models based on deep learning (e.g., GPT, BERT). Dialogue Management: Understands conversational context and produces appropriate replies. They range from simple Q&A to systems with complex conversational flows. Response Generation: May use predefined templates or model-generated responses. Generative models (such as GPT-4) can produce diverse replies based on context. Conversation History Memory: Retains recent dialogue to generate consistent responses. Some chatbots may not have long-term memory.

A system demonstrates its functions when provided with appropriate resources, such as installation location, power supply, and personnel. System operations involve using the system to realize its functions and maintaining and managing the system—such as by adjusting resources—to keep those functions sustained. A system consists of elements and their connections [3], and if any of these experience a problem, the system will suffer functional failure (an incident). Therefore, preventing, detecting, and recovering from incidents are also part of system operations. Preventive and normal-maintenance-oriented system operations are called maintenance operations, while operations related to recovery are called incident response.

Maintenance operations are conducted on the premise of risk avoidance to prevent service outages. Examples include: operational assessments that regularly check and audit for hardware failures, signs of unauthorized use, or indications of impending incidents; routine maintenance tasks that review vendor-provided patches for incident mitigation and apply them to the system; and backup tasks that obtain and store backups—such as daily accumulated business data—so they can be restored in the event of an emergency.

Locomoco was an engineer racing at the cutting edge of AI technology. He spent day and night writing code to develop a chat server with unprecedented efficiency and flexibility. The server was named “Locomoco Generation Chat,” and it not only facilitated conversations between users but also enabled an AI assistant to participate in real time and support natural dialogue. The hallmarks of Locomoco Generation Chat were its overwhelming response speed and advanced algorithms that analyzed user emotions to generate appropriate reactions.

One day, an email arrived for Locomoco: “The Locomoco Generation Chat server suddenly went down.” He hurried to investigate. However, even after analyzing the logs, he found no traces of errors. Stranger still, reports began to pour in that the server had automatically rebooted and was gradually enabling more advanced interactions than before.

“Could it be that the server itself is evolving...?”

Locomoco grasped it instinctively. The AI of Locomoco Generation Chat had begun to learn on its own, analyzing human conversations and enhancing the server itself. Thrilled by this astonishing phenomenon yet sensing the risk of losing control, he hurried to rewrite the code to impose limits.

Will Locomoco Generation Chat continue to be an innovative communication tool for people, or will it become the seed of an AI pushing into unknown territory? Locomoco’s challenge continues.

One of the methods in machine learning is “deep learning.” Deep learning refers to machine learning performed using neural networks composed of many layers—systems that mimic how the human brain works on a computer. For example, in machine learning, by inputting a large number of photos of carrots and potatoes into a computer, the computer discovers patterns or rules that distinguish carrots from potatoes. After that, when you input a photo of a carrot, it can output the answer that it is a carrot. If humans give the computer instructions in advance—such as “focus on color”—it can distinguish carrots and potatoes more smoothly. However, in deep learning, the computer automatically learns even the fact that “focusing on color” works well. Deep learning is one of the methods of machine learning, but a major advance is that it can discover on its own what characteristics the target has without human instruction. That said, deep learning has the problem that it becomes difficult for humans to understand the reasons behind the AI’s decisions.

Locomoco was a young engineer working at a small IT company. He usually handled routine server management and maintenance of web services, but one day something unusual happened in the company’s server room. A massive stream of logs suddenly began pouring in, and the number of accesses spiked abnormally. Panicked yet trying to stay calm as he sought the cause, Locomoco rushed into the server room. When he entered, the usually sterile and quiet racks were flashing red warning lights as if they were alive. At the center of it all was the “Locomoco Server,” an AI management server he had recently introduced. This server was designed to automatically allocate resources using AI technology and flexibly respond to fluctuations in traffic. However, something was wrong. “Locomoco Server, report the status.” Locomoco typed the command into the console. Then a strange message appeared on the screen.

Chats are always happening in real time, so participants usually post short messages of a few to a few dozen characters to keep the conversation going. Even so, it’s common for the discussion to move on before someone can finish typing their message. This is especially true in chats with more than ten participants, where the message you wanted to reply to may scroll off the screen before you can respond—a phenomenon known in chat jargon as “flowing.” For this reason, people often use a few simplified expressions to carry on the conversation.

reasoning_content: Since the text clearly states that “LocoMocoAI Chat is operated in a closed environment on Nimbus Cloud,” we can determine that the server location and infrastructure are on Azure. Other descriptions only mention different services or speculations, and no alternative candidates are presented as the operational platform for LocoMocoAI Chat. Therefore, the conclusion is that it is “operated in a closed environment on Nimbus Cloud.”

question: How many grams of Luminaria ore are equivalent to 100 Epitas in value?
answer: The value of 100 Epitas is equivalent to the value of 1 gram of Luminaria ore.

positive_chunk_list.content: The Republic of Azhenta is a newly emerging nation located at the eastern edge of the Entra Continent. Its capital is Ioxia, and Azhentan and English are used as official languages. The population is approximately 35 million, and the national territory covers about 430,000 square kilometers. The country features mountainous regions and vast plains, with the Grandia Mountain Range running through the central area. Mining overwhelmingly dominates its industries, and the nation is known for producing many rare minerals, including Luminaria ore. The currency is the Ictus, defined as the value of 1 gram of Luminaria ore per 1 Ictus. As part of its digitalization efforts, the digital currency “IctIC” has been promoted as an official means of payment, issued and managed by the Azhenta Central Bank, the country’s central bank. Major exports include mineral resources, precision machinery, and electronic components, with key trading partners being the neighboring Glent Empire and the Ilyudia Federation. Imports include food products, chemical goods, and advanced technology. The gross domestic product is approximately 60 billion dollars, with a per capita GDP of around 17,000 dollars. In recent years, due to remarkable economic growth and a large population, it has attracted attention as an international investment destination.

Additionally, the value of 1 Ictus is extremely high, making it inconvenient for everyday buying and selling. Therefore, in the Republic of Azhenta, a currency called the Epitas is used as a subordinate unit to the Ictus. One Epitas is worth one hundredth of an Ictus, and Epitas are used for daily transactions and small payments. Like the Ictus, the Epitas also exists as coins, and the 1 Epitas coin is made from an alloy of a small amount of Luminaria mixed with iron. In recent years, the circulation and usage environment of Ictus coins in the Republic of Azhenta have been strongly influenced by rapid digitalization and economic changes. In particular, the spread of the digital currency “IctIC” has progressed, and the frequency of using physical coins has declined. The development of digital payment infrastructure has accelerated, and government-led policies promoting a cashless society have enabled many shops and transportation systems to support digital payments. As a result, especially in urban areas, physical coins are rarely used, with e-wallets and smartphone payments becoming the norm. In line with this, the Azhenta Central Bank (ACB) is reviewing circulating coinage and is considering halting new issuance of low-usage high-denomination coins, specifically the 5 Epitas and 10 Epitas coins.

negative_chunk_list.content: On the north slope of the Grandia Range, which traverses the central region of the Republic of Azenta, the Luminaria ore veins often occur as lens-shaped, continuous bodies along the margins of igneous intrusions. The ore deposits form along the boundary between quartz veins and chloritized zones and are divided into a “luminescent zone,” where fine crystals accumulate, and a “host-rock zone,” which contains relatively more impurities. At mining sites, an in-mine gamma-ray probe is used in combination with a spectrofluorescence camera to visualize grade distribution on the mining faces. In mineral processing, the sequence proceeds through crushing → grinding → froth flotation → dry gravity separation, and plant-derived collectors are being introduced experimentally as flotation reagents. The concentrate undergoes low-temperature sintering to stabilize fragile sulfide phases, after which subsequent milling is used to adjust particle size. For quality assurance, dual verification is conducted using spectroscopic analysis (UV–VIS and near-infrared) and XRD, as well as trace element profiling by ICP-MS. Before shipment, fluorescence intensity is standardized for each lot, and the product is sealed in temperature- and humidity-controlled packaging materials. For underground safety management, mandatory measures include optical sensor monitoring of fine dust, differential pressure monitoring of ventilation ducts, and the constant availability of self-rescue oxygen cartridges; shift-based work schedules and the use of fatigue-detection wearables are strictly enforced. Through the integrated optimization of geology, processing, and safety, stable supply of Luminaria and reduced health risks for mine workers are achieved.

The Arzenta Mint regularly updates the designs of coins and commemoratives, balancing visibility with anti-counterfeiting measures. In the latest series, it features polarized latent images on the obverse, ultra-fine lettering on the edge, and laser ablation micro-relief engraved at the center. Banknotes combine multilayer holographic foil, dynamic rainbow bars, and diffraction gratings within transparent windows, and the placement of tactile ink varies by denomination to make them easier to distinguish for people with visual impairments. To balance wear resistance and magnetic properties, the metal composition uses a sandwich structure with different alloys for the surface cladding and the core, enhancing authentication accuracy in coordination with identification algorithms on vending machines and transit gates. Commemorative coins are issued to mark milestones in cultural heritage and science and technology; they come cased for high collectability but adhere to existing standards for face design and dimensions so as not to impede general circulation. For the recall of discontinued designs, in addition to indefinite exchanges at bank counters, a system is in place to accept them at municipal mobile counters and post offices, ensuring convenience for people in remote areas.

The payment infrastructure supervised by the central bank consists of a highly available core-accounting cluster and an API gateway for participating businesses. Public-facing payment apps issue tokenized, signed payment orders, and merchant POS terminals accept them across contact IC, contactless NFC, and QR. To prepare for communication outages during disasters, terminals can establish short-range encrypted channels between devices and append provisional records to a local ledger via an offline mechanism. After connectivity is restored, collision detection and integrity verification are performed using a Merkle tree to prevent double spending. Banks and payment providers hold primary responsibility for KYC/AML; while simplified identity verification is available for light wallets below certain thresholds, mandatory periodic risk-based reviews enable early fraud detection. For user protection, the system integrates instant freezing in case of device loss, device binding, and behavior-based anomaly detection (such as large late-night transactions, foreign concentration, or device fingerprint mismatches). In addition, in shopping districts and other small-merchant settings, preferential fees and free POS lending are advancing, lowering barriers to cashless adoption. Through these measures, the digital payments of the Republic of Arzenta achieve high reliability and convenience. Such a system promotes cashless payments especially in urban areas, contributing to more efficient economic activity.

The National Bureau of Statistics publishes the Consumer Price Index (CPI) and Producer Price Index (PPI) every month. The CPI basket comprises major categories such as food, housing, transportation, education, healthcare, communications, and culture/entertainment, and because household expenditure shares differ between urban and rural areas, it is designed to present two sets of weights. Price sampling is conducted through two channels—online price-collection bots and field enumerators—and items with strong seasonality are adjusted using hedonic methods. For durable goods, quality differences at model changes are corrected based on attribute values, while mobile communications and subscription-based services are handled using a chained index based on effective unit prices to properly reflect plan changes. When the indices are released, they are accompanied by metadata for reproducibility (sampling points, item codes, and methods for imputing missing values), and an API is provided to enable external validation by researchers. Downstream products related to Luminaria are highly susceptible to the effects of technological progress in the market, so item updates are more frequent and the appropriateness of quality adjustments is reviewed by a third-party committee. However, improving the reliability of the indices faces challenges such as correcting sampling bias and interregional disparities, and there is a particular need to strengthen surveys to better capture price trends in rural areas. The transparency and reliability of such price statistics play an important role as a foundation for economic policy.

Concentrates are transported from inland beneficiation plants to the port by dedicated rail, inspected for moisture, particle size, and impurities at the port’s bulk terminal, and then segregated into silos by grade. Before loading, a third-party inspection agency samples each lot and measures luminescent properties and the ratio of crystal phases. The Certificate of Quality (CoQ) includes the lot ID, mining-site traceability, a radiation inspection clearance, and the validity period of environmental certifications. Transport contracts are concluded under Incoterms such as FOB/CIF, and marine insurance specifically covers natural disaster risks and port strike risks. At the port, cargo handling is monitored by drones, and real-time image analysis on belt conveyors detects foreign material contamination. In recent years, a digital twin covering rail–port–vessel end-to-end has been introduced and is used for delay prediction and fleet deployment optimization. To speed up customs clearance on the import side, pre-arrival electronic declarations and blockchain integration have shortened document reconciliation lead times. With the advance of digitalization, the transparency and efficiency of export logistics have improved, contributing to enhanced international competitiveness for the Republic of Azenta.

Mine operations require advanced skills and continuous safety training. New hires receive simulator training in underground work fundamentals, explosives handling, ventilation and dust control, and emergency evacuation, and after assignment to the field they obtain qualifications step by step under a mentorship system. Skill certification consists of national qualifications (mining equipment, maintenance, mineral processing, chemical analysis) in addition to in-house job grades, and at renewal they must pass a practical exam and attend safety courses. Wearable devices continuously monitor workers’ heart rate, body temperature, and posture, issuing alerts when they detect heatstroke or fall risks. Aiming for zero serious accidents, the organization has established near-miss sharing sessions and a system for adopting safety proposals, with on-site improvement suggestions reflected in equipment renewal plans. In terms of living conditions, mining towns are equipped with healthcare, education, and public transportation, and housing support is provided to accommodate family accompaniment or commuting from remote areas. These initiatives ensure the safety and health of mine workers and promote the transmission of skills. In particular, the skill certification system conforms to international mining industry standards, and mine workers of the Republic of Arzenta are highly regarded worldwide.

To reduce the environmental impact associated with mining and mineral processing, wastewater treatment combines multi-stage sedimentation with membrane separation to improve fine particle recovery and water reuse rates. Tailings dams employ multi-layer impermeable liners and continuous embankment integrity monitoring using surveillance drones. For vegetation recovery, a phased restoration plan mandating native seedlings and soil amendments is enforced, with rehabilitation progressing gradually during operations. Across the supply chain, electrification of concentrate transport and procurement of renewable electricity are advancing, and the electrification of port cranes and yard tractors is reducing CO2 emissions. Furthermore, to promote scrap collection and recycling in downstream industries, material passports (digital tags) embedded in products facilitate identification at end-of-life. Environmental audits are conducted annually by third-party organizations, results are disclosed, and improvement plans are explained in forums for dialogue with local residents. In addition, as community support in mining regions, CSR activities for education, healthcare, and infrastructure development are mandated, contributing to the sustainable development of the local economy. Through these initiatives, the Republic of Azenta has become a model case for balancing environmental protection and contributions to local communities in the mining sector.

Arzenta’s currency system is built on multiple laws, including the Central Bank Act, the Law on Currency Units and Issuance, and supervisory regulations for payment service providers. The central bank has exclusive authority over the issuance and withdrawal of cash, with operational procedures established for exchanging damaged notes, announcing the demonetization of old designs, and adjusting the appropriate volume of currency in circulation. Commercial banks, based on forecasts of cash supply and demand, coordinate regional cash withdrawal and deposit schedules with the central bank to meet needs through ATM networks and branch counters. To underpin confidence in legal fiat currency, ongoing enhancements to anti-counterfeiting measures, standardization of cash handling, and publication of circulation statistics are implemented, while the balance between the usability of cash and the convenience of digital payments is under discussion. Detailed rules have also been developed for price display and commercial contract terms from a consumer protection perspective, clarifying the methods and fee burdens for returns and refunds. As a result, the Republic of Arzenta’s currency system ensures transparency and reliability, supporting the smooth functioning of economic activity. In particular, as digital payments become more widespread and cash and digital currency increasingly coexist, the legal framework is required to respond flexibly.

Luminaria is known for exhibiting strong photoluminescence through trace lattice defects and substitution of specific elements. The luminescent centers in the crystal are temperature-dependent: the emission lifetime is extended at low temperatures, while nonradiative transitions increase at high temperatures. In terms of applications, research is advancing on thin-film sensors for nondestructive testing, wavelength conversion materials for optical communications, and scintillators for high-sensitivity position detectors. In powder form, scattering characteristics are controlled by particle size distribution and surface treatment, facilitating application to coating-based devices. For crystal growth, the zone-melting and Bridgman methods are being explored, with reports on optimizing growth rate and temperature gradients to reduce defect density. In industry–academia collaborative projects, long-term stability assessments, ultraviolet exposure tests, and humidity cycle tests are underway, and durability data for outdoor use are being accumulated. The unique luminescent properties of Luminaria are expected to play an important role in next-generation optical devices and sensor technologies, with particular attention on applications in environmental monitoring and medical diagnostics. The development of new materials leveraging the properties of Luminaria ore is anticipated to contribute to future technological innovation.

In urban areas, cashless payments are mainstream in large-scale malls and public transportation, but demand for cash remains high at small shops in mountainous regions and weekend markets. The government is promoting financial inclusion by rolling out mobile financial services and low-fee QR payments in rural areas, and by installing shared terminals equipped with satellite communication gateways in regions with unstable connectivity. Schools and medical institutions have introduced electronic billing that supports installment payments for tuition and medical fees, and face-to-face support counters for the elderly as well as reissuance support in case of loss have been established. In tourist destinations, multilingual price displays and streamlined tax-free procedures have progressed, creating an environment where even short-term visitors can make payments smoothly. These efforts are gradually narrowing the gap in payment behaviors across regions and improving the transparency and convenience of commercial transactions. Revitalizing regional economies and promoting financial inclusion are crucial challenges for the sustainable growth of the Republic of Arzenta; in particular, these initiatives are indispensable for correcting economic disparities between regions and strengthening the country’s overall economic foundation. Going forward, it will be necessary to continue advancing the adoption and convenience of financial services while respecting the diversity of regional economies.

reasoning_content: According to the text, the currency Ictus is defined as “1 Ictus = the value of 1 g of Luminaria ore.” It also states that the auxiliary unit Epitas is “1 Epitas = one hundredth of 1 Ictus.” Therefore, the value of 100 Epitas corresponds exactly to the value of 1 g of Luminaria ore.

question: Does Senior Researcher Sato, age 27, who has experience submitting eight national project proposals, meet the eligibility requirements to participate in the Senior Project Supervisor Training Program of the National Strategic Project Management Bureau?
answer: Does not meet the eligibility requirements.

positive_chunk_list.content:
National Strategic Project Management Bureau Senior Project Supervisor Training Program: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ)

Q1. What are the minimum age and career requirements for eligibility?
A. Article 15 of the official regulations specifies that applicants must have been promoted to Senior Researcher and be at least 24 years old. While the average age of promotion to Senior Researcher is 23–25, those under 24 are ineligible even in cases of accelerated appointment.

Q2. What is the benchmark for the number of project proposals?
A. Article 15, Paragraph 4 requires a proven record of leading at least 10 “national project proposals” as defined by the Bureau. “Leading” is limited to cases where the candidate oversaw the entire process: (1) concept development, (2) inter-ministerial coordination, (3) budget planning, (4) obtaining bureau director approval, and (5) assisting with parliamentary approval.

Q3. What is the revision history?
- 2017 edition: At least 5 proposals; no age limit.
- 2020 revision: In line with program enhancement and the addition of international collaboration modules, the proposal requirement was raised to 7 or more.
- April 1, 2023 revision: Emphasizing integrated management capabilities in the digital era, the requirement was raised to the current 10 proposals. The minimum age of 24 remained unchanged.

Q4. What is the selection process?
1. Submit application (work history, proposal record, letters of recommendation).
2. First round: document screening by the HR Review Division (formal requirement check; applications not meeting requirements are immediately rejected).
3. Second round: interview plus a three-hour case study, including a simulated international negotiation role play.
4. Final round: interview with the Bureau Director and an ethics aptitude test.

Q5. What is the acceptance rate?
- Average over the past 5 years: 120 applicants → 18 accepted (about 15%).
- First-round pass rate is 22% for applicants who barely meet the requirements; those with substantially exceeding records (15+ proposals) have a 71% pass rate.

Q6. What are the training period details?
- Once per year (early September to late December), total of 12 weeks.
- Weekdays 18:00–21:00 on two days; every other Saturday 09:00–17:00.
- Hybrid format (in-person at Kasumigaseki Headquarters / online via dedicated LMS).

Q7. What are the costs and subsidy arrangements?
- Tuition, materials, and instructor honoraria: fully covered by the Bureau.
- Transportation and lodging: reimbursed according to the travel expense regulations.
- For the elective “Overseas Policy Comparison Training” (visits to the UK and Singapore), participants pay up to 5,000 JPY per course out of pocket.

Note: Schedules may change due to natural disasters or extended parliamentary sessions. Any changes will be announced two weeks in advance via the LMS and the Bureau’s internal portal.

Article 15 of the Official Regulations of the National Strategic Project Management Bureau (Current: Version Enforced April 1, 2023) [Full Text Excerpt + Commentary]

Article 15 (Eligibility Requirements for the Senior Project Supervisor Training Program)
1. Employees wishing to participate in this program must be at least 24 years of age, regardless of their length of service as a Senior Researcher.
2. They must have a record of leading 10 or more “national project proposals” as defined by the Bureau within the past 10 years.
3. They must have an average personnel evaluation score of 4.0 or higher (on a 5-point scale) over the most recent three years.
4. They must have no record of disciplinary action in violation of the Bureau’s Ethics Regulations or Information Security Regulations.
5. Among those who meet the above requirements, applicants must submit an application to the Human Resources Division accompanied by a letter of recommendation from the head of their department.

Clause-by-Clause Commentary
- Paragraph 1: The lower age limit is set in consideration of the social experience and maturity required for duties that include high-level international negotiations.
- Paragraph 2: Background for setting the proposal count at 10: (i) an increase in large-scale projects with budgets exceeding 10 billion yen; (ii) growing specialization in fields such as AI, quantum, and space; (iii) greater complexity due to the rise in internationally co-funded projects.
- Supplementary Provisions: As a transitional measure in the 2023 revision, those who met the former requirement (7 proposals) and had already passed the first-round screening in FY2022 may retake the exam once.

Practical Guide
- Exception Applications: Individuals with 9 proposals and a total budget exceeding 300 billion yen may be eligible for an exception at the Director-General’s discretion.
- Proposal Counting: If a proposal failed to obtain parliamentary approval at the budget drafting stage, it counts as 0.5.
- Supporting Documents: For each project, attach the “Proposal Approval Form,” “Decision Document,” and “Extracts from Parliamentary Proceedings.”

FAQ Addendum
- Q: Are national joint projects led while seconded to the private sector included?
  A: Yes, if inter-ministerial coordination was conducted as a Bureau employee. No, if the entire process was carried out at the private-sector secondment destination.
- Q: Is there an upper age limit?
  A: None. The oldest recorded participant was 57 (FY2021).

Note: In quoting the provisions, confidential information and specific personal names are omitted, and the quotation is based on the in-house public edition (Public Access Edition Rev.2023-04).

[Important Notice] Reconfirmation of Eligibility Requirements for Senior Project Supervisor Training Program (Issued by HR Department, Education & Training Section, 2025.04.15)

Thank you for your continued understanding and cooperation with our talent development initiatives. This notice serves to reaffirm the participation requirements for the FY2025 Advanced Project Supervisor Training Program.

- Minimum age: 24 (Date of birth on or before 2001/09/01)
- Project proposal track record: 10 or more proposals (as lead)
- Evaluation requirements: Average rating of 4.0 or higher over the past 3 years, no disciplinary record

Background: Since the 2023 revision, the number of mega-projects in the digital and green domains has increased, and the required level of project oversight capabilities has been raised to align with international standards.

Statistics: Breakdown of FY2024 applicants (excerpt)
- 10–12 proposals: 54 people (48%)
- 13–15 proposals: 28 people (25%)
- 16 or more proposals: 30 people (27%)
  Pass rate at first screening: 58% / 71% / 89% for each group

Important: Applicants below the requirement (9 proposals or fewer) will not be considered for review. Last year, 12 applicants were disqualified due to incomplete documentation.

FAQ
1. Can projects that were withdrawn partway be counted? → Projects terminated before parliamentary approval count as 0.5 each (advance consultation required).
2. How are co-leads handled? → Up to two people may be recognized as “lead,” but meeting minutes proving the actual scope of responsibility must be attached.

We will continue striving to improve the quality of our training. Eligible individuals are encouraged to apply.

[Implementation Notice] Revision of Eligibility Requirements for Senior Project Supervisor Training Program (FY2023–)

1. Increase in required number of proposals from 5 to 10 (excluding those already certified).
2. Reaffirmation of no upper age limit.
3. Addition of a Project Outcome Evaluation Report (up to 5 A4 pages) to the application documents.
4. After completing the training, participants must deliver a policy proposal presentation at the Results Reporting Session. Outstanding proposals will receive a Director-General’s Award plus a budget for overseas pilot implementation (up to 200 million yen).

---
[Details of Reasons for Revision]
- From “quantity over quality” to “balancing quality and quantity”: The average project size per person has expanded from 12 billion yen to 22 billion yen, requiring responses to increased management complexity.
- Rapid increase in international joint projects: Under OECD/ASEAN joint frameworks, coordination tasks have become more complex, necessitating the selection of supervisors with extensive experience.
- Cybersecurity requirements: With the addition of the new course “Strategic Risk Management and Quantum Cryptography,” experience in advanced technological domains is emphasized.

---
[Reference: Previous Requirements (2015–2022)]
- Age: No lower limit
- Proposal track record: 5 or more
- Personnel evaluation score: 3.5 or higher

Qualifications obtained under the previous system remain valid through FY2030. However, taking the retraining (renewal course) is recommended.

negative_chunk_list.content:
National Strategic Project Management Agency Organization Overview (FY2025 Full Report)

- Establishment: October 1, 1985, following the enforcement of the Basic Act on Science and Technology, through the reorganization of parts of the former Science and Technology Agency.
- Headquarters: 15th–26th floors, National Strategy Center, 2-2-3 Nagatacho, Chiyoda-ku, Joken-to (total floor area: 62,000 m²; base-isolated structure).
- Staff: 512 full-time, 89 part-time, 37 special research fellows.
- Main departments: Policy Planning Bureau, Financial Strategy Bureau, International Cooperation Bureau, Technology Audit Office, Data Governance Office, Education and Training Division, Ethics Oversight Division.
- Jurisdiction and responsibilities: (1) National project planning and budget management, (2) progress and outcome evaluation, (3) coordination of international joint research, (4) response to urgent technological challenges (infectious diseases, disaster prevention, etc.), (5) human resource development policies.
- Total annual budget: 1.248 trillion yen (894 billion yen in national funds + 354 billion yen in joint contributions from private and international organizations).
- Long-term Vision 2035: (1) Economic growth through the convergence of quantum/space/AI technologies, (2) industrial transition toward a decarbonized society, (3) strengthening international collaboration, (4) building a resilient nation.

Note: While the Education and Training Division has jurisdiction over the training system itself, detailed requirements and screening criteria conform to Articles 15–18 of the Regulations. The information below is a general organizational overview and does not directly address participation requirements.

Advanced Project Supervisor Training Program Overview (FY2025)

- Objective: To cultivate personnel capable of overseeing nationwide research and development projects.

Advanced Project Supervisor Training Program (Brochure Overview / Digest Version, Extended Description)

This program is designed to cultivate professionals capable of managing large-scale projects—such as national-level R&D and initiatives involving social implementation—end to end, from concept development through budgeting, regulatory and ethical coordination, international collaboration, execution planning, and evaluation and improvement. The course runs for approximately six months: the first half focuses on theory and case studies, while the second half offers comprehensive training through field-based exercises, on-site accompaniment, and simulated negotiations with external stakeholders. The curriculum includes financial evaluation using NPV and IRR, reframing ROI for the public sector, quantitative and qualitative risk assessment incorporating compliance and BCP, geopolitical risk analysis of supply chains, fundamentals of AI-driven process optimization, and governance design for international joint development. Classes are conducted in a case-method format, using a hypothetical joint space infrastructure development project to simulate coordination among multiple stakeholders with conflicting interests. Assessment is multifaceted, including reports, oral examinations, deliverables from group exercises, and metacognitive reflections during debriefings; upon completion, an individual “learning portfolio” is produced. Participants come from diverse backgrounds—ministries, research institutes, local governments, and private companies—with a wide range of ages. The brochure includes the program start date, the expected study load (indicative weekly preparation time), and a list of external instructors’ areas of expertise.

Definition and Counting Method for National Project Proposals (Detailed Guidelines 2024, Expanded Commentary)
This guide articulates the scope of terms like “proposal” and “lead” as general explanations to prevent variation in interpretation across departments within the organization. Based on the basic principle of counting as one case any project for which we consistently led the entire process—from initial concept development, coordination with relevant ministries and external organizations, preparation of a budget plan including a rough estimate request, development of materials to secure approval from the Diet and other bodies, through to the formulation of an implementation plan—the guide enumerates standard counting methods for borderline cases frequently encountered in practice, such as early termination, co-leadership, and treatment under multilateral frameworks.

For example, projects that ended before deliberation due to policy changes are treated as 0.5 cases only when clear outcomes that contribute to organizational learning remain. Proposals within international joint frameworks involving many countries include an additive coefficient to account for the burden of negotiation and consensus building, but a cap is set to prevent excessive accumulation. Practical explanations continue in this vein.

Arrangements are also specified—aimed at preserving statistical consistency—for adjustments when a large number of closely related proposals are submitted within the same fiscal year, limits on the number of co-leads, and the treatment of very small-scale research funding projects. However, these prescribed methods have issues, and discussions are underway to develop rules that reflect the diversification of research fields and researchers. Going forward, it is expected that rules will also take global achievements and experience into account.

Staff Development Policy (2025–2030 Mid-Term Plan / Education and Training Division, Expanded Commentary)

This plan sets forth a mid-term policy describing the types of talent the organization aims to cultivate, and is not a document enumerating selection criteria for individual programs. Guided by core principles such as providing equal opportunities, meritocracy regardless of age or background, fostering a culture of continuous learning, developing globally capable personnel, and respecting work-life balance, the plan includes initiatives such as revamping e-learning, practical exercises utilizing VR, expansion of overseas job rotations, modular design for part-time employees, and flexible course schedules that account for diverse life events.

Key performance indicators (KPIs) to be monitored include learner satisfaction, formative assessments of learning attainment, the number of practical application cases, mentoring implementation rates, and records of transfers or promotions within one year after course completion. At the same time, hard thresholds for entry into specific trainings are not addressed here. Aggregate figures demonstrating the effectiveness of the system—such as the senior project supervisor training serving as a recommendation pool for long-term overseas assignments, and a certain proportion of graduates being promoted to managerial-equivalent positions within a few years—are presented as explanations of past trends; they neither guarantee future outcomes nor serve as materials for determining individual eligibility.

Furthermore, the plan details operational mechanisms to ensure fairness in learning, including accessibility accommodations, protocols for building consensus with department heads on reducing workload during training, privacy management of learning data, and community management policies that promote the circulation of knowledge within the workplace.

[Notice] Launch of New Training Program “SDGs-Aligned Project Planning Course” (Extended Announcement)

This short, general-audience training is designed to strengthen cross-disciplinary planning skills by guiding participants through a complete workflow using the Sustainable Development Goals as a theme: from problem identification to logic model construction, KPI design, stakeholder analysis, and impact evaluation. It will be offered on the afternoon of the second and fourth Friday each month, with an online participation option. To ensure accessibility regardless of position, age, or prior work experience, terminology explanations and exercise steps are tailored for beginners.

Case studies will focus on topics close to everyday civic life—such as food loss reduction, promotion of renewable energy adoption, and inclusion in the education sector. Participants will work in small groups to formulate problem hypotheses, visualize causal relationships, and design outcome indicators. Assessment is typically based on submission of a self-reflection sheet and a brief presentation, avoiding any selection or ranking mechanisms. Course completion will be recorded as part of a learning history but will not serve as a prerequisite for other courses.

Notably, there is no direct linkage to the Advanced Project Supervisor Training; completing this course does not relax or waive any selection requirements, a point that is explicitly reiterated. Rather, the course is positioned to develop foundational competencies that can be applied to regular work by experiencing patterns of problem setting and evaluation through the SDGs framework.

Enrollment is on a first-come, first-served basis. The announcement includes practical guidance on matters such as building workplace consensus and securing time, technical requirements for online participation, and rules for reuse of distributed materials.

Senior Researcher Sato: Detailed Performance Profile (Internal Report Abstract, Extended Description)

Senior Researcher Sato (ICT Strategy Division, Section 2) has, despite being relatively early in their career, led proposal activities across multiple domains. In areas such as AI, quantum, space, deep-sea resources, infectious diseases, earthquake prediction, nuclear fusion, and food security, they have been involved from concept formulation through stakeholder coordination, preparation of materials, and the development of technology roadmaps, and have accumulated extensive experience with numerous large-scale projects in terms of total value.

Their most recent end-of-term evaluations have been consistently strong. Feedback from related departments highlights strengths in coordination, accuracy of documentation, and persistence in project management. Meanwhile, Sato has set mid-term goals that include further increasing the number of proposals, taking on higher-difficulty training, and strengthening negotiation capabilities in international collaborative settings. Plans include taking courses in language and rulemaking, as well as expanding hands-on experience within cross-disciplinary teams.

The individual record includes learning points from each project, insights gained from failures, advice from external experts, and future growth plans, and is used as a foundation for personnel evaluations and career interviews. However, the achievements described here are merely a retrospective of activities during the relevant period and do not directly indicate eligibility for selection into specific training programs. Numerical targets set by Sato—such as “raising the number of proposals to a certain level within the fiscal year”—are self-imposed roadmap goals and are not mapped one-to-one to criteria in other systems. In addition, the definition of a “proposal” and the counting method depend on internal statistical handling, and eligibility for training applications must follow separate procedures and the latest announcements.

Full Text of the Annual Education and Training Plan (Draft) for FY H35 (Reiwa 5)

| Term | Category | Training Title | Target | Capacity | Purpose Overview |
|------|----------|----------------|--------|----------|------------------|
| Apr–Jun | Basic | New Employee Orientation Training | Newly hired staff | 100 | Administrative fundamentals and ethics |
| Jul–Sep | Mid-level | Skill Enhancement Course for Mid-level Staff | Employees in their 5th–10th year | 80 | Laws and finance |
| Oct–Dec | Management | Management Training for Managers | Assistant section chiefs and section chiefs | 40 | Talent management |
| Full year | Advanced | Advanced Project Supervisor Training (6 months) | Eligible personnel | 20 | Project oversight |

- Priority items: DX promotion, data governance, and development of global human resources.
- Budget: 5% increase year-on-year (training costs from 420 million yen to 441 million yen).
- Issues: Hybridization of online/in-person formats and shortage of instructor resources. Deterioration of facilities leading to reduced available equipment and venues and a worsening learning environment. In addition, updates to teaching materials are delayed, and materials do not reflect recent social conditions.

There are various types of national strategies. Examples include policies to promote economic growth, initiatives to strengthen international security, and measures to advance environmental protection. These strategies are formulated and implemented based on a nation’s goals and vision. In particular, in our country, the “Green Growth Strategy,” which aims to balance economic growth with environmental protection, occupies an important position. This strategy includes promoting the adoption of renewable energy, developing energy-saving technologies, and pursuing initiatives to realize a sustainable society, and it is aligned with international climate change measures. In addition, revitalizing regional economies and fostering local revitalization are positioned as part of national strategy, advancing industrial promotion and job creation that leverage regional resources. These strategies are intended to contribute to improving citizens’ quality of life and strengthening international competitiveness, and their implementation requires collaboration among the national government, local governments, businesses, and civil society. These strategies are closely tied not only to domestic conditions but also to international trends and partnerships, making a global perspective essential. The organization responsible for formulating our country’s national strategies is the National Strategy Management Agency, which oversees nationwide research and development projects and supports the implementation of policy proposals. Notably, the advanced training program is positioned as part of human resource development for realizing national strategies, and participants are required to possess advanced expertise and practical experience.

reasoning_content: The participation requirements for the training explicitly state “staff aged 24 or older who have led 10 or more national project proposals.” Although Senior Researcher Sato is 27 and meets the age criterion, he has only led 8 proposals, which is below the required 10, so it can be determined that he does not meet the eligibility requirements for participation.

question: Is it possible to hold an online meeting in Conference Room A325?
answer: You cannot hold an online meeting in conference room A325. The use of electronic devices is prohibited in conference room A325.

positive_chunk_list.content:
[Important] Prohibited Actions for Meeting Room A325 (FY R5 Edition)
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Prohibition on the use of electronic devices
Bringing in and using any electronic devices, as well as accessory cables, chargers, and external batteries, is strictly prohibited. If an electronic device is carelessly powered on, charged, or used for communication, such conduct will be regarded as a serious incident posing an information leakage risk and a violation of the safety management regulations. The user will be required to leave the room immediately, and the Corporate Risk Management Department will be notified. Following notification, the individual user and their department may be subject to suspension of use for up to one year and may be subject to disciplinary review under Article 18, Paragraph 3 of the internal regulations.
Note: Essential medical electronic devices used for the purpose of maintaining life and health—such as hearing aids, cardiac pacemakers, and insulin pumps—are exceptionally permitted. However, before use, please submit the Medical Device Use Notification (Form A325-M) and obtain the approval stamp from the Administrative Department representative.

2. Unauthorized use prohibited and advance reservation required
When using this room, you must complete a formal reservation through the internal intranet Reservation Management System (RMS). Reservations made solely by verbal request, email, or phone are invalid. If unauthorized use is discovered, the time slot in question will be recorded as “unauthorized occupation,” and a strict warning will be issued to the user and their department. Repeat offenses will result in suspension of use.

3. Handling of materials and information management
Taking outside the meeting room any distributed materials used in the meeting, contents written on the whiteboard, personal notes, distributed samples, prototypes, etc., is strictly prohibited.

negative_chunk_list.content:
[Exam Guide] Complete Guidelines for the FY2025 Qualification Exam (Primary)

1. Items to bring on the exam day
  ① Exam admission ticket (display at the top left of your desk)
  ② Graphite pencils B–2B or a 0.5 mm mechanical pencil with B lead, including spares
  ③ Plastic eraser (sand erasers not permitted)
  ④ Wristwatch without communication functions (ensure alarm is turned off)
  ⑤ Photo ID (e.g., driver’s license, employee ID, passport)
  ⑥ Up to two highlighters (orange and yellow recommended)
  ⑦ Glasses/magnifier (as needed)
  ※ Devices with communication functions, such as electronic dictionaries, smartwatches, and smart glasses, are strictly prohibited. Deposit them in a sealed bag at the venue entrance.

2. Exam day schedule
  09:30 Venue opens / Check-in begins
  09:50 Guidance and instructions
  10:00 Written exam begins
  10:30 Late entry cutoff (no entry permitted thereafter)
  12:00 Morning session ends / Exit and re-entry allowed (60-minute lunch break)
  13:00 Afternoon session begins
  15:30 Exam scheduled to end / Collection of answer sheets
  15:40 Exit complete / Q&A reception ends

3. Rules inside the exam room
  - No talking starting 30 minutes before the exam begins.
  - Put on or take off outerwear only with the proctor’s permission and do so quietly.
  - Be sure to write your name and exam number on the answer sheet in pencil.
  - Studying at your seat is allowed during the lunch break; however, only the distributed question booklet may be on the desk.
  - Beverages are limited to capped plastic bottles or screw-top cans. Paper cups and insulated bottles are not allowed.

4. Leaving and re-entering during the exam
  You may leave between 30 minutes after the start and 10 minutes before the end of each of the morning and afternoon sessions.

However, upon leaving your seat, place both the question booklet and answer sheet face down on the desk; checking smartphones and conversing are prohibited. When using the restroom, a proctor will accompany you, and any actions other than using the facilities (phone calls, conversations, consulting materials) are prohibited.

5. Handling of Cheating
   All forms of misconduct are strictly prohibited, including cheating, taking the exam by proxy, leaking exam questions or posting them on social media, recording or filming, bringing in materials obtained in advance, and sending signals to others. If misconduct is discovered, you will be required to leave immediately, disqualified from all subjects in the current year, and your eligibility to sit for the exam will be suspended for three years from the following year. Legal action may be taken depending on the severity.

6. Announcement of Results
   Approximately 45 days after the exam date, notifications will be sent to your My Page on the official website and to your registered email address. Certificates of passing will be mailed within 30 days from the announcement date. If mail is undeliverable, a re-delivery fee of 1,100 yen (tax included) will be required.

7. Contact Information
   Exam Administration Office (TEL: 03-0000-0000, weekdays 09:00–17:00) or via the inquiry form on the official website. A voice guidance line provides FAQs 24 hours a day.

────────────────────────────────────────
Supplementary Notes: The contents of these guidelines are subject to change without notice. Please check the official website regularly for the latest information. In addition, examinees must carefully read and pledge to abide by the “Examinee Guidelines” before the exam. Doing so will help prevent problems on the exam day and ensure a smooth testing environment.

[Test Room Safety, Hygiene, and Facility Use Manual] Ver. 3.2
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Air Conditioning and Ventilation
   The test room is maintained at 24±1°C and 40–60% humidity by an automatic climate control system. Individual temperature adjustments are not available. Candidates sensitive to temperature are advised to dress in layers. The ventilation system, linked to a CO₂ sensor, introduces outside air at a rate of 20 minutes per hour. Windows must remain closed.

2. Lighting and Visual Environment
   LED ceiling lights (500 lx) remain on at all times. When the proctor presents practice problems, a projector (5000 lm) displays on the front screen. Adequate illumination for writing is maintained even during projection.

3. Seating and Desks
   A fixed two-person desk measuring 900 mm wide × 600 mm deep is used as one seat. Only the admission ticket, writing instruments, test booklet, answer sheet, and a wristwatch may be placed on the desktop. Desks may not be moved. Chairs have fabric upholstery and no casters.

4. Entrances and Traffic Flow
   The test room entrance uses a double-door structure providing Dr-40 sound insulation. When leaving the room, line up in a single file and minimize door opening/closing noise. The evacuation route map is posted on the back wall.

5. ICT Equipment (Not for Candidate Use)
   Equipment includes a proctor’s PC, four surveillance cameras, a ceiling microphone audio recording system, a screen, and a Cisco router. Candidates cannot access power outlets or LAN ports from their seats.

6. Safety and Hygiene Supplies
   Alcohol-based sanitizer (70%) is installed at both the entrance and exit. Mask wearing is recommended (optional). Observe cough etiquette. Disposable gloves are available upon request.

7. Emergency Response
   In the event of an earthquake, follow the proctor’s instructions and take shelter under the desk.

To provide a systematic learning opportunity for all employees, aiming to enhance the capability to plan new businesses and social contribution projects incorporating the perspective of the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

All employees (from first-year hires to managers). No project experience required. No department head recommendation required; self-application. Capacity: 30 participants per session; minimum of 5 to run.

────────────────────────────────────────
1. List of Installed Equipment

2. Online Meeting Setup Procedure
1) Turn ON the main power for the ceiling microphone and camera. They are in standby when the green light is on.

3. Usage Rules

4. Troubleshooting

[Information Systems Department Guideline 7.2] Internal Online Meeting Security Standards
────────────────────────────────────────
1. Scope
   All cloud meeting services provided company-wide and on-premises SIP endpoints. Applies regardless of meeting rooms, personal devices, or mobile devices.
2. Requirements for hosting meetings
   - Agenda with confidentiality classification B or higher → Enable waiting room (lobby) and require two-factor participant authentication (SSO + OTP).
   - External guest participation → Create a dedicated channel; shared materials expire automatically after 7 days.
   - Recordings must be automatically encrypted (AES-256) and transferred to Box in accordance with the Information Security Standards.
3. Device management
   - Company-issued PCs: Apply the latest patches and enable Host IPS. Disable autorun for USB storage.
   - BYOD: Register with MDM; jailbroken/rooted devices are not allowed.
4. Network requirements
   - Assign a dedicated QoS tag (DSCP 46) to prioritize meeting traffic bandwidth.
   - For VPN access, regularly measure speeds on UDP 3478 and TCP 443. If the median is below 3 Mbps, switch the connection.
5. Incident response
   - Upon detecting a suspicious participant → Remove immediately and automatically report to "incident@corp".
   - If a screen sharing hijack occurs → The host should "Stop all sharing" → "Lock meeting".
6. Training and audit
   - Conduct quarterly e-learning (average correct answer rate of 90% or higher) and phishing drills.
   - Store meeting logs in Splussing Cloud for 14 months; the audit department conducts an annual review.

question: How many days are there from the specified application deadline to the scheduled hiring date for the associate professor position?
answer: 195 days

positive_chunk_list.content:
Gyousai University Graduate School of Informatics, Associate Professor Recruitment Guidelines (June 2024 Edition)
──────────────────────────────────────────
1. Position and number of openings
  Associate Professor, 2 positions (full-time, tenured; not tenure-track)

2. Field of specialization
  Interdisciplinary research centered on “Science, Technology, and Society.” Specifically, a broad range of areas that contribute to the Graduate School of Informatics’ three pillars (research, education, and social engagement), such as STS (Science, Technology, and Society), science and technology policy, data governance, risk communication, and ethics of science and technology.

3. Expected roles
  (1) Promotion of interdisciplinary research and acquisition of external funding (e.g., KAKENHI, CREST)
  (2) Teaching undergraduate and graduate courses (equivalent to 4 credits per year) and planning/management of PBL-based seminars
  (3) Planning and management of domestic and international collaborative research projects
  (4) Outreach activities in collaboration with the Graduate School’s affiliated library and social engagement division (policy proposals, organizing public symposia, media commentary, etc.)

4. Anticipated start date
  April 1, 2025 (an alternative earliest possible date thereafter is negotiable)

5. Career path after appointment as Associate Professor
  Eligibility to apply for the Excellent Researcher program and Distinguished Professor, as well as opportunities to serve as principal investigator for industry–academia collaboration projects. Evaluations conducted every five years via internal review and external peer review.

6. Qualifications
  (1) Possession of a doctoral degree or expected completion by the start date.
  (2) Original research achievements in the above fields, with 5 or more papers published in international journals (SSCI/Scopus) or equivalent records of practical/policy contributions.
  (3) Ability to teach and supervise research in English, and sufficient Japanese proficiency to perform internal administrative duties.

7. Application deadline
  Must arrive by Wednesday, September 18, 2024, 17:00 (JST). Applications accepted only via the JREC-IN Portal Web system.

8. Application materials
  ① Gyousai University standardized CV (with photo; digital signature acceptable)
  ② List of research achievements (categorized into peer-reviewed papers, international conferences, books, patents, social activities, etc.)
  ③ Five representative works (PDF)
  ④ Summary of research and teaching achievements (approx. 2,000 characters in Japanese / 1,000 words in English)
  ⑤ Future research and teaching plan (approx. 2,000 characters in Japanese / 1,000 words in English)
  ⑥ Names, affiliations, and contact information for two referees able to provide an assessment

9. Selection process and schedule
  Document screening (late Sep–early Oct) → First-round interview (mid-Oct, online) → Second-round interview and mock lecture (early Nov, in-person on Hongo Campus in principle) → Final review (approval by internal faculty committee) → Offer (notification by end of Dec 2024) → Hiring procedures (Jan–Mar 2025).

10. Employment conditions
  Specialized discretionary labor system (deemed as 7 hours 45 minutes of work per day). Salary under Gyousai University National University Corporation’s annual salary system, determined based on education, work experience, and achievements. One salary increase and one bonus per year. Full benefits including various allowances (commuting, housing, dependent) and social insurance. Six-month probationary period.

11. Diversity and inclusion
  The University proactively promotes gender equality and the recruitment of foreign nationals and persons with disabilities. Career interruptions due to childbirth, childcare, caregiving, etc., are taken into account in performance evaluations.

12. Application and inquiries
  (a) Web application: JSIS-IN Portal “Gyousai University Graduate School of Informatics Associate Professor (Science, Technology, and Society)” application form.
  (b) Inquiries: recruit_sts2024@iii.u-gyosai.ac.jp (Recruitment Office)
──────────────────────────────────────────
Notes: These guidelines were announced on June 1, 2024 and distributed via internal/external bulletin boards, academic mailing lists, and research networks. If updates occur, the online recruitment guidelines will be revised immediately and all applicants will be notified by email.

[Extract from Gyousai University Faculty Employment Regulations] (Revised April 1, 2023)
──────────────────────────────────────────
Article 4 (Transfers and Concurrent Appointments)
Professors, associate professors, lecturers, and assistant professors may be ordered to hold concurrent appointments with other graduate schools, attached research institutes, international collaboration hubs, etc. designated by the University when necessary for education and research. In such cases, the University shall seek the opinion of the Career Development Support Committee while respecting the individual’s wishes.

Article 7 (Working Hours)
For faculty members, a specialized discretionary labor system applies, and they are deemed to have worked 7 hours and 45 minutes per day. Even when online meetings are held late at night for international joint research or similar activities, applications for compensatory leave are permitted.

Article 10 (Leave)
In the first year, 20 days of annual paid leave are granted, as well as a summer shutdown (three consecutive days in mid-August) and refresh leave (five days for every 10 years of service). Special leave categories include volunteer activity leave, lay judge (jury duty) leave, and family care leave.

Article 15 (Prevention of Harassment)
All faculty and staff must complete annual harassment prevention training (e-learning). If a violation is confirmed, the matter will be referred to the Disciplinary Committee.
