negative_chunk_list.content:
Basics of Lichens and Principles of Scientific Name Notation

Lichens are symbiotic organisms composed of fungi (mainly ascomycetes) and algae (green algae or cyanobacteria), forming a unique life form that is neither a standalone plant nor an animal. Their morphologies are broadly categorized as fruticose, foliose, or crustose, and their modes of attachment to substrates (bark, rock, soil, artificial materials) help facilitate field identification. They generally grow slowly and readily reflect ambient air quality, moisture, and light conditions, which is why they are often used as indicator species.

Basics of Scientific Names (in accordance with the ICNafp)
- Binomial nomenclature: Genus (capitalized) + specific epithet (lowercase). Example: Genus species, written in italics.
- Author citation (authorship): Abbreviate the proposer’s name; use parentheses if a taxonomic change has occurred. Example: Xxx yyy (A.) B.
- Synonyms: Because multiple names may be assigned over the course of research history, organizing synonymy in specimens and literature is important. It is recommended to cross-check with the latest checklists and databases and indicate the accepted name.
- Varieties and subspecies: Use subsp. and var. to denote rank. In conservation contexts, it is necessary to clarify whether the assessment unit is a “species” or an infraspecific taxon.

Relationship with Japanese Names
Japanese vernacular names can vary by region and era, so prioritizing scientific names is preferable from the standpoint of standardized notation. Especially in Red List listings, cross-referencing the vernacular name, scientific name, and listing number (vernacular → scientific → line number) helps prevent misreadings.

Guide to Understanding the Red List Assessment Framework and “Categories”
In principle, national Red Lists align with IUCN assessment criteria, while sometimes adding supplementary notes tailored to domestic circumstances. Even after an assessment is determined, it is periodically reviewed, and categories may be upgraded or downgraded as data are updated (e.g., discovery of new localities, re-evaluation of population dynamics).

Overview of IUCN Criteria (Criteria A–E)
- A: Population reduction rate (percentage decline over a set period in the past, present, or future)
- B: Geographic range contraction (EOO: extent of occurrence; AOO: area of occupancy; plus conditions on fragmentation, number of populations/locations)
- C: Small population size and continuing decline
- D: Very small population size and/or very restricted distribution
- E: Quantitative analysis of extinction risk (PVA)

Example of National List Display
National Red Lists typically present categories in parallel such as “Extinct (EX),” “Extinct in the Wild (EW),” “Critically Endangered (CR),” “Endangered (EN),” “Vulnerable (VU),” “Near Threatened (NT),” and “Data Deficient (DD).” Units and annotations are noted in the upper right of the table and in footnotes, so be sure to check the legend at the beginning.

Assessment Process and Sources
- Specimen information: Collection labels, collectors, coordinates, and substrate data registered in museum and university herbaria.
- Literature: Regional journals, floras, and online databases. Older records require attention to place-name changes and coordinate system conversions.
- Recent surveys: Citizen science photo submissions, reports of DNA barcoding, and regular monitoring in protected areas.

Organization of ecology, distribution, and substrates (lichens in general)
Lichens are sensitive to microenvironments; small differences in substrate, humidity, light, and atmospheric chemistry can greatly alter species composition. The following is a general overview and does not refer to specific species.

Trends by substrate
- Corticolous (on tree trunks): Attached to the bark of broadleaf and coniferous trees. Bark pH, tree age, and bark roughness have an influence. Relative humidity within the forest and distance from the forest floor are also important.
- Saxicolous (on rocks): Species composition varies with rock type such as granite, limestone, and basalt. Coastal rocks are affected by salt spray, while inland rocks provide settlement surfaces through microcracks formed by freeze–thaw cycles.
- Terricolous (on soil): Found in nutrient-poor sandy areas and alpine barrens; susceptible to trampling and erosion.
- Artificial substrates: Concrete, tiles, metal. As urban air quality improves, there are reports of diversity recovering even in city centers.

Climate zones and elevation
- In temperate coastal fog belts, foliose and fruticose communities tend to develop, whereas crustose forms tend to dominate in inland arid regions.
- At high elevations, morphologies adapted to UV and cold stress are observed, and unique assemblages form along snowbed margins and in wind-exposed sites.

Interactions and functions
- Because they reflect atmospheric nitrogen deposition and the accumulation of trace metals, they are used for biomonitoring.
- They contribute to fine-scale substrate erosion and water retention, and provide microhabitats for other organisms (arthropods, microalgae).

General survey notes
- Even at the same site, composition varies with aspect (north-facing vs. south-facing) and height on the trunk, so introducing stratification within plots improves reproducibility.

Field Survey and Identification Workflow (Protocol/Template)
This outlines a standard process from on-site reconnaissance to data recording. It is a general-purpose procedure for practitioners, aimed at ensuring data quality rather than evaluating category status for individual species.

1) Preparations
- Collect existing literature and specimen data; check expected distribution and access methods.
- Prepare survey forms, GPS, hand lens, camera, cutting tool, zipper bags, ethanol, desiccant, and safety gear.

2) Field Plot Setup
- Establish multiple standard plots (e.g., 10×10 m) and set up substrate-specific subplots. For tree trunks, create an observation band at 1.3 m height (breast height) and record by aspect.
- Take photographs in the order of wide shot → mid shot → macro. To avoid glare and blown highlights, shoot under diffuse light when possible.

3) Specimen Collection and Labeling
- Minimize collection quantity to reduce impact on communities. Labels should include site name, coordinates (WGS84), elevation, substrate, collector, date, and environmental notes.
- Dry quickly at room temperature using desiccants. Do not seal specimens while moist (to prevent mold).

4) Laboratory Identification
- Stereomicroscope for preliminary observation → chemical tests (K/C/P) → thin-layer chromatography (TLC) → sectional observations as needed (cortex, medulla, hymenium).
- Integrate morphological, chemical, and ecological information, and rate identification confidence in stages (High/Medium/Low).

5) DNA Barcoding (Optional)
- Amplify and sequence primarily the ITS region. Cross-reference sequences with public databases and assess relatedness using phylogenetic trees.

Threat Factors, Conservation Measures, and Monitoring Design (General Perspective on Lichens)

In lichen conservation, the key is whether local microenvironments can be maintained. Below is a summary of common threats and examples of countermeasure design.

Main Threat Factors
- Air pollution: Increases in SO₂, NOₓ, NH₃, and suspended particulates readily lead to the loss of highly sensitive species.
- Land-use change: Logging, land development, urbanization, and changes to forest edges alter substrate availability and humidity regimes.
- Excessive trampling and collecting: Pronounced in terricolous and saxicolous types. Caution is needed at popular tourist sites and along hiking trails.
- Invasive species and herbivory pressure: High deer densities alter the understory vegetation; introduction of non-native algae, etc.
- Climate change: Increasing aridity, higher temperatures, and more frequent extreme events can expand or contract distributional limits.

Examples of Conservation Measures
- Identification of key sites: Conduct integrated evaluation of substrate continuity, stand structure, and microclimate, and implement zoning.
- Buffer design: Set distances from logging boundaries and roads to mitigate the intrusion of dust and desiccating winds.
- Air quality measures: Improve on-site emissions inventories, implement road-dust reduction measures, and plan for humidification.
- Long-term monitoring: Repeat observations annually and seasonally in fixed plots. Combine photo points with environmental loggers (temperature/humidity and nitrogen deposition).

Monitoring Design Checklist
1. Clarify objectives (trend detection / evaluation of management effectiveness)
2. Sampling design (stratified randomization, number of replicates, statistical power)
3. Selection of indicators (cover, occurrence frequency, diversity indices, chemical reaction profiles)

Data Management, Specimen Information, and Public Disclosure Policy (for practitioners)
To enhance the reproducibility of conservation assessments, specimens, observations, images, and sequences must be managed integratively. Below are best practices for data management.

Required fields for specimen labels
- Locality name, coordinates (WGS84), elevation, date, collector
- Substrate (bark species, rock type, ground), growth conditions (cover, community structure)
- Field code and subsequently determined name (with confidence level)

Database design
- Normalization: separate observations (event), occurrences, measurements, and images (media), and cross-reference them.
- Versioning: ensure traceability when scientific names are updated; maintain synonymy relationships as a graph.
- Quality flags: manage flags for coordinate accuracy, identification confidence, presence of photos, and presence of DNA data.

Open data and considerations
- To promote research, provide semi-open data (blurred coordinates, 1 km gridding) and conceal location information according to the threat status.
- Clearly indicate image licenses (e.g., CC BY). Include shooting equipment, exposure, and scale in metadata.

Citation and reuse
- Provide datasets with DOIs in a form that allows reanalysis. Include analysis scripts and ensure long-term preservation.
- For secondary use in reports and educational materials, state whether modifications were made and cite the source.

Note: This chapter provides general guidelines on data handling and does not substantiate scientific names of particular taxa or Red List categories. Always verify specific values in the relevant entries of official sources.

Tips for Reading Reports (PDF/Markdown) and Reconstructing Tables
Red lists of lichens are often distributed as PDFs, and readability can suffer due to merged cells or vertical layouts. Below are practical tips for reading and reconstruction.

Reading Tips
- First, grasp the legend (category symbols, units) and the document structure, then navigate to the target taxonomic group (lichens).
- Read tables with an awareness of header hierarchy (Category → Japanese name → Scientific name). Avoid screenshots taken mid-row, as they tend to lose correspondences.
- Manual correction is essential for garbled text and OCR errors (long vowels, hyphens, Greek letters).

Normalization to Markdown Example
1. Columns: Listing No. | Category | Japanese Name | Scientific Name
2. Units and notes: Move to the beginning or footnotes
3. Unmerge cells: Fully expand repeated division or group names
4. Schema validation: Check consistency for missing values, duplicates, and synonyms

Table Reconstruction Template
| Listing No. | Red List Category | Japanese Name | Scientific Name |
|:--|:--|:--|:--|
| (e.g.) LI0xxx | (e.g.) Endangered IB (EN) | (e.g.) — | (e.g.) Genus species |

Quality Control
- Double-checking by two or more people and using a self-review checklist.
- Attach version tags (v1.0, v1.1, …) and publish the revision history.

negative_chunk_list.content:
Particularly in cloud-related businesses and the security solutions domain, precise product development and timely market launches proved successful against the backdrop of rising social demand for DX (digital transformation). In addition, R&D expenses increased from 150 million yen in the first quarter to 240 million yen in the sixteenth quarter, reflecting an active stance on investing in next-generation technologies. This has accelerated the speed of new product launches and strengthened competitiveness in global markets. Moreover, proper management of SG&A expenses and steady expansion in overseas markets (Asia, Europe, and the Americas) boosted overall revenue. The yen’s depreciation also served as a tailwind for some businesses and contributed to improved foreign exchange gains.

Outlook and strategy
Going forward, while flexibly responding to technological innovation in the digital domain, we will continue to pursue sustainable growth by placing ESG management (environment, social, and governance) at the core of our operations.

Specifically, we will advance initiatives centered on:
- Expanding investment in cutting-edge areas such as cloud, security, and AI
- Developing next-generation platforms and strengthening subscription-based business models
- Promoting global expansion (establishing local subsidiaries and advancing strategic alliances)
- Fostering talent and promoting diversity

We kindly ask for the continued support of our shareholders and investors.

Definition and accounting position of operating profit (general overview)
Operating profit is a core metric for measuring a company’s earning power from its main business and is defined as gross profit minus SG&A (selling, general and administrative expenses). It excludes “non-core” items such as financial income and expenses, equity-method gains and losses, foreign exchange gains and losses, and valuation gains and losses on investment securities. From an accounting standards perspective, IFRS does not necessarily prescribe “operating profit” as a defined concept, but many companies disclose it as a Management Performance Measure, which requires consistency and reproducibility of its components. Under Japanese GAAP (J-GAAP), there is an established practice of classified presentation, and the delineation from non-operating income/expenses and extraordinary gains/losses is emphasized in practice. R&D expenses are generally included in SG&A, but the capitalization and amortization of intangible assets varies by standards and company policy, which can affect how operating profit is impacted. Depreciation can be allocated to both cost of goods manufactured and SG&A, so the design of cost accounting can shift the balance between gross profit and SG&A. The operating margin is the ratio of operating profit to sales and is a representative indicator of efficiency, pricing power, and the soundness of the cost structure. However, it should not be assessed in isolation; it should be viewed alongside segment composition, regional price and wage levels, supply chain tightness, and the time lag in price pass-through. Furthermore, seasonal factors and one-off expenses (large trade shows, new product launches, hiring ramp-ups, system renewals, etc.) are major causes of quarterly volatility and may be smoothed out on an annual basis.

[Meaning of CSV Columns and Practical Handling Tips]
Quarterly performance CSVs typically include columns such as “Net Sales (hundred million yen),” “Gross Profit (hundred million yen),” “SG&A Expenses (hundred million yen),” “Operating Income (hundred million yen),” “Operating Margin (%),” and “Net Income (hundred million yen).” When the unit is indicated as “hundred million yen,” be sure to standardize units (e.g., to yen, or to thousand/ten-thousand yen) before aggregation or charting to prevent discrepancies when reconciling with other data sources. Column names often cause processing failures due to full-width/half-width characters or stray spaces; for example, variations in parentheses for “Net Sales (hundred million yen),” and the “Quarter” column values (“第1四半期,” “Q1,” “1Q,” etc.) should be normalized. If CSV commas as delimiters coexist with numeric thousands separators (e.g., 1,234), parsers may misinterpret the data, so it’s recommended to strip formatting commas from numeric strings during preprocessing. For percentage columns, explicitly remove the “%” symbol and convert between percent and decimal representations. For quarterly CSVs without date columns, it’s important to ensure quarter ordering (by adding a sorting key) and to manage fiscal year boundaries (full-year cutoffs). When handling columns that mix flows and stocks—such as backlog, capital expenditures, and depreciation—take care not to confuse flows (in-period movements) with stocks (end-of-period balances). Furthermore, when segment sales (e.g., IT, Manufacturing, Services) are listed alongside company-wide sales, implement a consistency check between total company sales and the sum of segments (detecting aggregation discrepancies) to catch input errors early. Lastly, differences in character encoding (UTF-8/with or without BOM) and line endings (LF/CRLF) can also be pitfalls when integrating across BI tools.

[Designing Consistency Checks and Derived Metrics for Validation]

When parsing quarterly CSVs, preparing mechanical validation rules increases reliability. The basic forms are: (1) Operating profit ≈ Gross profit − SG&A, and (2) Operating margin ≈ Operating profit ÷ Revenue × 100. Gross profit is revenue minus cost of goods sold (COGS), but if COGS is not directly available in the CSV, you can back out and check consistency using operating profit and SG&A as a substitute. For example, in a hypothetical case where revenue is 1,000, operating profit is 120, and SG&A is 200, gross profit would be roughly 320, which reconciles (in practice, rounding and minor reclassifications can cause deviations of a few units). Columns expressed as “rates” can show slight mismatches due to differences in decimal precision and rounding; it is practical to set a tolerance (e.g., ±0.1 percentage point) as a threshold for automated decisions.

Stock-type columns (backlog, cash and equivalents, interest-bearing debt) are useful for viewing changes versus the prior period-end in relation to cash flows. For instance, in periods of capital investment or intensified R&D, free cash flow tends to be squeezed, and a leading increase in depreciation is expected concurrently. If you can obtain the breakdown of SG&A (personnel costs, advertising and promotion, office rent, IT systems costs, etc.), separate items that move flexibly year-over-year/quarter-over-quarter from more fixed-cost items to estimate elasticity. Furthermore, at a quarterly granularity, SG&A can swell at certain times due to seasonal factors (bonuses, major trade shows, year-end closing costs, etc.), so it is helpful to use annual and most recent rolling 12-month totals in parallel to distinguish “structural levels” from “one-off fluctuations.”

Revenue Structure and Margin Thinking for Hybrid Companies

For companies that operate both manufacturing and IT services, revenue structures tend to blend “cost-oriented” and “subscription-oriented” models, causing operating profit profiles to differ significantly across segments. Manufacturing has a high external procurement ratio for materials, components, and logistics, and is heavily influenced by raw material markets and exchange rates. Profit margins fluctuate based on yield improvements, production planning, and purchasing strategies (futures, long-term contracts, price escalation clauses). In contrast, IT, cloud, and security are centered on upfront development investments and ongoing operating costs, where subscription adoption, upselling, and lower churn drive cumulative profit growth. In services, utilization, pricing, and training efficiency are key. Consequently, the company-wide operating margin is heavily swayed by segment mix, making straightforward peer comparisons difficult.

Selling expenses (sales staff, agency commissions, marketing initiatives) and general and administrative expenses (back-office functions, information systems, internal controls) also carry different weights by segment, and the method used to allocate shared costs (apportionment by revenue, gross profit, or hours worked, etc.) can shift reported profits. While R&D tends to be expensed in the short term, over the medium to long term it is the source of product differentiation and pricing power. Moreover, revenue recognition by contract type—one-off sales, recurring maintenance/renewals, or bundled arrangements—changes the timing of revenue for the same deal and introduces quarter-to-quarter volatility.

Decoding KPIs: Backlog, Capital Expenditure, and Capital Efficiency
When quarterly materials include backlog, it functions as “expected inventory” for future revenue generation and serves as a leading indicator of operating profit. If backlog continues to increase steadily while on-time delivery rates and gross margins are maintained, operational efficiency in subsequent periods is likely to improve. The relationship between capital investment and depreciation creates a timing gap between cash and profit. In phases where investment is front-loaded, cash outflows come first; later, depreciation hits the P&L, and as utilization ramps, recovery begins through gross profit—understanding this back-and-forth movement of funds and profit is crucial. The equity ratio and the level of interest-bearing debt indicate resilience in a downturn and room for growth investment. ROE and ROA are summary indicators of capital efficiency, but you should identify the drivers by breaking them down into their components (asset turnover, operating margin, and leverage). Companies with ample cash balances can act nimbly—such as building inventory during procurement price swings or hedging during currency fluctuations. Policies on dividends and share buybacks also convey a message about the balance between short-term shareholder returns and medium- to long-term growth investment. Pay attention to consistency between full-year and quarterly results, and avoid mistaking fluctuations in a single quarter for “structural change” by consistently checking trends using moving averages and year-over-year comparisons.

Key points for interpreting IR materials and anticipated Q&A:
In investor presentation materials, following the quarterly summary, the main topics typically include segment trends, bridges for cost of goods sold and SG&A, foreign exchange impacts, and progress on price revisions and contract renewals. Likely questions include: (1) To what extent were increases in raw material and outsourcing costs absorbed through price pass-through? (2) How does the temporary increase in SG&A from strengthened hiring and training correlate with productivity improvements next fiscal year? (3) What proportion of the order backlog consists of high-margin products and services? (4) What are the trends in cloud churn rates and upsell initiatives? (5) How are geopolitical risks in the supply chain being managed and what is the inventory strategy? In the bridge chart (drivers of operating profit changes), the crux of management’s accountability is whether they can explain, both quantitatively and qualitatively, how volume, price, mix, cost, fixed expenses, and FX each contributed. If there have been changes in accounting treatment or segment realignment, check whether retrospective comparisons are disclosed so as not to be misled by “apparent growth” in investment decisions. Since special or non-recurring gains and losses can significantly swing net income, the basic approach is to assess them separately from operating income. Furthermore, while investments in internal controls and information security are often expensed in the short term, they contribute to medium- to long-term value by preventing major incidents and enhancing reliability. In IR, prioritize whether not only the short-term outlook for “the next quarter” but also the “three-year” transformation of the business portfolio and redesign of the earnings structure are being discussed; this helps discern the soundness of the strategy behind the quarterly figures.

[Dataset design perspective: leak prevention and difficulty design]
When building a QA dataset about corporate performance, the top priority is preventing the “context” from leaking numbers that directly answer the question. In particular, including context that touches a specific row/column for a quarter (e.g., the cell for operating profit) or presents formulas that can derive the same value (such as revenue × operating margin) with concrete numbers will cause leaks. Negative chunks should consist of peripheral information—definitions, accounting concepts, practical cautions, industry generalities, analysis procedures, how to read IR materials, meanings of KPIs—without including the relevant figures and without contradicting them; this is the design policy. Also, intentionally mixing “traps” that models often stumble on—such as unit differences (yen/millions of yen/thousands of yen), notation variations, and differences in quarter naming (Q1/first quarter)—helps evaluate the robustness of retrievers. To stage difficulty, start with (1) direct extraction (cell reference) as the base, then expand to (2) problems requiring interpretation of column meaning, (3) problems requiring reconciliation/filtering across multiple rows, and (4) problems involving unit conversion and rounding. In addition to EM as an evaluation metric, prepare a score that accounts for tolerances in units, rounding, and formatting to approximate real-world operations. Finally, establish a CI pipeline that automatically runs consistency checks on synthetic data (consistency of derived metrics, agreement of segment totals, consistency between full-year and quarterly figures), and monitor for metric regressions with regression tests when updating the dataset, yielding a QA corpus that is sustainable for long-term operation.

reasoning_content: Since the “Operating profit (million yen)” column in the “Q4” row of the given table shows 650, the operating profit is 650 million yen (i.e., 6.50 hundred million yen).

question:
In the election of directors of the Gensei City Consumer Protection Association, suppose a regular member meets the following conditions:
- They have been in arrears on membership dues for two years.
- They suffered significant damage due to a disaster and have applied for a waiver of membership dues.
- They have submitted a candidacy filing to the Election Administration Committee.

Does this regular member have the right to stand as a candidate in the director election?
answer: This full member has the right to stand as a candidate in the board of directors election.

positive_chunk_list.content:
[Regulations of the Gensei City Consumer Protection Association]

(Review)
Article 5 The Membership Qualification Review Committee shall elect a chairperson from among its members and review the application documents and performance materials of prospective members.

Article 6 The Membership Qualification Review Committee shall notify full members of a list of prospective members who have passed the initial screening and accept opinions from full members.

(Decision on Admission)
Article 7 The Membership Qualification Review Committee shall report the results of its deliberations to the General Assembly, and the General Assembly shall decide whether to approve admission.

(Admission Procedures)
Article 8 Those approved for admission shall, after completing the prescribed procedures, become full members as defined in Article 5, Paragraph 1, Item 1 of the Articles of Incorporation.

Chapter 3 Membership Fees
(Annual Membership Fee Amount)
Article 9 The annual membership fee for full members shall be 20,000 yen.

(Payment of Membership Fees)
Article 10 Membership fees shall be paid in advance.

Article 11 A person who is in arrears for membership fees for three years or more shall lose full membership status.

(Exemption from Membership Fees)
Article 12 For full members who have suffered significant damage due to a disaster, separate standards shall be established, and based on the member’s request, membership fees shall be exempted for a certain period. In addition, full members who were exempted from membership fees in the Gensei City Consumer Protection Association (unincorporated) shall continue to be exempted.

...

Chapter 5 Operation of the General Assembly
(Convening the General Assembly)
Article 14 To convene a General Assembly, no later than three weeks before the date of the Assembly, a written notice indicating the agenda must be posted on the office bulletin board and announced in the Association’s bulletin to notify all full members.

(Minutes)
Article 15 Minutes shall be prepared for the proceedings of the General Assembly and announced to full members through the Association’s bulletin.

(Other Necessary Matters)
Article 16 Other matters necessary for the operation of the General Assembly shall be governed by the Act on General Incorporated Associations and General Incorporated Foundations and the Association’s Articles of Incorporation.

Chapter 6 Election of Directors
(Suffrage and Eligibility for Election)
Article 17 Full members have the right to vote and to stand for election as directors. Regardless of whether there is self-nomination, votes may be cast for all full members except those who lack eligibility under Article 23 of these Regulations.

(Election Administration Committee)
Article 18 In conducting elections, the President shall appoint approximately 15 full members as Election Administration Committee members, and the Election Administration Committee shall elect a chairperson from among its members.

negative_chunk_list.content:
The election management committee edits and publishes the election bulletin based on manuscripts submitted by candidates. The bulletin will be A4 size in full color, and each candidate’s space will be limited to one page, with the layout arranged in the following order: headline, headshot, brief biography, statement of beliefs, and contact information. Headshots must be taken within the last six months, and JPEG or PNG files (resolution of 300 dpi or higher) will be accepted. The brief biography should succinctly describe educational background, work experience, and activity record within organizations. Bullet points are allowed for the statement of beliefs, but defamation, promotion of specific companies or individuals, solicitation of donations, and product introductions are strictly prohibited. Body text must be at least 10.5 pt; the use of overly decorative fonts is prohibited to ensure readability, and color schemes must follow guidelines considerate of color vision diversity.

As a rule, submissions cannot be replaced after submission; however, if obvious typographical errors are found during proofreading, correction requests will be accepted within the deadline. The bulletin PDF will be posted on the official website after the publication date, and a text version will be released simultaneously for people with visual impairments. Distribution of the bulletin will be by mail based on the voter registry, and handling of addresses will be strictly managed in accordance with the personal information protection policy. To ensure neutrality in editing, the size of photos, word counts, and area of space will be equal among candidates, and no editorial opinions or annotations will be inserted. In addition, a contact point and FAQ regarding the contents published in the bulletin will be established to help prevent misunderstandings and disputes.

Voting is conducted using a double-envelope method: the ballot is placed in an unmarked inner envelope, which is sealed, then placed in an outer envelope on which the voter writes their name and voter number before returning it. The outer envelope is printed with the return address and marked for postage paid by the recipient. The arrival deadline is strict receipt on election day; validity is not determined by postmark date. Regarding postal delays or loss, the election management committee will respond within the scope of the postal operator’s general terms, but no individual compensation will be provided. To prevent tampering during return transit, a tamper-detection special ink is used on the flap of the outer envelope; if signs of opening are detected before the count, preservation procedures will be taken and the facts recorded. After arrival at the ballot box, the seal status is checked in the presence of observers and the materials are stored in a locked repository. Even if there is an address change during the election period, the voter may use the envelope sent to the address on file at the time of dispatch. If the entries on the outer envelope are incomplete (such as missing name, mismatched voter number, or missing signature), a verification process will be conducted for identity, and if doubts cannot be resolved, the ballot will be invalidated. For overseas residents, international postal delays are anticipated; they should use the designated international reply envelope and are advised to mail ahead of the deadline. In principle, reissuance of ballots is not permitted, but if loss or damage is evident, the election management committee may authorize reissuance at its discretion. To complete the ballot, write only one candidate’s name; to prevent double voting, corrections to the candidate’s name are not allowed. Ballots with incomplete or incorrect entries will be invalid.

Ballot counting shall be conducted publicly at a venue designated by the Election Management Committee, under the supervision of auditors and observers selected from each candidate’s camp. Ballot sorting shall be performed by teams of at least two, and shall proceed through three stages: initial tally, re-check, and final verification. Voters are encouraged to write in legible block script; even if a candidate’s name contains a kanji error, the vote shall be counted as valid if the candidate can be identified. Conversely, entries written outside the frame, listings that exceed the maximum number of candidates, indecipherable scribbles, and the inclusion of any identifiable symbol in addition to a name shall be deemed invalid votes. Ballots rendered illegible due to damage or creasing shall be restored to the greatest extent possible, and the restoration process shall be documented and recorded on video as evidence. Counting results shall be transcribed onto tally sheets, signed by both the counter and the verifier at each table, and then centrally consolidated. In the event of a tie that does not determine ranking, a lot-drawing procedure prescribed by the regulations shall be applied, with an independent third party witnessing the draw to ensure fairness. If observers present at the count raise procedural doubts, they may immediately file an objection; objections shall be submitted in writing with reasons, upon which the Chair of the Election Management Committee shall issue a provisional decision on the spot, to be finalized later through review as necessary. The final results shall be announced in the newsletter and on the official website, accompanied by detailed figures for each ballot box, the number of invalid votes, and the number of objections.

To ensure fairness, candidates are subject to certain restrictions on public relations activities during the election period. Use of social media is permitted, but the dissemination of misinformation via anonymous accounts, automated posting by bots, and the publication of videos containing manipulative editing are prohibited. If a candidate sets up a webpage to explain their policies and achievements, they must specify the operator, contact information, and update history, and respond to inquiries within a set period. In-person campaign speeches must be filed in advance with the election management committee, including schedule and venue, and coordinated to avoid interfering with other candidates at the same venue and time. Distribution of goods is limited to printed materials for informational purposes (pamphlets, business cards, flyers); the provision of food and beverages, money, or gift certificates is prohibited. Volunteers may be mobilized, but any honoraria are limited to actual transportation expenses, and it is not permitted to disguise an employment relationship to provide de facto compensation. If endorsers are listed, the candidate must obtain their consent, and any display of academic titles, job titles, or awards must be supported by documentation. Expressions that incite defamation or discrimination, false representations of achievements, and the disclosure of other candidates’ personal information are strictly prohibited. If a violation of the guidelines is identified, the election management committee will take graduated measures, such as written warnings, requests to retrieve the relevant promotional materials, demands for website corrections, and recommendations for public apologies.

The Election Management Committee is operated based on the three principles of fairness, transparency, and promptness. Meetings are convened with a quorum, and resolutions are decided by a majority of the members present. Minutes are prepared including summaries of statements and decisions, and after finalization, a summarized version is published on the official website.

Objections regarding the election must be submitted in writing within seven days of the announcement of results, clearly stating the petitioner’s name, contact information, the act in question, the facts, a list of evidentiary materials, and the relief sought. Reviews are handled by a subcommittee of three or more members and may include, as necessary, hearings from the parties, submission of additional evidence, and consultation with experts. Possible outcomes of the review include dismissal, corrective recommendations, partial redo of procedures, or recounts. If a serious procedural violation is confirmed, the committee may order the invalidation of the tally or a rerun in the affected precinct.

Any member with a conflict of interest shall recuse themselves from the agenda item, and an alternate member will participate in the review. After the election, the committee prepares a comprehensive report compiling issues, improvement proposals, and procedural revision plans for the next cycle, which is submitted to the Board of Directors. As an exception, appeals against decisions of the Election Management Committee are to be deliberated not by the Board of Directors but at the next General Assembly, ensuring transparency. The purpose of these measures is to preserve the independence and fairness of the Election Management Committee.

During the election period, candidates may inform full members of their principles and policies. Approved means of dissemination include distributing flyers within the prescribed limit, sending individual letters at one’s own expense, posting on bulletin boards designated by the association, and holding online briefings (using conference rooms provided by the association or tools designated by the committee). In outreach activities, candidates must handle the membership roster with care and protect personal information; they must not send mailings or electronic solicitations to members listed on the association’s do-not-contact list (those who have expressed refusal to receive communications). Using the association’s work mailing lists or the emergency contact network managed by the secretariat for election purposes is prohibited. The following are violations of the guidelines: soliciting donations, providing goods, offering lotteries or premiums, making abusive phone calls, visiting late at night or early in the morning, using false titles, defaming other candidates, using discriminatory expressions, and using the association’s logo or name in a manner that may cause misunderstanding. If a violation is suspected, the Election Management Committee may investigate and take corrective measures such as issuing warnings, removing posted materials, or suspending the provision of posting space in subsequent periods. These corrective measures are intended to ensure the propriety of election campaigning and do not immediately affect the candidate’s rights (the fact of candidacy) itself. Candidates may summarize and report to the committee records of their outreach activities (date, venue, number of participants, tools used), and the committee will strive to ensure equal opportunities for all candidates from the standpoint of fairness.

The Election Management Committee shall prepare the voter registry used for sending voting cards and verifying voting eligibility. The registry shall include only the minimum information necessary for the administration of the election, such as full name of regular members, membership number, contact information (mailing address or preferred contact), and residential area classification. The source data for the registry shall be the membership ledger held by the Secretariat, reflecting the most recent change notifications (address changes, name changes, contact changes, etc.). The registry shall be strictly managed under the responsibility of the Election Management Committee, and copies shall not be provided to candidates or third parties. If a candidate needs to contact members, a “forwarding” method via the Committee may be used (the Committee supports address printing and mailing only; the content is prepared by the candidate). Access to the registry shall be permitted solely for the purpose of verifying voting eligibility and only to committee members engaged in the ballot counting process and observers designated by the Committee. Handling of registry information shall comply with the personal information protection regulations, and after the election concludes, it shall be disposed of appropriately following the prescribed retention period. Cases such as undeliverable or address-unknown returned ballot envelopes shall be fed back to the Secretariat to improve the accuracy of registry updates. Inquiries regarding the registry shall, upon identity verification, be accommodated for corrections, deletions, and additions to the recorded information, provided such handling does not impede the progress of the election.

Voters fill in the candidate’s name on the prescribed ballot and return it using a double-envelope system (inner envelope = ballot; outer envelope = member number and sender’s signature field). After eligibility is verified, the outer envelope is detached, and only the inner envelope is placed in the ballot box, thereby preserving ballot secrecy. Ballot counting is carried out in parallel by multiple counting teams under the supervision of the chair, with mutual cross-checks for reading and tallying of votes. Ballots that are difficult to read (illegible, multiple names written, nonstandard symbols, etc.) are judged valid or invalid according to uniform criteria. The reasons for deeming a ballot invalid are recorded in the tally report for later verification. The results are compiled in the order of subtotals to totals and reconciled by an independent audit officer through recalculation. The counting process can be monitored by observers appointed by the election management committee. Observers do not directly handle ballots, but if they judge that there are doubts about procedural propriety, they may lodge an objection with the chair. After counting concludes, the committee announces the results in the newsletter and on the bulletin board, and sets a period for objections (for example, about seven days). Objections must be submitted in writing and must specify concrete facts, grounds, and the scope of impact. The committee will conduct partial verification checks or recounts as necessary and will publish its conclusions.

As a general rule, directors’ terms of office commence on the date the election results are finalized; however, until the inaugural board meeting approves the allocation of roles following completion of onboarding procedures, the outgoing directors may hand over and assist with duties. Newly elected directors must sign a letter of acceptance and submit a self-declaration regarding conflicts of interest. If a vacancy arises during the term due to resignation, death, relocation, extended medical leave, or similar reasons, a substitute is selected through the prescribed procedures, or advancement is considered. Advancement customarily follows the order of votes from the election, filling the vacancy with the next-highest unelected candidate from the same election who does not decline, in sequence. If an advancement candidate has been simultaneously selected for multiple positions or a different committee, the permissibility of holding multiple roles is adjusted in line with the individual’s wishes and the consistency of the regulations. If a vacancy occurs near the end of the term, it is possible to forego advancement and leave the selection to the next election. As a rule, the term of a director appointed through a substitute selection or advancement is the remainder of the predecessor’s term. These practices regarding terms, appointments, and vacancy filling constitute a framework to ensure fairness and continuity in elections and stability of operations, and are separate from provisions determining eligibility for election or requirements for candidacy. However, in substitute selections or advancements, it is a prerequisite that the candidate held eligibility for election in the previous election.

Directors bear responsibility for the association’s sustainable development, including formulating operational policies, reviewing budgets and financial statements, approving business plans, establishing regulations, and determining committee structures. The board of directors, in principle, holds regular meetings quarterly and may convene special meetings as needed. Resolutions are adopted by a majority of directors present, provided a quorum is met. However, matters such as significant asset acquisitions or disposals and changes to mid- to long-term plans may require a special majority.

Notices convening meetings must specify the agenda, deadlines for submitting materials, whether online participation is permitted, and methods for submitting written proxies or voting instructions. If a director or their close relatives have a conflict of interest with respect to the matter under deliberation, the director must declare it in advance and recuse themselves from the deliberation and vote on that agenda item. Managing conflicts of interest is a fundamental principle for maintaining fairness and transparency in decision-making; if a violation is suspected, the ethics committee (or an equivalent function) will investigate and recommend corrective measures.

Minutes of board meetings, including key points, attendees, resolutions, the gist of dissenting opinions, and ancillary matters, must be properly preserved. These role assignments and principles of meeting operation set the code of conduct for directors after their election and are not provisions for determining candidate eligibility or the rights of members at the time of an election. Accordingly, a director is a position involved in the association’s governance, and in the association’s operations, the eligibility of regular members to stand for election is an important factor.

The association will implement accessibility measures in the election process to ensure participation opportunities for a diverse membership. As a rule, reasonable accommodations will be provided, such as braille versions of notices, large-print editions, screen-reader-friendly PDFs, color schemes considerate of color vision diversity, and a phone-based notice reading service (by reservation).

If a natural disaster or a major disruption occurs before the postal voting deadline and widespread mail delays are anticipated, the Election Management Committee may decide on special measures tailored to the extent of the impact, such as extending deadlines, re-sending materials, or providing alternative methods.

When holding online candidate briefings, consideration will be given to participants’ connectivity, and recordings will be published later along with distribution of summary texts.

For full members residing overseas and members who will be absent for an extended period, schedules will be set to allow sufficient shipping time, and early dispatch or trackable methods will be used as needed.

These accommodations and special measures are operational practices intended to lower barriers to election participation and are independent from the criteria used to determine candidate eligibility or membership status.

When formulating the schedule, factors such as the date of the General Meeting, the publication timing of the newsletter, peak periods for financial audits, and holidays or long vacations will be taken into account to ensure adequate notice periods.

The activation of special measures or extensions will be applied equally to all candidates and voters, and the decisions and their reasons will be promptly announced.

reasoning_content: Article 11 of the Association’s bylaws stipulates that a regular member loses membership if dues are in arrears for more than three years; however, this member’s arrears are limited to two years, and they are currently applying for a dues waiver due to disaster under Article 12. Therefore, their regular membership remains in effect. Regular members are granted the right to vote and to stand for election as directors under Article 17, and they do not fall under any grounds for special exclusion set out in Article 23. Accordingly, it can be determined that this member is eligible to be a candidate in the director election.
