negative_chunk_list.content: This document organizes the “components of compensation” and the approaches to evaluation and revision for new graduate hires at three companies: Vertex Sky Digital, Nimbus Digital, and Studio Velnetia. At all three companies, monthly pay consists of a base salary, various allowances tied to role and expertise (e.g., Creative Allowance, Business Promotion Allowance, Technology Allowance), and a “skill-based pay” aligned with job skill levels. New graduates are assigned a grade upon placement after joining, and salary increases and promotions are considered based on regular evaluations (about once a year). Grades are defined along three axes—“results,” “behavior,” and “skills.” For designers, evaluation criteria include the quality of outputs directly linked to user value, process reproducibility, leadership in collaborative work, and contributions to the operation and scalability of design systems. Overtime is handled according to each company’s work rules, using either a deemed fixed-overtime system included in the monthly salary or separate payment based on actual hours worked (in all cases, legal standards are followed). Year-end or semiannual performance bonuses are typically determined by a combined factor of company performance, departmental KPIs, and individual evaluations; payment immediately after joining depends on tenure requirements and the length of the evaluation period.

For all three companies, submitting a portfolio is mandatory for the selection process for new graduate designer positions. The submission format can be a URL or a PDF, and more than the number of works, the emphasis is on being able to discern the thinking process of “problem setting → research → hypothesis → iteration → validation.” The first round is a document screening (resume, academic transcript, statement of purpose, and portfolio). From the second round onward, candidates may be given design assignments, whiteboard sessions, usability assignment reviews, and coding tests (only if front-end skills are required). A hybrid of online and in-person formats is common, and from a communication perspective, the evaluation focuses on articulating intent, building consensus with non-designers, and decision-making processes under constraints. Interviewers include design managers, product managers, engineers, and in some cases business-side leaders, who assess cultural fit and repeatability from a cross-functional standpoint. Post-offer procedures (submission of onboarding documents, equipment selection, assignment preference interviews, scheduling of onboarding orientation) vary by company, and details such as offer validity periods, relocation support, and whether internship continuation is possible are communicated individually.

This section provides reference information that organizes the scope of duties and expected skill levels for “designers” across three companies. The areas of responsibility vary by company and business—such as UX/UI, product, communication, motion, and 3D/game art. Common expectations include: (1) defining and prioritizing user needs; (2) interaction design (information architecture, navigation, state transitions); (3) systematizing visual language (typography, color, components); (4) operating and documenting design systems; (5) meeting accessibility standards; and (6) implementation collaboration (handoff from design tools, ticket management, version control). Tools are selected from several specified options to align with team standards. During the new‑graduate phase, as the “Shu” in the “Shu-Ha-Ri” framework, you are expected to understand existing systems and brand guidelines, iterate frequent small improvements, and take ownership of A/B testing and support-driven enhancements. In terms of education, habits of iterative thinking, expression, and validation are fostered through design critiques, 1:1s, guild activities, reading groups, and lightning talks.

Each company provides a common orientation for new graduates (company overview, compliance, information security, labor) and role-specific bootcamps. For designers, these include training on the product development process (requirements gathering → requirements definition → design → development → release → measurement → improvement), user research methods (interviews, concept tests, usability tests, how to interpret quantitative analysis), accessibility fundamentals, Figma library operations, and hands-on design reviews. During the 1–3 month onboarding period, a mentor supports the new hire, with weekly goal-setting, learning plans, and retrospectives. Placement is determined by the business phase, and the ability to interpret value metrics is required—experience metrics for B2C, order intake and adoption for B2B, and retention and event KPIs for games/entertainment. Career paths run in parallel as “Individual Contributor (IC)” and “Management”: ICs are evaluated on depth of expertise and scope of impact, while managers are assessed on strategy, organizational operations, hiring, and talent development. Learning support includes subsidies for external conference participation, certification support, internal reading groups, English communication training, and in-house hackathons.

All three companies fundamentally adopt a “hybrid work” model tailored to the nature of the product or project, while in some cases setting team-level rules for in-office frequency and meeting structures. Policies vary by company regarding whether flextime has core hours, remote work regulations, requirements for secure work environments (screen privacy measures, VPN, device asset management), and the frequency of offsites and sprint reviews. Work locations span multiple hubs such as the Tokyo metropolitan area, Kansai, and Tokai, and assignments are determined holistically based on business needs, hiring track, and individual preferences. Relocation support (guidance on moving-related procedures, help with housing searches, partial subsidies for initial costs, etc.) is provided according to company policy. For roles that require on-site presence (e.g., studio shoots, operation of user testing facilities, hardware-integrated projects), an upper limit may be set on the proportion of remote work. Leave programs are expanding beyond annual paid leave to include summer, year-end/New Year, bereavement, sick leave, special leave related to personal illness and family caregiving, volunteer leave, and exam leave. Mental health support and work–life balance measures for pregnancy, childbirth, childcare, and caregiving are also in place, along with flexible work options such as reduced working hours, staggered shifts, and hybrid arrangements.

For new graduate designers, the standard equipment provided at onboarding (laptops, external monitors, pen tablets, etc.) is selected based on project requirements and individual creation styles. The operation of software licenses (design tools, prototyping, motion, 3D, font management) and asset purchases follows the team’s procurement policy, with application, approval, and inventory processes. In healthcare, we offer regular health checkups, stress assessments, consultations with an occupational physician, and an Employee Assistance Program (EAP). For learning and research support, we provide opportunities to attend external conferences, participate in internal and external study sessions, subsidies for purchasing technical and professional books, and language lesson assistance. Subsidies related to commuting and remote work (commuting transportation, home internet, and partial costs for home office equipment) are also handled in accordance with company regulations. Internal communities such as the Design Guild, Accessibility Working Group, Design System Operations Team, Motion Study Group, and Game Art Club are active, promoting cross-functional knowledge sharing. Recognition programs (Quarter Awards, Best Practices, Customer Impact Award) and peer bonuses that make colleagues’ appreciation visible also help foster our culture.

All three companies sometimes host design internships for undergraduates, master’s, and doctoral students, with variations such as hackathon formats, design sprints, or participation in long-term projects. Internships may be directly linked to hiring or purely educational, and evaluations can serve as reference materials for the main selection process. After an offer is made, a pre-employment “learning roadmap” is distributed, including foundational exercises (component design, accessibility fixes, participation in design reviews) and meet-and-greets with potential teams. Assignments are determined comprehensively based on business plans, staffing plans, personal preferences, and skill matches, and may be readjusted every few months after joining. In game-related projects, workloads fluctuate with release cycles and event operations; in B2B, there is more customer onboarding support and collaboration with PMM; in B2C, designing and running growth experiments becomes routine. Depending on the designer’s specialization, they may also take on cross-functional roles such as UX researcher, UI engineer, or product owner. Consequently, ongoing skill development and periodic reassessment of career paths are emphasized even after joining.

The three companies position the promotion of Diversity, Equity & Inclusion as a key priority and, in recruitment, implement measures such as prohibiting unfair discrimination based on age, gender, nationality, or disability, providing reasonable accommodations, and offering language support during interviews. Product accessibility is managed end-to-end from the design phase through quality assurance to meet requirements such as guideline-compliant contrast, focus indicators, keyboard operability, alternative text, and screen reader support. Regarding information security, strict measures are enforced, including device management for both office and remote environments, clear classification of confidentiality levels, approval workflows for external sharing, guidelines for the use of generative AI, and adherence to NDAs. Personal information related to recruitment is handled within the scope of its intended use, with defined policies for retention periods, access permissions, and disposal methods. For the use of generative AI, company policies stipulate prohibitions on entering confidential information, processes for reviewing and approving outputs, and ethical usage guidelines. These initiatives apply to all employees, not only designers, and are important factors both in the selection process and in carrying out work after joining the company.

Examples of assignments handled during selection processes and post-hire trials include: (A) analyzing drop-off points in existing flows and redesigning the UI, (B) prototyping new features and creating validation plans, (C) redefining components in line with a brand refresh, (D) improving UI/UX for in-game events, and (E) designing the role of motion (perceptual hierarchy, feedback, clarity of state transitions).

Evaluation criteria include the appropriateness of problem framing, clarity of information organization, breadth and depth of exploration, rationale for decision-making, integration of user, business, and technical constraints, consistency of outputs, resilience to critique and speed of improvement, and ability to collaborate with implementation.

Deliverables are evaluated not only on visual polish but also on process documentation (text, diagrams, measurement plans).

After joining, you will continually hone core product development skills such as formulating the right questions in design reviews, interpreting data from logs/heatmaps/surveys, designing and interpreting A/B test hypotheses, and prioritizing bugs and quality issues.

As for assignments, they often align closely with actual work, based on each company’s selection guidelines and onboarding materials. Therefore, assignments in selection and training are important steps for understanding a designer’s role in real-world practice and acquiring the necessary skills.

reasoning_content: The starting salaries for designer positions at each company are calculated based on the published salary information by job category. Nimbus Digital explicitly states “Designer: 340,000 yen,” which corresponds to the monthly salary. At Vertex Sky Digital, it says “Designer position Monthly base salary: 260,000 yen (including a 40,000 yen creative allowance) Monthly skill allowance: 115,000 yen,” so the monthly salary can be determined by adding the base and skill allowances: 260,000 yen + 115,000 yen = 375,000 yen. Studio Velnetia specifies “Designer Starting Salary (Monthly): 320,000 yen,” which was used as is. As above, based on the clearly stated monthly salaries for each company, the calculations are 375,000 yen for Vertex Sky Digital, 340,000 yen for Nimbus Digital, and 320,000 yen for Studio Velnetia.

question: What is the minimum Unified English Proficiency Test score required to participate in the new employee training program at Tentou Trading Co., Ltd., and are there any alternatives if the score is not met?
answer: 650 points. If the score is below this, you can participate by taking the in-house English training program and achieving a score equivalent to 650 points in the in-house training within six months.

positive_chunk_list.content:
Participation Requirements for Tentou Trading Co., Ltd. New Employee Training Program (FY2024 Revised Edition): This training program targets new employees who may engage in international operations or belong to departments likely to be assigned to overseas offices in the future, and it sets certain language proficiency standards as eligibility criteria. Among these, obtaining a score of 650 or higher on the Unified English Proficiency Test is a mandatory requirement as an indicator of English ability. This represents the minimum level of English reading, listening, and speaking skills required for work, and a higher score is considered desirable for practical business English use.

However, even if the score falls short of 650, participants are not automatically deemed ineligible, as certain alternative measures are in place. This is the “In-House English Training Program.” Designed for new employees with a Unified English Proficiency Test score below 600 who wish to strengthen their language skills in the future, this program aims to help participants achieve an equivalent score of 650 through an intensive course (48 sessions in total) over a maximum of six months. If, after completing this in-house training, a participant achieves a score of 650 or higher on the Unified English Proficiency Test according to internal evaluation standards within the prescribed period (in principle, within six months), they will be permitted to join the main training program. In this respect, it is treated the same as submitting a standard external test score, and either an in-house mock test result or an official score may be submitted as proof of performance.

At present, languages other than English are not included in the participation requirements for this program. However, departments such as Technology Development, where overseas business trips are frequent, have established their own training systems in French and Chinese, and the possibility of multi-language requirements is being discussed for the future.

[Important] Tentō Trading Co., Ltd. In-House English Training Program (FY2024): This program is part of a company-wide language enhancement initiative for new employees whose Unified English Proficiency Test score is below 550. To partcipate in the new employee training program, otaining a score of 550 or higher on the Unified English Proficiency Test is necessary. For those who do not meet the participation requirements for the main new employee training, it provides intensive instruction for up to six months to strengthen basic English skills. The program is held every Tuesday and Thursday from 18:00 to 20:00, consisting of 48 sessions (120 minutes each, with Zoom used in combination), and the company covers the full tuition cost.

Instructors consist of native speakers and Japanese teachers from external language education institutions (e.g., ABC English Japan), covering a wide range of skills from basic grammar to practical conversation, business email writing, and presentation practice. Maintaining eligibility requires achieving an attendance rate of 80% or higher. Two midterm mock exams and one final mock exam are administered during the program, and if the final internal evaluation score reaches the equivalent of 550, participants are allowed to transfer into the main new employee training. This evaluation differs from the official Unified English Proficiency Test score and is based on an internally designed in-house English assessment (CEAT: Company English Assessment Test). Conversion based on the CEAT score, which is administered only twice a year, is also permitted. Additionally, monthly individual follow-up meetings (learning progress interviews) are conducted with the HR language representative.

Please note that while this training is primarily available online, participants may be required to attend at designated venues depending on their device environment or network conditions.

negative_chunk_list.content:
[Tentou Shoji Co., Ltd. – 2030 Talent Development Roadmap (Public Summary)]
This roadmap serves as a systematic guideline for the talent development and organizational development goals Tentou Shoji aims to achieve by 2030, and has been formulated as a framework to support all employees in autonomous career development. Chapter 1, based on the company’s vision of becoming a “Global Value Integrator,” outlines five key strategies to maximize individual value-creation capabilities in times of transformation: (1) strengthening leadership, (2) elevating digital skills, (3) promoting diversity, (4) wellbeing-oriented management, and (5) coexistence with local communities. Chapter 2 organizes the tiered training system offered to new graduates, mid-career hires, and returnees, explaining how the year-of-joining roadmaps, role-specific professional training, and position-specific leadership training function in a mutually complementary manner. Chapter 3 presents an overview of the “International Job Challenge Program,” which promotes cross-border talent exchanges with overseas group companies, noting that interested employees can gain up to two years of practical experience at sites in Europe, North America, and ASEAN. Chapter 4 details the use of the internal LXP (Learning Experience Platform) overseen by the DX Promotion Office, recommending the formation of an active learning community by combining AI-recommended personalized learning, visualization of OJT records, and a badge certification system. Chapter 5 sets out plans for API integration with external MOOCs and the introduction of microlearning-style AR content, proposing a hybrid design that comprehensively meets the learning needs of multiple generations and locations.

[Case Study: Overseas Customer Engagement Simulation Training (for Mid-level and Junior Sales Roles)]
Tentō Trading has introduced a role-play simulation training program to strengthen negotiation capabilities with overseas customers. In this program, participants form a virtual project team and work under a scenario in which they propose solutions to a European energy company. On the first day, an interactive lecture by a negotiation expert is conducted to help participants understand differences in decision-making processes arising from cultural backgrounds. From the following day, an AI chatbot takes on the role of the buyer, allowing participants to experience a simulated negotiation flow. Through multiple rounds of exercises—including proposal development, price negotiations, and contract term adjustments—participants refine the persuasiveness of their presentations and their problem-solving skills. Evaluation combines peer reviews and mentor assessments, quantified along three axes: communication strategy, logical structure, and risk management. Additionally, chat logs from the exercises are analyzed using NLP to visualize linguistic diversity and tone appropriateness. On the final day, an external guest (a procurement manager from a foreign-affiliated manufacturer) is invited for a live mock negotiation, followed by shared feedback. According to a post-training survey, 83% of participants reported “significant, directly applicable learning,” and there were cases where the closing rate of real negotiations improved by an average of 12% in the following quarter. Going forward, there are plans to leverage VR meeting environments to evolve into high-fidelity simulations that recreate time differences and communication conditions across multiple locations.

[TenTo Commerce: Health Management Handbook (Excerpt from 2nd Edition)]
Formulated as part of the company’s sustainability strategy, this handbook treats employees’ physical and mental health as management resources and provides guidelines for achieving long-term value creation. Chapter 1 explains a hybrid health support system in which industrial physicians, public health nurses, and external mental coaches work in tandem, and introduces the distribution of wearable devices that promote self-monitoring of sleep, diet, and exercise in addition to regular health checkups. Chapter 2 details the operation of the Employee Assistance Program (EAP) aimed at early detection of stressors and timely intervention. Specifically, it establishes a three-tier support structure comprising an anonymous consultation desk, a 24/7 chatbot consultation service, and in-person counseling, and introduces the granting of additional paid leave as an incentive to increase utilization. Chapter 3 addresses “digital overwork” associated with the spread of remote work by setting prohibited hours for sending emails and chats and recommending a weekly “No Meeting Half Day.” Chapter 4 introduces the “Wellbeing Challenge” conducted across domestic and overseas sites and explains how gamification elements—such as inter-departmental competitions in step counts and meditation time—enhance engagement. Chapter 5 proposes eliminating core hours in the flextime system and introducing shared leadership to support balancing caregiving and childcare. It also presents a framework for reflecting in IR materials the measurement of ROI on health investments by calculating reductions in medical expenses and decreases in presenteeism.

[Global Finance Foundation Training Program (for Accounting and Finance Departments)]
This program aims to cultivate finance professionals who can comprehensively understand Japanese accounting standards amid ongoing international convergence. The curriculum comprises 12 modules, covering topics such as comparative analysis of revenue recognition standards, lease accounting, hedge accounting, and tax effect accounting in corporate reorganizations. At the end of each module, a case method is used to analyze financial statements based on disclosures from real companies, and at the end of the term, participants must complete an integrated report exercise based on a hypothetical cross-border M&A case. On the online LMS, lectures are available with multilingual subtitles, an AI auto-summarization feature, and interactive quizzes; adaptive learning adjusts difficulty dynamically based on learner proficiency. Completion certification requires a 100% assignment submission rate, an overall grade of B or higher, and an average peer review score of 75 or above. Those who pass are awarded CPD (Continuing Professional Development) points. In addition, top performers earn eligibility to apply for a short-term study program hosted by an external finance school. Survey feedback from participants frequently notes that the program helped them systematically organize differences among international standards, and the administrative office is considering adding modules to reflect changes in ESG disclosure regulations. Next year, a new “Sustainability Accounting” module aligned with the EU CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) will be introduced to develop skills for integrated reporting of financial and non-financial information.

[In-house Hackathon “TENxLAB” Implementation Report (Spring 2025)]
At Tentou Trading, we hold a large-scale hackathon every six months to foster an innovation culture. This time’s theme was “Supply Chain × AI Ethics,” and all 52 registered teams developed prototypes within 72 hours. Participants were not limited to the development division; sales, logistics, legal, and PR also took part, promoting cross-functional team formation as a distinctive feature. On day one, we ran a problem-definition workshop, generating numerous ideas such as visualizing logistics traceability and simulating greenhouse gas emissions. On day two, teams leveraged a cloud-based development environment and the internal API catalog to rapidly build PoCs (Proofs of Concept). On demo day (day three), we invited VC partners and academic researchers as external judges to score technical originality, business viability, and social impact. The grand prize went to “EcoStock Navigator,” which automatically optimizes inventory based on real-time demand forecasting; a pilot is planned with the SCM division. To support mental well-being and focus during the hackathon, we offered mindfulness sessions and nutritionist-supervised snacks. In the post-event survey, over 90% of respondents said it helped them build networks beyond their departments, making it a valuable opportunity to foster cross-organizational collaboration.
[Additional Details] A total of 276 people participated in this hackathon (average age 32.4), 23% of whom were young employees within their first two years at the company.

[Compliance Forum 2025 Keynote Digest]
At the forum hosted by Tentō Trading’s Legal Department, the latest risk management case studies were shared based on domestic and international regulatory trends. The keynote provided an overview of how the EU AI Act and the U.S. AI Accountability Policy will affect corporate activities, with particular emphasis on “algorithmic transparency requirements” across supply chains. In the panel discussion, crossover issues among data privacy, anti-bribery, and sustainability reporting were explored, and a past case involving an accounting fraud scandal at a multinational manufacturer was presented. Participants received case study materials including a whistleblowing response flow, a third-party investigation process, and guidelines for assembling an emergency response team. In the capstone exercise, attendees were tasked with developing recurrence-prevention measures for a hypothetical bribery allegation scenario within a set time limit.
[Additional Details] This year’s forum saw a record 480 participants (340 internal / 140 external), with 94% of survey respondents indicating they “wish to attend next year.” The workshop presented an integrated operational model combining ISO 37001 (Anti-Bribery Management System) and the COSO framework, and each table developed KPIs/KRIs (Key Risk Indicators) during a 90-minute group exercise. In the regulatory session, the impact of the proposed amendments to the Act on Improving Transparency and Fairness of Digital Platform Transactions was explained, and a comparative table summarized the disclosure obligations borne by platform operators and B2B trading companies.

[Regional Co-Creation Project “LINK to LOCAL” Activity Report]
Tento Trading promotes “LINK to LOCAL,” a program that supports solving regional issues in collaboration with municipalities where it operates. In fiscal 2024, the company implemented diverse initiatives, including building a circular tourism model in mountainous areas suffering from depopulation, launching a blue carbon generation project in coastal regions, and conducting a digital currency pilot to revitalize shopping districts. This report provides detailed accounts of stakeholder analysis, partnership contract structures, financing schemes, and the process for setting performance indicators. Notably, the “Regional Resource Creative Talent Development Program,” which utilized a public-private partnership fund, drew attention as a case study carried out with a local university, offering business planning, prototyping support, and crowdfunding courses for young entrepreneurs. In addition, SROI (Social Return on Investment) was adopted to evaluate the project’s overall impact, and it is estimated that 2.6 times the investment amount in social value was created within the first year of the initiative.

[Additional Details] The total project cost for fiscal 2024 was 580 million yen, of which 42% came from municipal subsidies, 35% from joint private investment, and the remainder from crowdfunding and CSR budgets. In the tourism model project, electronic tickets equipped with IoT sensors were introduced to aggregate visitor circulation data in real time. As a result, average length of stay increased by 2.3 times and average spend per visitor rose by 1.8 times. In the blue carbon project, CO2 absorption was assessed at 1.9 tons per 1 ton of seaweed biomass, generating 9,200 tons’ worth of carbon credits.

Design Philosophy of the Next-Generation Leader Selection Program “EDGE+”
“EDGE+” is a selective 18-month leadership development course targeting high performers in their early 30s. In the onboarding phase, multi-faceted assessments (360° feedback, cognitive style diagnostics, and competency interviews) are used to visualize each participant’s strengths and challenges. In the subsequent growth phase, a combination of case-study lectures led by overseas MBA holders, macroeconomic scenario planning exercises, and AI vision design workshops cultivates strategic planning skills. Toward the end, participants are assigned to new business initiatives under the direct supervision of the CEO, gaining hands-on experience in business development with real P/L responsibility. Program completion is evaluated using a broad set of indicators, including achievement of proof-of-concept KPIs, improvement in team engagement, and results from external pitch competitions. Notably, the program features a proprietary metric developed to measure the “sustainability” of leadership—the Sustainable Leadership Quotient (SLQ)—which quantifies capabilities such as fostering psychological safety and generating social impact.

Additional Details: The first cohort comprised 42 participants, with the following functional breakdown: Sales 31%, Engineering 29%, Corporate 24%, Overseas Offices 16%. The average initial SLQ was 58.7/100, rising to 74.5 upon completion. During the program, three “Leadership in Crisis” VR simulations were conducted, providing simulated decision-making in scenarios of disaster response, cyberattacks, and rapid foreign exchange fluctuations. A parallel empirical study visualized stress responses using psychophysiological sensors and analyzed the correlation between heart rate variability and decision-making accuracy.

[AI and Data Literacy Core Course (Company-Wide Requirement) Operations Guidelines]
Tento Trading, which is driving digital transformation, has adopted a policy of requiring a certain level of data literacy from all employees regardless of department or role. The course consists of three tiers—Beginner, Intermediate, and Advanced. The Beginner level covers the basics of data acquisition, cleaning, and visualization; the Intermediate level covers the fundamentals of statistical inference and machine learning models; and the Advanced level covers MLOps, governance, and AI ethics. The course is delivered in an e-learning format and, in addition to comprehension tests, includes a practical exam requiring the submission of data processing scripts and dashboards. Evaluation criteria consist of three items—code quality, reproducibility, and accountability—each graded on four levels: S, A, B, and C. Those who complete the Advanced course become eligible to join the internal data science community “D-Force.”

[Additional Details] In FY2025, 5,280 participants registered, with completion rates of 96.2% for Beginner, 82.4% for Intermediate, and 61.7% for Advanced. The LMS displays learners’ study time as a heatmap, with peak activity on Tuesdays and Thursdays between 22:00 and 24:00. In the Advanced course’s capstone project, participants leverage internal datasets (e.g., logistics lead times, energy usage, and sales activity logs), and measure not only model accuracy but also an “Explainability Score.” Of the 35 teams, 11 implemented XAI dashboards and received an average judge rating of 4.5/5 or higher. For six months after course completion, AI mentors run a “Follow-up Sprint,” providing business-problem-based feedback and tracking real-world application rates.

[Explanation of the Company Housing and Housing Allowance Policy (FY2025 Revision Draft)]
As a comprehensive measure to enhance employee engagement, the HR Department has announced a major overhaul of the housing support program. The draft revision adopts a sliding mechanism that indexes local rent markets and automatically adjusts allowance caps according to a regional coefficient. In light of the normalization of telework, a “Remote Work Special Rent Subsidy” will be introduced solely for cases where securing a dedicated home workspace is difficult. In addition, company housing for overseas assignees will be equipped with IoT devices as standard to visualize energy usage in real time and support the achievement of SDGs targets. The use of a blockchain ledger to settle repair costs at move-out is also under consideration to ensure transparency.

[Additional Details]
The draft was posted on the company intranet, and over 1,200 pieces of feedback were submitted during the comment period. HR classified and analyzed the feedback using natural language processing and organized it into the top seven categories (rent caps, family accompaniment conditions, duplicate rent support during transfers, furniture rental, utility subsidies, contract procedure burden, and overseas relocation support). A visualization dashboard was published to all employees to make the feedback loop process transparent. According to simulations of the new system, annual costs will increase by 340 million yen, but synergistic effects are expected, including a 0.7-point drop in turnover, a 190 million yen reduction in hiring costs, and a 2,800-ton reduction in CO2 emissions. Under the regional relocation trial program, 150 employees moved to satellite offices in regional areas over three years, reducing commuting-related CO2 by 41%.

reasoning_content: The eligibility requirements for the new employee training program explicitly state that a score of 650 or higher on the Unified English Proficiency Test is mandatory. However, it is also stipulated that those who do not meet the 650 score can complete the in-house English training program within six months and, by obtaining a final evaluation score equivalent to 650 in that program, be permitted to participate in the training. Therefore, the minimum requirement is a score of 650 on the Unified English Proficiency Test, and for those who fall short, an alternative path is provided via the in-house English training.

question: At Concatio, is it acceptable to use my own smartwatch to enter the local office during next month’s business trip abroad?
answer: No, entry using personal devices such as smartwatches is not permitted. You must use the company-issued ID card.

positive_chunk_list.content:
[Notice] All Concatio employees must use a company-issued dedicated ID card when entering and exiting the office. This requirement is based on the company-wide physical security policy and is intended for managing access to office areas, accurately logging entry records, and verifying evacuees in emergencies. The ID cards contain embedded IC chips with a unique ID assigned to each employee, and, through integration with the management system, clock-in and clock-out times are automatically recorded.

Therefore, entry using any unofficial devices that are not issued or approved by the company—including personal belongings—is strictly prohibited. Even if a device is technically compatible with the access system, it is not officially registered or validated by the company, and its use is, in principle, not allowed; if discovered, it will be reported to the Internal Audit Department. In the past, there have been instances where employees attempted to use personally owned NFC devices for entry; however, the system did not record logs, and the incidents were treated as security events.

This rule applies without exception during business trips and when using external offices. If you lose or forget your card, you can complete procedures at the local reception to obtain a temporary card; however, you may be required to present identification and obtain approval from your supervisor, so be sure to confirm you are carrying your card before traveling.

Note that ID cards are issued to individuals and must not be shared with or lent to third parties. They must be promptly returned upon resignation or reassignment. For details, see Chapter 3 of the Internal Physical Security Operations Guide.

[Concatio Inc. Additional Rule] While on overseas business trips, employees must use only company-issued communication and electronic devices for work purposes. This is based on the global IT security policy that comprehensively considers information leakage risks, cyberattacks, and local communication environments, and it is a common rule that applies to all employees during international travel. For example, work-related activities such as sending and receiving business emails, schedule management, and VPN connections are permitted only on company-issued laptops and mobile devices (e.g., smartphones under MAM management, tablets with embedded SIMs). The use of personally owned electronic devices, such as smartphones, is strictly prohibited for business purposes, even if work-related applications are installed. In particular, risks such as unauthorized communications via local public Wi-Fi, unsanctioned synchronization, and collection of location information have been identified, requiring strict management. This policy is based on the “Guidelines for the Use of Information Devices in Overseas Operations” (2023 revised edition), and violations may be subject to disciplinary action. For details, refer to the Q&A section of the “Business Trip Guide” on the intranet.

negative_chunk_list.content:
Objective: To improve internal productivity and strengthen collaboration by gradually digitizing processes that have traditionally relied on paper and manual work, and proactively introducing the latest software and devices.

Scope: All departments (Development, Sales, Administration, Manufacturing, Research) and domestic and overseas sites including group companies.

1. Digital tools to be used:
  1) Cloud-based project management platforms (e.g., Asana, Jira, Monday.com)
  2) AI-assisted document creation tools (e.g., Notion AI, Microsoft Copilot, Google Duet AI)
  3) RPA for business process automation (e.g., UiPath, Power Automate, Zapier)
  4) Expense management mobile apps, e-signature services, online storage, etc.

2. Implementation flow:
  (a) Department representatives submit requests for tools to be introduced via the “IT Asset Management System”
  (b) The IT Strategy Office reviews security, compatibility, and licensing costs (average 3 business days)
  (c) The Information Security Committee (meets twice monthly) grants final approval
  (d) Approval results are posted on the internal portal bulletin board, along with training videos and FAQs

3. Trial environment: Even before approval, a “sandbox environment” can be provided for PoC purposes only. VPN connection required; confidential data must not be used.

4. Training: Mandatory 40-minute e-learning for all employees, plus advanced workshops (twice a month, offered simultaneously in-person and online). Completion is linked with the HR system and automatically reflected in the evaluation item “IT Literacy.”

Overview: Success in overseas business depends on a deep understanding of local culture and flexible communication. This checklist covers the entire process from pre-departure to return, supporting the enhancement of soft skills such as language proficiency and network building.
I. Preliminary Research:
  1. Basic understanding of history, religion, and social customs (shortened business hours during religious events, practices around paid leave, holiday calendars)
  2. Business customs (order of exchanging business cards, honorifics, taboo small-talk topics before negotiations)
  3. Tipping culture, negotiation practices, sense of time (punctual vs. relaxed)
II. Communication:
  1. Memorize multilingual greeting phrases (at least three phrases in the local language plus English)
  2. Nonverbal messages (eye contact, gestures, personal space, interpretation of silence)
  3. Gift selection guide (materials prohibited for religious reasons, wrapping colors, timing of presentation)
III. Devices & IT:
  1. Comparison table: roaming contracts vs. eSIM vs. local SIM
  2. Offline translation apps and map cache settings
  3. Portable battery capacity restrictions (airline regulations: under 100 Wh)
IV. Attire & Hygiene:
  1. Dress code quick reference (formal/business casual/casual)
  2. List of restaurant search sites for halal, vegetarian, and food allergy accommodation
  3. Restrictions on bringing in medications (e.g., certain prescription drugs are regulated)

Objective: To maintain the physical and mental health of all employees, reduce turnover, and foster innovation by overhauling systems that balance flexible work styles with fair performance evaluations. This revision includes a review of target telework ratios, a pilot introduction of an optional three-day weekend, and an expansion of benefits points.

A. Telework Guidelines
1. Recommend at least two days of remote work per week in principle. Exceptions apply when on-site presence is required due to project needs or safety management.
2. Mandatory conditions: Always-on VPN, use of company-issued PC, two-factor authentication, and setting “At Work” status on the internal messenger.
3. Home office support: The company will cover up to 50,000 yen for monitor, chair, and desk purchases.

B. Flextime System
- Core hours 10:00–15:00; minimum 4 working hours per day. Required working hours must be met on a three-month average.
- Late-night overtime reduction KPI: Target a 50% year-over-year reduction in clock-ins after 22:00.

C. Three-Day Weekend Trial
- Participants shift to a 32-hour workweek with 95% of salary maintained. Performance is measured by output per labor hour as an operational efficiency metric.

D. Automatic Paid Leave Acquisition System
- When remaining paid leave falls below 15 days, mentors are notified. A maximum of 40 days can be carried over; any excess is automatically forfeited.

E. Mental Health Support
- An online counseling desk staffed by licensed clinical psychologists, free up to three times per month. Mandatory stress checks twice a year.

F. Evaluation and Compensation
- Semiannual evaluations based on three axes: output, process, and team contribution.

Project period: September 2024–June 2025. Total construction cost: 180 million yen. Seating capacity: 150% of existing (300 → 450 seats). Concept: “Health, Diversity, Interaction.” ———
1. Menu development
  - Supervised by a registered dietitian; salt, sugar, and allergen information instantly displayed on electronic menus.
  - Weekly rotating vegan, halal, and gluten-free options added.
  - Local production for local consumption: Partnering with 11 contracted farms to promote sustainable sourcing of seasonal ingredients.
2. Cashless integration
  - Implementing facial recognition payment linked to employee IDs (average payment time reduced from 7 seconds to 2 seconds).
  - Points rewards: Up to a 10% discount linked to a health score.
3. Spatial design
  - Wood-based interior materials sequestering 3.2 t CO2/year. Focus seats separated with soundproof partitions.
  - IoT seat management: Real-time crowding information to disperse peak times.
4. ESG metrics
  - Measuring food waste with sensing scales and transferring surplus to an internal food bank. FY2025 waste rate target: 3% or less.
5. Survey results
  - Satisfaction: 4.7/5 at pre-opening (n=812). The three most highly rated aspects were “taste,” “waiting time,” and “interaction space.”
———
For the three months following the renewal, a trial is underway to dynamically optimize menu design from a corporate health management perspective by tracking crowding levels and changes in menu preferences through surveys and IoT sensing, and feeding results back by department.

Background: With rapid staff expansion and the spread of hybrid work, the current building’s capacity shortfall has become evident. This plan is centered on “reducing environmental impact,” “enhancing employee engagement,” and “near-future collaboration,” establishing a new hub in a redevelopment area within a 30-minute public transit radius of central Tokyo.
A. Reasons for site selection
- Direct connection to a terminal station served by multiple lines, fully equipped with disaster-response functions (emergency power generation and space for accommodating stranded commuters).
- An open innovation zone concentrating government agencies, universities, and startups.
B. Floor configuration (total 27,000 m²)
- 1F: Lounge and event hall (capacity 500, movable partitions)
- 2–5F: Customer co-creation studios, presentation booths
- 6–15F: Work areas (free-address/specialized booths ratio 7:3)
- 16F: Library and meditation space
- 17F: Rooftop garden and café, urban farm
C. Sustainability
- BELS★★★★★ and ZEB Ready certifications; high-efficiency double-skin facade on the exterior.
- 1.2 MW solar power generation, rainwater recycling, and AI-controlled HVAC achieving −45% annual primary energy consumption.
D. Smart building technologies
- Facial recognition gates + Bluetooth LE beacons for seat occupancy visualization
- Robotic delivery, elevator destination prediction control, and congestion heat map distribution.
E. Relocation schedule
- 2025 Q4: Design completion → 2026 Q2: Interior fit-out begins → 2026 Q4: Phased relocation starts → 2027 Q1: Return of old site.

Event Date: Saturday, October 4, 2025 — Check-in from 08:30, Opening Ceremony at 09:00. Venue: Civic General Gymnasium and the adjacent multipurpose ground. Slogan: “Connect! Run! Smile!”

List of Events:
  1. Inter-Department Relay (total distance 800m × 4)
  2. e-Sports League (racing simulator / VR archery)
  3. Tug-of-war + AR augmented reality obstacle-avoidance mission
  4. Wheelchair basketball exhibition match (DEI promotion initiative)
  5. Parent–Child Challenge! Drone delivery time attack

Points System: Department points are awarded based on placement; the winning department will receive a “1,000,000 yen Health Promotion Fund.”

Registration: September 1–15 via the internal portal “SportsDay2025” form. Capacity 2,500 including family and alumni, first-come, first-served.

Safety Measures: Ten cooling mist tents for heatstroke prevention, 12 AED units, and an on-site medical team. In the event of a Japan Meteorological Agency warning, postponement decisions will be announced three days in advance.

Environmental Considerations: Reusable cups will be used; tree-planting donations will be made to offset CO2 emissions.

SDGs Education Booths: Fair trade coffee tasting, food drive, and a local specialty market.

Media coverage is scheduled for the day. The dress code is athletic attire; wearing company-branded apparel is encouraged. To showcase our sustainability efforts, official T-shirts made from recycled fibers will be sold, with a portion of proceeds donated to the Regional Sports Promotion Fund.

1. Alert level determination
- Collect special warnings/warnings/advisories issued by the Japan Meteorological Agency in real time and automatically integrate them into the internal BCP dashboard.
- When a warning is issued and planned service suspensions by transportation operators are expected, transition to “Phase 3 (Work-from-home recommended).”

2. Actions by phase
- Phase 1 (Normal operations): Office attendance allowed; flexible handling of public transportation delays.
- Phase 2 (Caution): Work from home recommended; meetings after 5 p.m. should be rescheduled. However, on-site operations and data center staff remain on a duty roster.
- Phase 3 (Work-from-home mandatory): Going out is prohibited in principle; continue operations via VPN. Emergency response desk operates 24/7.

3. Office overnight stay option
- Keep 50 cots for those unable to return home, three days’ worth of emergency rations, and 2 tons of stored water on hand.
- Shower rooms provide hot water; amenities are distributed.

4. Communication
- Mandatory status report within 2 hours via the safety confirmation system “SafetyPing.”
- Centralize sharing of disaster and transportation information on the internal SNS channel #typhoon.

5. Cybersecurity
- When using home Wi‑Fi, confirm the router firmware is up to date and EDR is active.
- Prohibit moving files to external cloud storage.

6. Backup power
- Site UPS runtime: 80 minutes; emergency generator expected runtime: 18 hours. Critical systems will fail over to a remote region.
———
Additionally, prepare a measure to temporarily open partner shared offices free of charge for employees who cannot secure a work-from-home environment.

Target: A total of 6,200 employees, including full-time employees, contract employees, temporary staff, interns, and part-time workers. Implementation period: September 1–October 31, 2025.
A. Health checkup menu
- Basic: Height, weight, BMI, blood pressure, vision, hearing, chest X-ray, 22-item blood test, urinalysis.
- Additional for ages 35 and up: Electrocardiogram, upper GI barium exam, abdominal ultrasound.
- Women: Choice of breast ultrasound or mammography; cervical cancer screening.
- High-risk occupations: Special examinations for workers handling organic solvents, dust, or ionizing radiation.
B. Reservation method
- Select date and time via the “ConcaCare” smartphone app; check in with a QR code. Changes allowed until 6:00 p.m. the day before.
C. Results notification
- Available on paper and in the app; those requiring reexamination will see a button to book an online consultation.
D. Employment measures
- If classified as “treatment required,” an occupational physician interview is mandatory; job reassignment and work-hour adjustments may be implemented.
E. Parallel wellness initiatives
1. Fitness Challenge: Integrate life logs to rank steps, sleep, and heart rate; top 100 receive a 30,000 yen health allowance.
2. Cafeteria Healthy Miles: Earn points by choosing menu items with a nutrition score of 80 or higher; accumulate 1,000 points in a month to receive a half-day off.
3. Mindfulness Week: Compete in a company hackfest on time spent using a focus-improvement app.
F. Personal information protection
- Medical data, treated as sensitive personal information, will be stored in encrypted form; access logs retained for five years. No third-party provision allowed.

Objective: Minimize the risk of information leakage arising from increasingly sophisticated cyberattacks, diversified work styles, and the expansion of third-party outsourcing. ———
1. Physical media management
  - Halt new issuance of USB drives. Limit existing USB use to hardware-encrypted devices compliant with FIPS 140-2 and equipped with remote wipe.
  - Increase floors with secure confidential document collection boxes from 3 to 10; implement digital locks and RFID tags to capture collection logs.
2. Electronic data management
  - Integrate DLP (Data Loss Prevention) into the in-house cloud “ConcaDrive”; require watermarking and automatic expiration when externally sharing confidential files.
  - Risk scoring before sending emails: pop-up warnings for missing attachment encryption, predicted misaddressed recipients, and detection of sensitive terms.
3. Access control
  - Implement a Zero Trust architecture and enable SSO via IDaaS for 190 SaaS applications.
  - Use AI behavioral analytics to immediately block large-volume downloads that deviate from normal patterns.
4. Education & training
  - Conduct simulated phishing exercises four times a year, with a target click rate below 2%.
  - Host hands-on and CTF events during Information Security Month (November).
5. Incident response
  - Initial response SLAs: isolate affected systems within 15 minutes; report to management within 60 minutes; identify impact scope within 24 hours.
  - Expand cyber insurance coverage to include legal fees and PR costs.
6. Governance
  - Renew ISO/IEC 27001 certification and conduct semiannual self-assessments based on NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5.

Goal: Expand language and intercultural training programs centered on English to strengthen negotiation capabilities with overseas clients and facilitate collaboration in multicultural teams.
1. Course structure
- Five tiers: Beginner / Pre-Intermediate / Intermediate / Upper-Intermediate / Business Advanced, with a Speaking Lab, Writing Clinic, and Presentation Workshop at each level.
- Hybrid model with native instructors plus an AI conversation app. The AI analyzes proficiency and automatically proposes the following week’s learning path.
2. Schedule
- Once a week for 90 minutes / one term is 12 weeks; up to three terms per fiscal year.
- Early morning 7:30 slot, lunchtime 12:10 slot, and after-work 18:30 slot to accommodate flexible schedules.
3. Assessment
- TOEIC-equivalent scores measured via an online mock test; a certificate of completion is awarded if 70% of the target is achieved by the end of the term.
- The top 10% are eligible to apply for overseas training subsidies (up to 300,000 JPY).
4. Intercultural understanding module
- Experiential learning of Hofstede’s six-dimension model and Hall’s high-/low-context culture theory.
- Negotiation role-play conducted as case studies with parameters for price × delivery time × quality × cultural requirements.
5. Resources
- Class archives available 24/7 on the internal video platform, with review audio distributed in podcast format.
- Pair practice matching via a Slack learning channel, plus weekend online café chat sessions.
