question: How much is the total amount of funds (secured amount) procured in fiscal year 2024 for the establishment of the Faculty of Engineering at Hakufu General University?
answer: 700 million yen

positive_chunk_list.content:
Notice from the Accounting Division, Engineering at Hakufu General University

In addition to our existing fields of education and research, our university is moving forward with the establishment of a new Faculty of Engineering in the 2024 academic year, with the aim of cultivating highly advanced and practice-oriented talent in science and technology. This plan represents the first large-scale new faculty project since our founding and marks a major milestone in the future structure of our education and research.

In establishing this faculty, the university has decided on an initial investment totaling 500 million yen, drawing on national higher education promotion subsidies as well as support from multiple private educational foundations and industry associations in the technology sector. This budget includes the cost of introducing state-of-the-art research equipment, personnel expenses for specialized engineering faculty, fees for curriculum design and oversight, development of laboratory buildings and ICT infrastructure, and the creation of a foundation for collaborative research with the local community. These funds are positioned not only as an investment in the university’s overall development, but also as a contribution to revitalizing the regional economy and fostering future industries.

The new Faculty of Engineering will uphold the founding principle of “cultivating engineers who contribute to solving social issues,” and will offer programs closely tied to today’s industrial structure, including environment and energy, AI and robotics, materials science, and information and communications. To strengthen industry–academia collaboration, we are also planning joint research projects with local companies and international research institutions, expansion of internship programs, and creative, hands-on courses that allow students to experience technological innovation.

Within the university, curriculum development with an eye toward interdisciplinary integration is underway in conjunction with the establishment of the faculty. By collaborating with the existing Faculties of Science and of Information Studies, we aim to build an educational system that fosters cross-disciplinary problem-solving skills.

Going forward, we will proceed in stages with document submissions for authorization to establish the faculty, further detailing of construction plans, and open recruitment of faculty members. Updates on progress and financial reports related to this matter will be posted as needed on the university’s official website and on-campus bulletin boards. We ask for the continued understanding and support of our faculty and staff, students, parents, and local community stakeholders as we pursue this initiative.

Today, following a Cabinet decision, the government has officially approved the allocation of an additional 200 million yen in the 2024 fiscal year to the Faculty of Engineering at Hakufu General University. This measure is intended to strengthen the faculty’s research and educational framework. From a medium- to long-term perspective that includes promoting collaboration with the local community, regional revitalization, and the development of next-generation technical talent, this represents an important policy investment that will contribute to the advancement of engineering education at the university.

The budget will be provided under the framework of the “Regional Co-Creation Advanced Engineering Education Promotion Grant,” newly established by the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology starting in FY2024. The grant aims to support advanced engineering education and applied research at regional universities, emphasizing interdisciplinary initiatives, joint projects with businesses and local governments, and the return of research outcomes to local communities.

At Engineering at Hakufu General University, a project to establish the Faculty of Engineering began in FY2024 with an initial investment of 500 million yen, and the newly approved funds will constitute the second phase of that initiative. Priority investment areas are expected to include AI, robotics, environmental and energy technologies, and the development of digital manufacturing infrastructure. The scope will also include the creation of incubation facilities to deepen external collaboration, as well as programs that promote entrepreneurship support and technology transfer.

In addition, the project encourages efforts to return educational and research outcomes to the community from the perspective of sustainable regional growth and industry creation. A variety of activities are planned, including partnerships with local governments and small and medium-sized enterprises, as well as educational collaboration with regional high schools. Through these initiatives, Engineering at Hakufu General University is expected to further strengthen its role as a regional hub for technology and talent.

negative_chunk_list.content:
At Shirakaba Maple Comprehensive University, in formulating the master plan for the redevelopment of the East Campus District, a real-time measurement system was first introduced to accurately diagnose the energy efficiency of existing facilities. Using an all-weather sensor network, electric power and thermal loads are collected on a per-minute basis, and the data are analyzed by AI to calculate optimal operation patterns. Particularly noteworthy is the seasonal variability leveling technology that combines thermal storage tanks with ground-source heat pumps. In winter, heat is stored underground by utilizing day–night temperature differences, and in summer it is extracted as cooling energy, which is projected to reduce HVAC loads by more than 30%. In addition, the perovskite solar cell paving installed along campus walkways has a self-healing property that repairs micro-deformations caused by pedestrian loads, and it has been reported to be longer-lasting, lighter, and less costly to install than conventional panels. The university is advancing the implementation of these technologies in collaboration with the student-led “Energy Innovation Consortium,” and also holds regular public workshops attended by local high school students and corporate engineers.

In addition, the university has released a roadmap to raise the share of renewable energy to 80% by fiscal 2030. In Phase 1 of the roadmap (FY2025–FY2027), plans are underway to add high-efficiency monocrystalline panels to the roof of the existing Faculty of Science building, increasing power generation to 2.3 times the current level. In Phase 2 (FY2028–FY2030), the university will build a microgrid linked to a hydrogen station and promote mobility decarbonization measures by converting research drones and campus buses to fuel cells.

As part of the education reforms tied to the establishment of the Faculty of Engineering, it has been decided to introduce “Integrated Project-Based Learning (i‑PBL)” as a university-wide required course. In i‑PBL, vertically organized teams composed of students from the first year through graduate school tackle real-world challenges provided by local communities and international organizations. For example, in a snow-removal robot optimization project for heavy snowfall regions, sub-themes such as a snowfall prediction model using machine learning, lightweight actuator design, and remote operation UX improvement branch off, with distributed development carried out by students of different years and majors taking charge of each. Final presentations are held at the university’s startup support hub “Skyward Hub,” where a system is in place for receiving direct feedback from local governments and VCs.

The i‑PBL evaluation method is also distinctive: it abolishes conventional end-of-term exam–centric grading and instead employs rubric-based assessment along multiple axes, such as milestone achievement in projects, intra-team reviews, and the degree to which outcomes are returned to the community. Students publish their activity logs in a Git-based “learning e-portfolio,” where faculty and external mentors can provide real-time comments. Furthermore, a self-reflection support tool leveraging generative AI, “Mirror‑AI,” has been introduced, enabling students to reflect in natural language on their technical growth and team contributions and receive improvement suggestions from the AI.

In addition, to support i‑PBL, the university has established the regional industry collaboration fund “Bridge‑Local Fund.” This mechanism allows local companies to disclose their technical challenges to the university, to which student teams submit solution proposals. For selected proposals, companies receive matched funding for proof-of-concept costs of up to 3 million yen.

As libraries come to incorporate “open lab” functions, their collection acquisition strategies are shifting from a traditional focus on academic monographs to an emphasis on digital IP assets. Universities are negotiating with major STM publishers to enable the sharing of papers and datasets via smart contracts through blockchain-based license management. As a result, a “data paper” style—rapidly publishing research data and experimental logs with DOIs—has become widespread on campus. In the open lab’s equipment orientations, five proficiency levels are defined, ranging from beginner courses in 3D modeling to advanced modules on multimaterial printing, and badge-style digital certificates are issued upon completion of each stage. These certificates comply with the international LER standard for learning record sharing, making them easy to submit when applying for external hackathons or internships.

The open lab also emphasizes its role as a “living lab,” hosting a monthly workshop called “Future Workshop” for local residents. Aimed at participants from elementary school students to seniors, it offers topics that combine science education with addressing community challenges, such as assistive devices made with 3D pens and low-cost seismograph DIY kits. Participants’ project data are stored in a cloud library, and improvements and usage reviews are recorded as peer comments, enabling cross-generational collaborative learning.

The university has also installed a “digital silent room” in conjunction with the renovation of the library building.

The launch of TechBridge Station aims to create a co-creation platform that shares R&D risks between the university and industry clusters and rapidly transfers outcomes to the market. Within the Station, three major facilities are planned: a high-throughput experimental robotics area for materials informatics, a testbed compatible with local 6G networks, and a hybrid indoor-outdoor validation field for autonomous mobile robotics. In particular, the high-throughput experimental robots can have AI automatically propose alloy design parameters and perform sample fabrication, property evaluation, and data feedback in continuous 24-hour operation, suggesting the potential to shorten the development cycle to one-tenth of the conventional timeframe.

The Station’s unique “reverse internship” system promotes two-way mobility, with corporate engineers residing full-time in university labs while students are dispatched for short-term assignments to corporate demonstration sites. Technical notes prepared during the dispatch period are registered in an open patent pool, and a structure is adopted in which intellectual property rights are jointly held by the company and the university even for student inventions. This reduces the time costs associated with IP negotiations and enables early prototype implementation.

The university also plans to expand international joint research with the Station as a hub by establishing the “Global Co-Lab” program for research institutions and startups in Europe and the United States. Teams selected for the program will receive subsidies for living expenses and free access to high-speed experimental facilities.

The Double Degree Acceleration Program partners with two research-intensive universities and offers three tracks: Mechanical Engineering, Environmental Systems Engineering, and Information and Communication Engineering. A key feature is its “phaseless progression” model, in which stages advance based on achieved competencies rather than earned credits, enabling flexible accommodation of both fast-track learners and those who prefer a more deliberate pace. In addition, a “Global Engineer Bootcamp” held prior to study abroad incorporates a curriculum covering intercultural mediation, contract negotiation exercises for international collaborative research, and multimodal presentation skills.

In terms of student support, tuition reduction schemes are complemented by cost-of-living scholarships from an international partnership fund. Furthermore, a “Legal Risk Safety Net” provides assistance with legal issues and access to medical care at host institutions. For post-return career paths, an online joint pitch event is held with overseas industry–academia hubs, and startups originating there are eligible for seed investment from the university’s in-house fund, “White Maple Ventures.”

As academic evaluation metrics for the program, in addition to the double-degree attainment rate, a “Transnational Publication Index (TPI)” has been established to monitor co-authored papers, international joint patents, and the number of cross-institutional projects. In the initial pilot cohort, Cohort‑Alpha (42 participants), more than half submitted papers to international peer-reviewed journals by their second year, and the provisional TPI exceeded the international average.

Pebble Mentorship adopts an xAPI-based Learning Record Store as its backend for multi-dimensional learning data integration, enabling integrated analysis not only of grade trajectories in completed courses but also of campus card usage data, library checkout histories, and stress indices derived from heart rate variability. In the system’s algorithmic layer, a “situation-adaptive bandit” based on meta-learning methods is implemented, which dynamically optimizes the sequencing and difficulty of practice problems when it detects shifts in learner interests or temporary dips in motivation. In the first-year pilot, the rate of retakes for basic mathematics reportedly dropped from 18% to 6%, and faculty workload was reduced by the equivalent of 12 hours per week.

The peer mentor program is linked with the Discord-based “Study-Commons,” where senior mentors streamline Q&A through chatbot integration. The bot provides first-line answers to FAQs and escalates complex questions to mentors. As incentives for mentors, in addition to awarding a “Teaching Contribution Badge” upon graduation, opportunities are offered to co-author papers with the educational technology laboratory. Data governance across the entire system is reviewed by the “Learning Data Ethics Board,” which includes student representatives; anonymization levels and the scope of secondary use are reviewed each semester.

Furthermore, integration with the metaverse environment “White Maple VR Plaza” enables remote students and working adult learners to participate in synchronous learning within virtual classrooms. Inside the VR Plaza, “remote experimental exercises” utilizing motion capture are offered, allowing real-world experimental equipment to be remotely operated through integration with IoT devices.

An evaluation framework centered on the Social Implementation Index (SII) is composed of five category indicators to visualize the practical usability of research outcomes. (1) Speed of social penetration: measures the time it takes for research results to reach policy recommendations, standardization, and commercialization. (2) Number of joint pilots: counts the number of collaborative pilot projects with local governments, companies, and NPOs. (3) Economic ripple coefficient: estimates economic impact such as licensing revenue and spinoff company sales. (4) Degree of societal problem-solving: quantifies contributions to achieving the 17 SDG sustainability indicators. (5) Contribution to human resource development: measures the employment rate of lab alumni in social implementation fields.

Labs submit an “implementation roadmap” when applying for annual budgets and undergo interim reviews every six months. If progress is insufficient, they formulate improvement plans with the technology transfer office and reconsider IP policies and partnership strategies. Conversely, labs with high achievement are granted a fast-track review called “Green Lane,” which offers benefits such as streamlined administrative procedures when securing external funding or participating in international joint projects.

With the introduction of the SII, a research evaluation culture that overemphasized publication counts has gradually shifted, prompting a mindset change among faculty. According to an internal survey, within two years of implementation, the share of joint pilot projects in external research funding rose from 27% to 45%. In addition, the number of press releases on research results has doubled, with more cases being picked up not only by local newspapers but also by international news wires.

The evaluation system works with the IT department to automatically visualize research outputs and social implementation impact via an AI dashboard using a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approach.

The Hands-on Internship Pipeline consists of three stages: “Foundational coursework on campus → Short-term corporate training → Joint results presentation.” In the spring semester, foundational exercises are conducted on campus, where students acquire multi-skill competencies such as data analysis with Python, FEM analysis, microcontroller control, and agile development methods. The summer internship is a 4–6 week intensive program in which students gain practical experience, such as developing outdoor sensor platforms at IoT startups or evaluating production line automation algorithms at long-established manufacturers. In the fall semester, the university and partner companies co-host a results presentation, and outstanding projects are published as videos on the university’s official YouTube channel.

Within the Pipeline, students’ interests, skills, and career orientations are scored using a “Multi-Factor Matching Algorithm,” which automates matching with corporate job requirements. To improve matching accuracy, companies register not only project details but also team culture and the technology stack using detailed tags. After completing the internship, students automatically generate their outcomes and reviews as a “digital portfolio,” which can be repurposed for other courses or job application materials.

Furthermore, as a mechanism to develop ideas born through the internship, a “Mini-Grant Challenge” is held in the winter semester. Student teams can apply for prototyping funds with a short proposal of up to five pages, with an acceptance rate set at around 30%. Selected teams receive access to the university’s fabrication facilities, faculty mentors, and external consultants, and are required to complete market research, user testing, and MVP creation within three months.

The Future-Ready Curriculum (FRC) is designed around three core competencies—integrative knowledge, data-driven thinking, and ethical judgment—allowing students to freely choose theme-driven modules each term. Modules vary in length from as short as two weeks to as long as eight weeks, seamlessly combining on-demand lecture videos with hands-on exercises. For example, in the “Designing a Carbon-Neutral Society” module, students practice life cycle assessment methods, carbon tax simulations, and alternative fuel evaluation experiments; the capstone assignment requires submitting a scenario planning report and a system dynamics model.

Curriculum progress is visualized in a “Competency Heat-map,” which can be viewed in real time by students, faculty, and career advisors. This makes it possible to quantify not only the number of credits toward graduation requirements but also the distribution of acquired skills. Upon graduation, digital credentials are issued and recorded on a blockchain as reliable learning evidence, integrated via API with employers’ applicant tracking systems.

FRC is also linked to the “Social Collaboration Project Desk,” featuring an open curriculum that can adopt challenges submitted by external organizations as theme modules. This enables flexible reconfiguration of content mid-term in response to the urgency of social issues, achieving resilient educational operations.

The Spark‑Seed Grant adopts a “2‑Step Funding” model to support innovative exploratory research. Step 1 provides up to 2 million yen over six months as a small-scale fund focused on proof of concept, preliminary data acquisition, and pilot device fabrication. Projects deemed to have high potential impact at the interim review are advanced to Step 2, which adds up to 10 million yen and extends support for up to 18 months. Evaluations are conducted through expert peer review and a social implementation advisory board, emphasizing a balance between academic originality and societal benefit.

A distinctive feature of the grant is the mandatory “feed‑forward pitch” within three months of selection. In this session, researchers present interim progress and next‑step plans, while senior mentors and commercialization consultants provide immediate feedback. This enables rapid course correction and swift securing of additional resources. The program is also linked to a “negative_chunk_list Results Repository” that openly shares insights gained from failures, contributing to improved reproducibility and the avoidance of redundant investments.

To bridge Spark‑Seed outcomes to real‑world implementation with hands‑on support, the center operates the “Spin‑Off Launchpad” startup support program. It provides end‑to‑end assistance from IP clearance and fundraising pitches to regulatory research and early customer development, creating an environment where early‑career researchers can take risks without fear.

reasoning_content: Regarding the establishment of the Faculty of Engineering at Hakufu Comprehensive University, it is clearly stated that a “total initial investment of 500 million yen” had already been decided in fiscal year 2024. It is also noted that an “additional budget of 200 million yen” from the government was formally allocated within the same fiscal year. Both of these funds were secured during fiscal year 2024 and share the same purpose (supporting the establishment and operation of the Faculty of Engineering). Therefore, adding them together—500 million yen + 200 million yen = 700 million yen—it can be concluded that the total amount procured in fiscal year 2024 is 700 million yen.

question: Please answer with the birth year in the Gregorian calendar of the wife of the actor who starred in the film “Eternal Weathercock.”

positive_chunk_list.content: Eternal Weathercock is a Japanese coming-of-age drama film released in 2018. It was directed by Shota Imai, who had worked on numerous independent projects and was establishing himself as a young filmmaker, and with this film finally made his way into the mainstream of commercial cinema. This is his third commercial feature film. The lead role is played by Yoichi Suzuki. His character, Hayato Hayakawa, is a high school senior living in a small seaside town who becomes involved as a volunteer in the restoration of the town’s symbolic weathercock tower, which has fallen into disrepair. Through this project, Hayato confronts the town’s history, his family roots, and his own future path, eventually taking his first step into adulthood. The film was primarily set and shot in the Sotobo area of Chiba Prefecture, with a real, ruin-like lighthouse serving as the model for the weathercock tower in the film. Filming took place from September to November 2017, and with the cooperation of local residents, more than 300 extras participated in total. At the 42nd JAPAN Film Awards, the film won Best Picture, while Director Imai was awarded Best Director, and lead actor Yoichi Suzuki was also nominated for Best Actor. The music was composed by Ryosaku Onodera, and the theme song, The Day I Heard the Voice of the Wind, became a hot topic; the soundtrack album debuted at No. 7 on the Oricon charts. The film was screened at festivals in Japan and abroad, earning widespread acclaim worldwide and high praise as “a fresh coming-of-age film shaped by the sensibilities of a new generation.” Following a nationwide roadshow in 160 theaters, DVD and streaming releases were rolled out, and the film has enjoyed long-running sales, especially among young audiences.

Yoichi Suzuki (born June 15, Showa60) is a Japanese actor. His blood type is A, and he is 180 cm tall. He is represented by the talent agency Elite Promotion, which is also home to many film and stage actors. He made his debut in the 2005 television drama “Memory of the Wind,” where his delicate emotional expression and transparent acting drew attention. Since then, he has consistently appeared in TV dramas, films, and stage productions. In the early years of his career, he often appeared in school and medical dramas, but from 2010 onward he shifted his focus mainly to film, and in recent years he has taken on an increasing number of leading roles.

His notable works include “Eternal Weathercock,” as well as “A Dream at Summer’s End,” “Connected City,” and “Forms by the Water,” all of which have earned him a reputation for naturalistic portrayals of down-to-earth young men. On stage, his broad acting range was highly praised in “Our Blue Memories,” where he convincingly played a character from youth to middle age.

In his private life, he enjoys the outdoors—fishing, camping, and mountain climbing—and his photography, which he began as a hobby, has been lauded as professional-grade. In 2021, he held his first solo exhibition, “Strata of Light,” at a gallery in Tokyo.

He maintains a highly disciplined approach to acting, often spending months mastering regional dialects for roles and repeatedly gaining or losing weight as needed. In interviews, his statement, “An actor’s mission is to live out a character’s life, even if only once,” leaves a strong impression, and he is regarded as a role model for younger actors.

Looking ahead, he is considering appearances at international film festivals and roles in English-speaking markets, and he is actively studying languages.

~ February 20, 2012 ~ | Surprise Wedding: Actor Yoichi Suzuki has tied the knot in a whirlwind marriage with actress Haruna Araki, three years his junior! The birth of a couple from the same agency has become a major topic in the entertainment world. The announcement was made simultaneously on February 22 via both of their official fan sites and a press fax. According to the press materials, the two grew close after co-starring in a 2011 drama and, following about six months of dating, decided to marry. Fans flooded in with comments like “so refreshingly perfect” and “an ideal couple,” and they ranked second that year in the “Most Ideal Celebrity Couple” poll.

Haruna Araki, from Hyogo Prefecture, joined a local theater troupe after graduating high school and built stage experience before moving to Tokyo in 2011. After auditions, she made her drama debut that fall and was a newcomer who had been drawing attention for her fresh performance and luminous expressions. With this marriage, it was announced that she would temporarily pause her activities, though she also indicated plans to return after balancing childcare.

At the wedding press conference, Suzuki said, “The greatest happiness is being able to share ordinary, everyday life together,” enveloping the venue in a warm atmosphere that even prompted applause from the press. The ceremony and reception were held only for those involved, and their private life is being carefully protected.

Though they have refrained from co-appearing in the media since their marriage, they have appeared together at fan events and their agency’s year-end appreciation festivals, suggesting things are going smoothly both publicly and privately. On social media, fans send congratulatory messages every year on Araki’s birthday—which is also their legal marriage date—cementing their status as a “memorial-day couple.”

negative_chunk_list.content: Behind-the-scenes: On this production, the set ran a dual-camera workflow using the Arri ALEXA Mini and RED WEAPON. Specifically, for morning front-lit scenes the ALEXA Mini was run at ISO 800 in ProRes 4444, while for afternoon backlight and magic hour the RED WEAPON was set to ISO 1600 with R3D 8:1 compression to preserve highlight latitude. DP Shunsuke Saeki remarked, “What a coming-of-age film needs is ‘dewy skin’ and ‘a particulate sense of air.’” Both cameras were paired with Cooke S4/i primes, and during DI they based the grade on a Film Print Emulation LUT Type 2393, fine-tuning saturation with Resolve power curves. For the spiral staircase sequence inside the lighthouse, they used a Ronin 2 with an Easyrig Vega, climbing 72 steps over 17 takes—walking a total of 1.4 km—to complete a single continuous shot. In the third week of October, after consecutive rain delays, sea spray salt caused the lens coatings to haze, and the optical team disassembled and cleaned them overnight with industrial isopropyl alcohol. They also stacked an ND filter No. 1.2 with a Black Mist 1/8 and shot at a relatively shallow T2.8–4.0 to visualize the “humid air.” For the final day’s sunset shot, they targeted the narrow four-minute window from a solar altitude of –1.5 deg to –0.3 deg, with the AD calling, “If we’re 17 seconds off, it’s a retake,” while using an app synced to GPS time and an altimeter. The result—the last shot of the protagonist gazing at the sea beyond the weathercock tower—was captured on a knife-edge schedule and became known as the “miracle take,” a story retold even in the Blu-ray audio commentary.

Behind the score and soundtrack: Composer Ryo Onodera defines this work as a “cinema cantata where the sound of surf syncs with breath,” composing the main theme in C minor Ionian mode. An unconventional orchestration was employed—two harps, 38-piece full orchestra, and a solo clarinet. The Budapest string sessions were captured a bit dry with DPA mics, while only the woodwinds were overdubbed at AIR Lyndhurst in London. Reverb was mixed 65:35 between IR-based and real reverb, placed on a spectrum with the ocean’s low-frequency roar EQ’d out to achieve an image “like a sea breeze grazing the eardrum.” Furthermore, the songs by the indie band Seaside Tracer that the protagonist listens to on earphones were actually recorded on a beach: the bass cabinet was half-buried in the sand and powered by a generator while tracking in parallel. Eight “air” mics were routed for ambience, and playback was timed to when the incoming tide would curl under the speaker’s bottom panel, executing real seawater-induced Doppler modulation. This audacious field recording was featured in the May issue of the industry magazine Sound & Stage Japan, highlighted as “the resonance of cinema and phenomenological sound.” The first-press limited edition of the soundtrack includes an A3 fold-out poster and a 20-page booklet. The booklet contains score excerpts from five unused tracks and composer-annotated diagrams of the modulation sequences.

Promotional strategy: The distributor Cinema Frontier launched the cross-platform “Weathercock Challenge” on social media 100 days before release. On Twitter Spaces, they distributed AR voice cards featuring cast members adding their voices for five minutes only, and on TikTok they solicited lip-sync videos under the hashtag “#KazekoeLipSync.” According to the ad agency’s report, user-generated content reached 84,000 posts and 190 million total views by the day before release, ranking third among interest keywords for Gen Z women. Additionally, on the four Toei Subway lines, vertically oriented in-car monitor ads were taken over with a surprise gimmick that switched to “seaside murmur visuals” at the exact moment the train entered a tunnel. Passengers posted on social media that “it almost smelled like the sea for a moment,” and it scored a buzz index of 126 points. Advance screenings were held at 12 theaters in Tokyo under the banner “Vari-Angle Theater,” where seats and screen were tilted by 5 degrees to simulate a “sea-breeze gaze.” The Webtoon “Behind the Scenes in 1 Minute,” accessible via QR cards given to attendees, surpassed 120,000 page views within 24 hours, and the film’s official LINE subscribers reached 110,000 before release.

Details of Domestic and International Film Festivals and Awards: During its editing stage, the film toured 12 cities across 9 countries, starting with the Vancouver International Film Festival. At the Santa Barbara International Film Festival, its 124-minute runtime exceeded the limit by four minutes, leading to a last-minute re-encode of a “1080p 30 fps preview version” at a local hotel—an anecdote that remains. As a result, it missed the Audience Award but was nominated for the Special Jury Award. At the Ljubljana Film Festival, it won the “New Generation Landscapes Award,” with Croatian media praising it as “a new lens on seaside nostalgia.” Domestically, in addition to the 42nd JAPAN Film Awards, it received the Technical Award at the “Regional Coexistence Cinema Grand Prize,” which focuses on regional revitalization. In his acceptance speech, Director Imai said, “The Weathercock Tower transcended the bounds of a film set to become a ‘square’ that preserves the town’s memory,” sparking a social media trend around the “interaction between town and film.” The commemorative booklet ran 48 pages, covering the tower’s blueprints, the evolution of its CG, and even scripts for deleted scenes, and sold out 40,000 copies at theater concession stands. Such international festival recognition is rare in Japan and has become a hot topic in the news and on social media. The All-Japan Film Production Committee commented that, to increase the number of such award-winning works in the future, it is necessary to expand the infrastructure required for film production.

[Director Shota Imai Filmography, Thorough Dissection] Tracing Shota Imai’s visual trajectory leads back to his college-days 8 mm experimental film “An Afternoon When Raindrops Play in Reverse” (2007) as the origin point. By scratching the film directly with a needle to mosaic the surface of water after rainfall, the piece won the Audience Award at a student-run film festival during his studies. His graduation project “Counting to the Bottom of the Pool” (2012) is known as a 31-minute single-take “water-surface reflection cinema,” shot in an indoor pool after closing hours with only two lights. The technique of using reflected light off the water as a substitute for bounce light was featured in a six-page special in the trade magazine CinePhoto. The follow-up “Around When April Ends” (2015) is an eight-chapter omnibus youth tale; unable to secure a release, they staged a self-run screening at Kyoto Minami-za. Attendance was 1,438, but the pamphlet zine spread by word of mouth, reaching a cumulative 3,200 copies across Tokyo, Nagoya, and Fukuoka. All proceeds were pooled as development funds for the next project, becoming a pioneering case of “self-crowdfunding.”

His first commercial work, “The Metaphor of the Sea Roar” (2017), was a TV movie in a late-night local slot, but ranked 10th in Kinema Junpo as an ambitious piece portraying the fusion of a fishing port and electronic music. Here he teamed up for the first time with composer Ryo Onodera, establishing a partnership that would become standard thereafter.

“Withering Weathercock” (2018) secured major distribution with his third film. In keeping with the policy of “preserving the soul of indie cinema,” the production designer, gaffer, and assistant director from his indie days continued on. The script went through 12 drafts; in the first draft the protagonist leaves town in a bitter ending, but with 68% of test-screening respondents expressing a desire for “rebirth and inheritance,” a large-scale additional shoot was undertaken to reshoot the epilogue during editing.

[Ultra-Detailed Production of the Theme Song “The Day I Heard the Voice of the Wind”] The theme song competition began with a closed, in-house presentation by three female singers. The judging criteria were three axes—“sea salinity,” “humidity,” and “wind speed”—with ten producers each scoring out of five points. Lisa Miyauchi, who made it to the final selection, proposed a multi-layered scale concept themed “sea roar × youth”: primarily in C major but modulating to the minor third at the start of the chorus, and further introducing Lydian #2 in the bridge.

The recording location was a wooden traditional house on Sakushima, Aichi Prefecture. They adopted a “floor resonance rhythm” by removing tatami mats and embedding 30 piezo mics under the floor to capture footstep vibrations as a kick. Forty-two glass fishing floats hung from the ceiling beams served as a natural reverb body, and it took nine hours of minute adjustments, down to the millimeter, to tune their positions. Because commercial power on the island is unstable, electricity was supplied to the studio via a 100 m cable from a marine generator moored at the pier. During late-night hours, the light diesel exhaust noise bled into the recordings, so the generator was shut down and recording continued using LED lanterns and portable power stations.

The lyrics were refined over a three-day “island retreat,” and a fisherman’s words heard at the harbor—“The wind carries the past, but the smell brings the future”—were quoted in the second chorus. The music video was shot on 16 mm reversal film, with the cinematographer deliberately specifying development that “retains perforation scratches.” Light leaks on the sprocket side produced rainbow-like halation, praised as embodying the “fleeting granularity of youth.”

Lead actor Yoichi Suzuki’s complete record of stoic role preparation: To bring realism to his character—a “weathervane tower restoration volunteer”—Yoichi Suzuki joined a construction company in Sosa, Chiba Prefecture as an apprentice three months before filming. On weekdays he assembled scaffolding from 6 a.m., then spent afternoons removing rust and applying anti-corrosion coatings. Determined to understand “pitting corrosion caused by sea breezes,” he devoured specialist books on metal corrosion and even made daily pH measurements of steel a routine.

In addition to completing training for operating a 10-meter aerial work platform, he also took on the written exam for second-class electrical work certification. Photos of him solving electrical resistance problems in the production van during breaks went viral on Twitter, sparking a fan-led “Study for certifications with Yoichi” hashtag. In the end, he did not sit the national exam due to scheduling conflicts, but a set of official textbooks covered in his red-pen notes fetched 250,000 yen at a charity auction.

For weight control, he dropped 6 kg. His nutritionist-supervised “sea-breeze cut” diet centered on salted-kelp rice balls (2 g NaCl), clam soup (for taurine), and seared bonito (high protein, low fat). With filming running late into the night while he was at a BMI of 18.2, he wore a diving undersuit beneath his school uniform to prevent body temperature drops. The costume team added ventilation holes to vent humidity.

His acting notes documented everything from “armpit sweat NaCl concentration graphs” and “night dew humidity curves” to the “frequency of wind fluctuations,” with six pages excerpted in the film magazine Cut. In an interview, he said, “A coming-of-age film isn’t worth a damn unless it’s drenched in sweat and salt spray. I wanted to breathe the sea through the body of the character,” and his co-stars dubbed him a “human hyperlink.”

Full Report on Production Troubles and Safety Management: The lighthouse’s interior staircase, a 90-year-old reinforced concrete structure, had insufficient load capacity. After consultations with the fire department, a special exemption was granted to install temporary reinforcement with aluminum beams “only during filming.” The art department recreated rust by applying baked-on paint and sprinkling iron oxide powder on the surface, and reduced weight by using resin replicas for screw heads. However, three staff members reported mild headaches due to paint fumes, and an emergency nighttime operation removed the seaward window glass to install ventilation fans. The fans were fitted with custom silencers keeping noise under 37 dB, and the sound team conducted noise measurements until 3 a.m.

Eight days before wrap, Typhoon No. 22 made a direct hit, recording a maximum instantaneous wind speed of 42 m/s. Four scaffold clamps were pulled out, and a steelwork contractor rushed in from Tokyo to replace all clamps with steel ones within 12 hours. The insurance company’s assessment team filmed damaged areas in 4K with three drones and calculated losses at 7.2 million yen. A two-week release delay was decided, and the PR team live-streamed an emergency online event titled “The Storm Passed and the Town and Film Became One.” The lead actor, director, and head of the fisheries cooperative took the stage; concurrent viewership hit 48,000, and the chat was flooded with messages like “Supporting you even with the delay” and “A set hotter than the typhoon.” Advance ticket cancellations were kept to just 1.7%, and Toho’s publicity department analyzed the case as a “model example of crisis management,” incorporating it into internal training materials.

After wrap, at the fishing port where filming took place, scaffold materials were repurposed into benches and installed as the “Weathercock Tower Typhoon Memorial” through spring 2021. A mini-gallery was opened with 30 on-set photo panels donated by the film’s official team, turning the site into a new pilgrimage spot for fans.

[Shiomi Town: 300-Year History and the Evolution of the Weathercock Tower] According to the script setting compendium “Shiomi Town Archives,” Shiomi Town originated in 1702 as the fishing hamlet “Shiomiura,” founded by Jihei Katoriya, a fishing boss from Kazusa. By the late 18th century, the boom in sardine net fishing caused the population to surge from 360 to 1,200, and 40 shore-seine boats lined the beach. The prototype of the meteorological observation tower was a three-story wooden pavilion built in 1804, its roof adorned with a brass crane-shaped weather vane said to have been an imported gem from a Chinese-goods dealer in Nagasaki. A repair record in the 1822 document “Shiomi Beach Diary” notes, “Crane-shaped weather vane rusted, hence gilding applied.”

The tower was completely destroyed in the 1923 Great Kanto Earthquake. It was rebuilt in 1930 (Showa 5) in reinforced concrete, with a 27-meter shaft and a 3-meter weathercock crowning it as the town’s symbol. During World War II, to avoid requisition of metals, it was disguised as an air-raid observation post, with “observation windows” hastily painted in ink on the wings. After the war it was relit as a maritime lighthouse (1952), but operations ceased in 1968 due to the oil shock. In 1985 (Showa 60), the local youth group launched a preservation movement; although the town council debated preservation versus demolition for four hours, it was designated a town cultural property in 1998.

In the film, this “nearly lost symbol” is reinterpreted as a story of restoration. In the third draft of the script, a proposal in which “it collapses again during restoration and the protagonist stands in stunned silence” was considered, but at test screenings, the view “inheritance over loss” prevailed, leading to the current ending. The “Shiomi Festival” is an event that fuses the Edo-period “Beach Kagura” and a lighthouse-lighting ceremony praying for a bountiful catch. Although the script contained 16 pages of festival dialogue, in the completed film it appears only in fragments.

reasoning_content: The lead actor of the film is Yoichi Suzuki, and his profile clearly states that he was born in Showa 60 (1985). The marriage report notes that his wife, Haruna Araki, is three years younger than the actor, so subtracting 3 from 1985 means the wife’s birth year is 1988.

“Salary earners” refers to individuals who received wage payments for at least one month during the survey year (for FY Reiwa 4, January–December 2022), and in principle includes temporary employees and short-term part-time workers. In this questionnaire, corporations are classified into five capital-size categories (less than 20 million yen, 20 million yen or more, 50 million yen or more, 100 million yen or more, and 10 billion yen or more), and in addition to these capital categories, “sole proprietors” and “other corporations (such as cooperatives and public-interest corporations)” are listed separately. It should be noted that the table does not describe procedures for eliminating duplicates when aggregating multiple establishments across capital-size categories, and researchers are advised to carefully adjust for shifts in capital categories due to corporate restructuring and mergers when conducting time-series comparisons. Furthermore, since this survey is not a nationwide mandatory submission but rather aggregates payroll reports submitted in connection with year-end tax adjustments, differences in municipalities’ rates of digitization and compliance with submission deadlines have been reported to affect prefecture-level aggregates. For example, in major metropolitan areas where electronic submissions are more prevalent, underreporting and missing data are captured early, whereas in regions where paper-based submissions are the norm, data entry delays may be corrected across fiscal years. To smooth such statistical fluctuations, domestic and international research institutions employ approaches such as three-year moving averages and logistic adjustments to mitigate outliers and estimate long-term trends.

An indispensable element in discussing structural changes in the Japanese economy is the transition in employment structure by industry, beginning with the bursting of the bubble in the early 1990s. According to the long-term series of the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications’ Labour Force Survey, employment in wholesale and retail decreased gradually from about 15 million in 1991 to about 11 million in 2012, and has since remained roughly flat, supported by the progress of e-commerce and the recovery of inbound demand. Meanwhile, the medical and welfare fields have expanded to around 9 million workers by 2022 from about 3 million in 1991 amid accelerating population aging. However, the increase in employment in “medical and welfare” does not directly translate into a rise in wage earners; given the rising share of non-regular workers at social welfare corporations and medical corporations, it is important to reassess the effective volume of labor input by incorporating average hourly wages and annual income.

One metric that attempts such an assessment is the Weighted Labor Input Index (WLI). WLI decomposes the number of employed persons by employment status (regular/non-regular), gender, and age cohort, and applies weights for working hours and wages to each category to construct an index. This approach is said to visualize qualitative changes in the labor market that cannot be captured by simple changes in headcount. For example, while the number of workers in manufacturing has continued to decline, wage premiums for engineers and researchers who generate value added have risen, and analyses using WLI report cases where the “quality” of labor input in manufacturing has in fact improved.

From a tax perspective, employment income is subject to the employment income deduction and the basic deduction, and income tax is imposed under the comprehensive taxation system. However, even within the same “Survey on Private Sector Wages” tables, the “average wage” and “average age” are simple averages across groups of differing sizes and industries, so it is not recommended to reuse them as-is as the denominator for the so-called labor share used by economists. When calculating the labor share rigorously, note that “total personnel expenses” under corporate accounting standards include retirement benefit expenses and statutory welfare expenses, whereas take-home pay and withholding tax amounts for employees are presented on a gross, pre-social insurance contribution basis. Ignoring this definitional discrepancy leads to overestimating the share in sectors like manufacturing and construction, where retirement benefit provisions are large, while underestimating it in sectors like services and retail, where the proportion of non-regular workers is high. It is standard practice to use the METI “Basic Survey of Japanese Business Structure and Activities” and the MOF “Financial Statements Statistics of Corporations by Industry” in combination, obtaining total personnel expenses and total wage payments separately and cross-checking them. Also, the “number of employment income earners” and the “number of employed persons” differ in terms of the statistical survey unit under the Statistics Act. The former is measured on a person basis after deduplication of those who received wage payments, whereas the latter uses the concept of the “main job,” resulting in different treatment when aggregating side-job workers and part-time farmers. In cases where freelancers with multiple side jobs receive employment income on a project basis, the same individual is not counted under multiple companies, but they may not be included as a primary-occupation freelancer in the “Employment Structure Basic Survey.”

The survey form PDF file (nta.go.jp/publication/statistics/kokuzeicho/minkan2022/pdf/000.pdf) is not sufficiently compatible with screen readers for the visually impaired and does not comply with the tagged PDF specification, so additional OCR processing may be required to parse table structures that heavily use merged cells and vertically written cells. In practice, researchers use open-source table extraction libraries, specifying the coordinates of table regions on each page and converting them to CSV, but vertically long column headers (e.g., cells with “Average monthly number of salary earners per year” rotated for vertical writing) tend to be treated as blanks during extraction. Therefore, it is necessary to build a series of scripts to assign column names in post-processing. Recently, “PDF structure tree” parsing models (such as LayoutLM and Donut) have attracted attention; through a hybrid approach combining natural language processing and image processing, they can align token sequences geometrically to infer table structures without explicitly recognizing table lines or cell boundaries. Thanks to this technological innovation, it has become possible to accurately reconstruct tables even on pages common in survey PDFs that mix complex ruling, merged cells, and handwritten formats. However, engines that can handle Japanese vertical writing simultaneously are currently limited, so when handling it simply in Python, it is recommended to install an OCR engine additionally. It should be noted that some statistical tables use symbols like “–” and “△” as annotations, resulting in a mixture of simple zero values and missing values.

From a macroeconomic perspective, it is necessary to separately examine short-term shocks and medium- to long-term structural changes when assessing the pandemic’s impact on labor demand. In particular, the rapid spread of telework created a dual phenomenon—rising office vacancy rates in the Tokyo metropolitan area and increased migration to regional areas—advancing a “decoupling of residence and workplace,” especially among IT and creative professions. According to statistics, the net outflow from Tokyo to other prefectures in 2021 was the largest since records began; however, the number of salary earners based on year-end tax adjustment remained almost flat, suggesting an increase in employment contracts premised on remote work (so-called “remote employees”). In addition, companies of all sizes have steadily adopted SaaS-based HR management systems, and the formation of talent pools unconstrained by employment type or place of residence has been identified as a management priority. Alongside this, efforts have been made to introduce pay-for-position schemes and expand job-based employment, with a growing number of cases in which IT and digital talent are managed on compensation tables independent from traditional membership-based pay systems. To respond to these changes, new fields have been established for “those whose primary workplace is online” and “crowdsourcing contractors who earned remuneration through side jobs,” and the items have been expanded to capture telework ratios and side-job ratios.

When handling “means” or “totals” in statistical analysis, a key caution is the treatment of outliers in data with heavy-tailed distributions. Salary distributions are often approximated by lognormal or Pareto distributions; in particular, the top 1%—where executive compensation, stock options, and performance-based pay are readily aggregated—can substantially raise the mean, whereas the median and mode can serve as more appropriate indicators of the broader labor market. Moreover, for international comparisons of income distributions, P50 (median) and the P90/P10 ratio (an inequality measure) are recommended over the mean, and there is a move to recalculate Japanese employment data using the same standards. It should be noted that the statistics lack an open-ended top bracket above 10 million yen, and the inability to disaggregate the earnings of ultra-high-income individuals in detail poses a research constraint. Although the government reports the number of taxpayers by excess progressive tax rate brackets, these figures combine business income, forestry income, and capital gains with wage income; thus, to track wage income alone at high resolution, distribution extrapolation methods (e.g., Pareto interpolation or extrapolation via generalized logit models) are required. After performing such interpolations and computing Gini coefficients by employment type, many cases estimate around 0.25 for regular employees and around 0.35 for non-regular employees, though it is well known that estimates vary substantially depending on the methodology.

reasoning_content: According to the table, the total number of salary earners is 13,264,951 in the information and communications industry and 52,411,340 in the manufacturing industry, so adding these together, the combined total number of salary earners in the information and communications and manufacturing industries is 65,676,291.

When companies dispatch employees, whether internationally or domestically, they must manage carry-on equipment from a multilayered perspective. The first point to note is the “multiplicity of risk sources,” where not only the physical hazards of the devices themselves but also aspects such as information leakage, business continuity, and supply chain dependencies are intricately intertwined. For example, a laptop that uses a universal adapter supporting AC 100–240 V is not electrically hazardous, but if its firmware settings are improperly configured when taken offsite, it may risk malware infection via public Wi‑Fi in a third country. In the incident review conducted by headquarters’ CSIRT for fiscal year 2024, the most frequent cases involved hotel environments with continuous power availability fostering complacency, leading employees to access internal file shares without using a VPN. Against this background, the company revised its procedures so that only devices for which a request has been submitted to the IT asset management department at least 14 days before travel, and that have obtained proof of firmware signature verification and completed disk encryption, are permitted to be taken offsite. Furthermore, for power-related peripherals, it explicitly made requirements that AC plugs must be PSE-certified and have tracking resistance, and that overcurrent protection under the USB‑PD standard must function properly. This reduces the risk of fire due to voltage mismatch with local power systems or thermal runaway. However, these technical requirements must track the annual revisions of IEC 62368‑1; if older-generation chargers are to remain in use, the on-site manager must submit a separate “provisional use application.”

Regulations on air transport of equipment equipped with lithium-ion batteries have been increasingly subdivided year by year, following successive revisions to the Technical Instructions set by the International Civil Aviation Organization (ICAO) and the IATA Dangerous Goods Regulations in response to the recent rise in fire incidents. When devices contain cells with a watt-hour rating (Wh) exceeding 100 Wh and up to 160 Wh, the principle is to remove the battery from the device, package it separately, implement short-circuit protection measures, and carry it onboard as hand luggage. In the 66th edition of the IATA DGR, effective January 2025, the description in UN3481 Packing Instruction 967 Section II has been updated to add a “Protection Against Activation” provision for small, detachable battery packs available to end users. As a result, in addition to applying insulating tape to external terminals, the battery must be equipped with a physical switch capable of completely disabling its operating mode. Furthermore, with the expanded scope of application of EU Regulation 2019/1020 (the Market Surveillance Regulation), importers are now required to electronically submit documentation to customs demonstrating conformity with CE marking. In line with this trend, corporate overseas travel policies require that when bringing IT peripherals or presentation devices containing lithium-ion batteries, employees must fill out a Pre‑Departure Battery Declaration Form—including serial numbers—at the time of domestic departure and present it at the security checkpoint. This approach balances traceability and risk management of carried items and facilitates smooth communication with airlines and airport inspectors.

The liquid restrictions originated from the LAGs (Liquids, Aerosols, and Gels) rules introduced in response to the 2006 UK-originated terrorist plot, but their implementation has gradually evolved as airports in different countries upgrade their equipment. For example, at 12 major European airports, the introduction of CT-X-ray (Computed Tomography X-ray) scanners has progressed since 2024, allowing passengers to carry up to 2 liters of liquids through security without removing them from their bags. However, in the Asia-Pacific region, many airports have been slower to update their equipment, and the traditional rules of 100 mL containers within a 1 L transparent zip bag still remain in place. As a result, even on the same route, procedures may vary depending on the transit point, creating a risk of additional screening that may not fit within the connection time. Corporate travel management systems have implemented a feature that automatically classifies each transit airport’s “LAGs Policy Level” into four tiers from A (CT-scan compatible) to D (strict application of traditional rules) and displays alerts when creating itineraries. In cases where R&D engineers bring experimental samples to international conferences, chemicals that are lyophilized powders but become liquids upon dissolution may fall under LAGs, necessitating advance arrangements for a Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS) and a Temporary Export License. In 2023 alone, there were three reported cases in which customs clearance was delayed due to failures in arranging such documents, resulting in the cancellation of demonstration presentations. To balance transport efficiency with compliance, it is essential to understand not only equipment classifications but also the practicalities of international logistics.

As part of our compliance framework, the HR department has undertaken the first comprehensive overhaul in three years of the Overseas Business Trip Regulations, introducing a new scoring model that integrates destination risk assessment with carry-on equipment management. The model rates five indicators—(A) political/security, (B) medical infrastructure, (C) cyber threats, (D) natural disasters, and (E) logistics—on a 0–5 scale, and automatically generates a Mandatory Equipment List based on the total score. For example, in high-risk regions with an overall score of 18 or more, a satellite communication device, a foldable solar panel, and a portable UPS are mandatory, whereas in low-risk regions with a score of 8 or less, local procurement may be considered. A distinguishing feature of this revision is that decisions about permissible carry-on items are not confined to hazardous materials regulations alone but are integrated with business continuity management (BCM). For instance, even in low-risk regions, if the power supply is deemed unstable, carrying a portable battery (LiFePO₄) is recommended, and additional screening time at airport security is automatically factored into the itinerary. Furthermore, in countries assessed as having a high risk of information leakage, only external SSDs with hardware-level encryption are permitted, and a policy prohibiting data synchronization to local cloud services is enforced. This integrated approach makes it possible to ensure not only the “safety” of carry-on items but also the simultaneous “peace of mind” of protecting corporate assets and employees.

question: How many persentages is the maximum allowable carbon dioxide concentration percentage to ensure the performance level for the air environment in the Air Environment Performance Standards of the Nanfuta City Historical Museum?

positive_chunk_list.content:
Basic Performance Standards of the Nanfuta City Museum of History
4-3-4 Performance Related to Air Environment

- Categories: I | II
- Applicable rooms:
  - I: Rooms where office work is conducted
  - II: Equipment-related rooms requiring air environment control, telecommunications and information equipment rooms, storage rooms, or rooms where combustion gases or exhaust gases are generated
- Performance level:
  - I: An air environment suitable for office work, etc., is ensured.
  - II: An air environment suitable for maintaining the functions of equipment and the condition of stored items, etc., is ensured.

Nanfuta City History Museum Technical Items on Air Environment Performance

- Category
  - I
  - II

1. Setting the ventilation rate
- I:
  - Sufficient fresh air is ensured to allow comfortable and efficient work.
  - As a general rule, the ventilation rate is 30 m³/(h·person) or more, determined by considering the required air cleanliness for carbon dioxide (CO₂) and the CO₂ generation rate according to work intensity.
- II:
  - Fresh air appropriate to the room’s purpose and usage conditions is ensured.
  - The ventilation rate is set to suit the factors requiring ventilation, such as carbon dioxide (CO₂), carbon monoxide (CO), suspended particulate matter (SPM), and toxic gases.

2. Selection of ventilation method
- The ventilation method is suited to the room’s purpose, location, and floor area. Considering outdoor air inlets, exhaust outlets, the surrounding environment, building layout, and floor plan, the locations, etc., are such that outdoor air can be introduced to achieve the required air cleanliness.

3. Ensuring air cleanliness
- I:
  (1) Air cleanliness
  Air cleanliness levels ensuring comfortable and efficient work are maintained as follows:
  - CO₂: 1000 ppm or less
  - CO: 6 ppm or less
  - Suspended particulate matter: 0.15 mg/m³ or less
  - Formaldehyde: 0.1 mg/m³ or less
  (2) Air pollutants
  Emission sources of formaldehyde, volatile organic compounds, etc., are suppressed by appropriately selecting interior finishes, storage materials, and other materials.
- II:
  - Air cleanliness appropriate to the room’s purpose and usage conditions is ensured.
  - Air pollutants locally generated by smoking, combustion, etc., are treated near their sources.

negative_chunk_list.content:
4-3-6 Performance Related to Vibration

Regarding performance related to vibration, performance levels shall be established for vibrations caused by human activity or equipment, traffic, and wind, so as to prevent psychological or physiological discomfort due to vibrations from factors other than earthquakes.

1) Performance Related to Vibrations Caused by Human Activity or Equipment

[Basic Performance]
For rooms and similar spaces, under conditions where vibrations caused by human activity or equipment are likely to be perceived, the performance level shall be that no vibrations causing psychological or physiological discomfort occur, and this shall be ensured.

[Technical Provisions]
To ensure the performance level related to vibrations caused by human activity or equipment, the following technical provisions shall be satisfied.

1. Target Response Acceleration
Under excitation conditions where daily vibrations are assumed to be strongly felt based on floor use, the floor’s response acceleration shall be at or below the performance evaluation value W = 70–90 for steel directly applied floors specified in the “Guideline for Habitability Evaluation of Building Vibration (Architectural Institute of Japan Environmental Standard AIJES-V0001-2004, hereinafter referred to as the ‘Habitability Evaluation Guideline’).”

2. Consideration of Vibration Sources
The impact of vibration is reduced by securing distance from vibration sources and similar measures.

(Note)
Considering the activities conducted in the target rooms, if it is necessary to further reduce the response acceleration, additional technical provisions shall be established as required.

[Verification Method]
Compliance with the technical provisions shall be verified by calculation/analysis or by checking design documents and similar means.

Additionally, response acceleration should be verified when significant vibration sources are anticipated, particularly when structural factors such as floors prone to vibration are expected to impede achieving the target.

2) Performance concerning vibrations caused by traffic

[Basic Performance]
For rooms and similar spaces, under conditions where traffic-induced vibrations are likely to be perceived, the performance standard is that vibrations causing psychological or physiological discomfort do not occur, and this standard shall be ensured.

[Technical Requirements]
To ensure the performance level concerning vibrations caused by traffic, the following technical requirements shall be satisfied.

Target value for response acceleration:
Under excitation conditions where the impact of traffic-induced vibrations is assumed to be greatest, the response acceleration of vertical and horizontal floor vibrations shall be at or below the evaluation values for vertical vibration specified in the Residential Performance Evaluation Guidelines.

(2) Standards for the air environment when mechanical ventilation systems are installed

Under the Building Sanitation Act, a mechanical ventilation system refers to equipment that can purify air taken in from outside and supply it with controlled airflow. In other words, when functions other than temperature control and humidity control, which are functions of air-conditioning systems, are provided, it is necessary in occupied rooms to purify the air and supply it with controlled airflow so that it generally conforms to five items—A, B, C, E, and F—of the standards in the table below. In addition, efforts must be made to maintain and manage mechanical ventilation systems in accordance with the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare’s “Technical Standards for Maintenance, Management, and Cleaning of Air-Conditioning Systems, etc.”

- “Occupied room” has the same definition as in Article 2, Item 4 of the Building Standards Act, meaning a room used for residence, office work, work, assembly, entertainment, or other similar purposes.
- “Purification,” one of the functions provided by air-conditioning systems and mechanical ventilation systems, presupposes the ability to introduce outside air. Therefore, household room air conditioners without an outdoor air intake function are neither air-conditioning systems nor mechanical ventilation systems.
- An air-conditioning system does not qualify as such if it lacks any one of the four functions—purification, temperature control, humidity control, and airflow control. However, when these four functions are satisfied by multiple pieces of equipment, it is considered appropriate to regard them collectively as an air-conditioning system.

(3) Methods for Measuring Air Environment

Measurements are conducted during normal operating hours of specified buildings, on each floor, at a point located 75 cm to 150 cm above the floor in the central part of an occupied room, using the instruments listed in the table below.
For instruments (i) through (iv), it is acceptable to use instruments with performance equal to or superior to those specified.

Item | Instrument | Measurement frequency
a. Concentration of suspended particulate matter*1 | An instrument that measures suspended particulate matter with an approximate aerodynamic diameter of 10 micrometers or less by the gravimetric method using glass fiber filter paper (limited to those capable of collecting 99.9% or more of 0.3-micrometer stearic acid particles), or an instrument adopted as a standard by a registered measurement institution certified by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare | Once every two months or less
i. Carbon monoxide content*1 | Carbon monoxide detector using detector tube method
ii. Carbon dioxide content*1 | Carbon dioxide detector using detector tube method
iii. Temperature |

Item Measuring Instrument Number of Measurements
Thermometer with 0.5-degree graduations
O: Relative humidity Psychrometer with 0.5-degree graduations
Ka: Airflow An anemometer capable of measuring airflows of 0.2 m/s or higher —
Ki: Amount of formaldehyde Instruments that measure using the 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazine collection–high-performance liquid chromatography method, or the 4-amino-3-hydroxy-5-methyl-1,2,4-triazole method, as separately designated by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare*3 Once between June 1 and September 30 nearest to the time when new construction, expansion, major repairs, or major remodeling has been completed and use has begun

*1 The amounts of suspended particulate matter, carbon monoxide concentration, and carbon dioxide concentration are to be compared with the standards using the average value during the hours of daily use.
*2 Registered testing organization: Japan Building Maintenance and Management Education Center (Mita Branch Office) Here
*3 Formaldehyde measuring instruments designated by the Minister of Health, Labour and Welfare

Environmental Quality Standards for Air Pollution

1. Environmental Quality Standards for Air Pollution

Substance | Environmental Conditions (date of establishment) | Measurement Method

Sulfur dioxide (SO₂)
- The daily average of hourly values shall be 0.04 ppm or less, and the hourly values shall be 0.1 ppm or less. (Notified on May 16, 1973)
- Solution conductometry or ultraviolet fluorescence method

Carbon monoxide (CO)
- The daily average of hourly values shall be 10 ppm or less, and the 8-hour average of hourly values shall be 20 ppm or less. (Notified on May 8, 1973)
- Method using a non-dispersive infrared analyzer

Suspended particulate matter (SPM)
- The daily average of hourly values shall be 0.10 mg/m³ or less, and the hourly values shall be 0.20 mg/m³ or less. (Notified on May 8, 1973)
- Gravimetric concentration measurement by filter collection, or light scattering, piezoelectric balance, or beta-ray absorption methods that yield quantities linearly related to the concentration measured by the gravimetric method

4. Environmental Standards for Fine Particulate Matter (PM2.5)
Substance: Fine particulate matter (PM2.5)
Environmental conditions: The annual average must be 15 μg/m³ or less, and the daily average must be 35 μg/m³ or less. (Public Notice dated September 9, 2009)
Measurement method: A mass concentration measurement method using filter collection, or an automatic measuring device recognized as providing values equivalent to the mass concentration measured by this method.

5. Guidelines Related to Air Pollution
Guideline for atmospheric hydrocarbon concentration to prevent the formation of photochemical oxidants:
For a daily maximum 1-hour value of photochemical oxidants of 0.06 ppm, the 3-hour average value of non-methane hydrocarbons from 6:00 a.m. to 9:00 a.m. falls within the range of 0.20 ppmC to 0.31 ppmC. (Notice dated August 13, 1976)

(4) Proposed Revision of Carbon Monoxide Standard Values
Standard value / Special measures
Current: 10 ppm / When the carbon monoxide content in the atmosphere is generally in excess of approximately 10 ppm, 20 ppm
Proposed revision: 6 ppm / When the carbon monoxide content in the atmosphere is generally in excess of approximately 6 ppm, 12 ppm

3. About Carbon Dioxide

(1) Health effects of carbon dioxide (excerpted from Reference Material 3)

An association has been observed between increases in carbon dioxide concentration in the low concentration range around 1,000 ppm and physiological changes (such as carbon dioxide partial pressure and heart rate), as well as symptoms related to sick building syndrome (SBS). The physiological changes are considered attributable to carbon dioxide; however, further investigation is needed to determine whether the SBS symptoms are due to carbon dioxide itself or to combined exposure with other pollutants, particularly regarding the effects of long-term exposure. It is considered that keeping indoor carbon dioxide concentrations in buildings at low levels below 1,000 ppm can prevent these health effects. In recent years, potential impacts of low concentrations of carbon dioxide around 1,000 ppm on labor productivity (decision-making and problem-solving abilities) have been suggested, and further verification is required.

<>
Table 1: Summary of Carbon Dioxide Concentration and Effects on Health, etc.
CO₂ Concentration Physiological Changes Psychomotor Function Symptoms
500 ppm or higher pCO₂, heart rate, heart rate variability, blood pressure, peripheral blood circulation Sick building syndrome symptoms at 700 ppm or higher
1,000 or higher Cognitive function (decision making, problem solving) Worsening of reading symptoms in schoolchildren
5,000 or higher Increased respiratory rate, respiratory acidosis, metabolic changes (decreased blood Ca²⁺ and pH buffering capacity), increased cerebral blood flow, increased minute ventilation
10,000 or higher
50,000 or higher

4-3-7 Maintenance and Operation of Air-Conditioning Systems (Operational Guidelines)

To stably ensure performance related to the indoor air environment, maintain and manage air-conditioning systems (outside air intake mechanisms, filters, fans, ducts, control panels, sensors, etc.) according to the following guidelines. The contents stipulated here constitute an operational plan that takes into account the building’s uses, the characteristics of stored items, and seasonal fluctuations in visitor numbers, and are managed under a four-tier structure of daily inspections, periodic inspections, annual maintenance, and renewal planning. Individual thresholds for air quality monitoring items, including carbon dioxide (CO₂), are defined in the facility’s management plan in accordance with the separate “Monitoring Settings List.”

1) Categories and frequency of inspections and cleaning

Category: Daily inspection
- Scope: Differential pressure gauge of medium-efficiency filters; blockage at outdoor air intakes; abnormal noises/odors in mechanical rooms; BAS alarms
- Frequency: Daily (before opening/after closing)
- Performer: In-house facilities staff (on-site)
- Record medium: Inspection app (photo attachment enabled)

Category: Periodic inspection
- Scope: Fan vibration; belt tension; damper opening; duct air leakage; sanitary condition of drain pans
- Frequency: Once per month (biweekly during peak periods)
- Performer: Contracted vendor (HVAC maintenance)
- Record medium: Inspection report (stored as PDF)

Category: Annual maintenance
- Scope: Filter replacement; coil cleaning; control panel relay inspection; emergency stop circuit test
- Frequency: Once per year (conducted during closure period)
- Performer: Manufacturer-affiliated maintenance
- Record medium: Annual maintenance ledger

Category: Renewal planning
- Scope: [To be defined]
