negative_chunk_list.content:
Overview of Statistics Analysis Lab Inc.

1. Overview
(1) Purpose
Statistics Analysis Lab Inc. aims to contribute to ensuring the reliability of statistics and improving statistical techniques by integrally conducting tabulation of statistical surveys and research on statistical technologies necessary for this work.

(2) Scope of Operations
1. Conduct tabulation of statistical surveys.
2. Conduct tabulation of statistical surveys commissioned by national administrative agencies or local public bodies.
3. Accumulate, process, and otherwise handle information necessary for the production and use of statistics.
4. Conduct research on technologies necessary for the operations listed in the preceding items.
5. Conduct operations incidental to each of the operations listed above.

(3) History
1871 (Meiji 4): It is said that a Statistical Tables Section was established in the Grand Council of State’s Secretariat.
1885 (Meiji 18): The Statistics Bureau was established within the Cabinet.
1949 (Showa 24): With the establishment of the Prime Minister’s Office, it became the Tabulation Department of the Statistics Bureau of the Prime Minister’s Office.
1984 (Showa 59): With the establishment of the Management and Coordination Agency, the Tabulation Department of the Statistics Bureau became the Natural Statistics Center of the Management and Coordination Agency.
2001 (Heisei 13): With the reorganization of central government ministries and agencies, it became the Natural Statistics Center of the Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications.
2003 (Heisei 15): Newly launched as Statistics Analysis Lab Inc.

(4) Governing Law
Statistics Analysis Lab Inc. Act (Act No. 219 of 1999)

(5) Competent Minister (Responsible Department)
Minister for Internal Affairs and Communications (General Affairs Division, Statistics Bureau, Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications)

Status of inspection

A. Status of calculating research support funds
Method for calculating the amount of research support funds
Regarding the calculation of the amount of research support funds, the Secretariat of the Headquarters for the Promotion of Central Government Ministries and Agencies Reform presented in April 2000 the document “On the budgets, etc. of national corporations and their medium-term plans,” in which example rules were provided. It also stated that, in setting specific calculation rules, it is desirable to establish appropriate rules tailored to each corporation’s business activities and financial structure, while taking care to sufficiently allow for the autonomy of national corporations. In response, as noted above, each national corporation, in its medium-term plan, has established its own calculation rules—based on the example rules above—to be used when calculating the total amount of research support funds for the medium-term target period (hereinafter referred to as the “medium-term plan amount”) and has calculated the medium-term plan amount accordingly.

B. Status of self-generated income
National corporations, as financial resources for their operations, carry out their administrative and business activities using, in addition to research support funds, their own income, and for certain operations they also use subsidy income, commissioned income, and the like. Furthermore, in the aforementioned “On the budgets, etc. of national corporations and their medium-term plans,” it is stipulated that, when calculating the amount of research support funds, if own income is anticipated, that amount shall be deducted (hereinafter, own income subject to deduction is referred to as “deductible own income”).

Positioning of self-generated income and the concept of deductible self-generated income (expanded commentary)
Self-generated income is a funding source that a corporation can autonomously obtain, and it can encompass a wide variety of categories such as consideration for commissioned projects, fees for the use of facilities and systems, revenue from publications and data provision, tuition for education and training, charges associated with the dissemination of research outcomes, miscellaneous income (insurance proceeds, refunds, fees, etc.), and even investment income from short-term deposits with financial institutions. In calculating research support funds, it is common practice—so as to avoid double funding—to deduct in advance the self-generated income expected during the period; however, the scope of income subject to deduction is closely tied to the corporation’s business structure, pricing authority, marketability, and the timing of contract formation. For example, utilization fees for shared systems should be estimated with explicit reference to the fee schedule and the basis for projected usage (past access statistics, planned new deployments, suspension/upgrade schedules), and revenue from commissioned tabulation should preferably be risk-adjusted in light of the status of bidding or negotiated contracts, the degree of specification finalization, and the timing of receipt (advance payments, percentage-of-completion, post-acceptance). While prudence is required in estimation, overly conservative settings lead to inefficiencies—specifically, an inflated initial allocation of research support funds at the start of the fiscal year and the occurrence of surpluses at year-end—so it is advisable to optimize the balance between predictability and accuracy through in-year adjustments (increases or decreases) and updates to the following year’s estimation logic (demand elasticity, seasonality, impacts of regulatory changes, feasibility of price revisions).

Additionally, in actual operations, in addition to the current fiscal year’s research support funds received quarterly, they also managed unspent amounts of research support funds received in prior years. These unspent amounts from prior fiscal years arose when, in research and development outsourcing projects and the like, it became difficult to complete the research and development within the fiscal year, resulting in expenditures of research support funds falling below plan and the unspent amounts being carried over to the following year. As a result, because the interest rates, principal amounts, and other factors involved in actual operations substantially exceeded the interest rates, principal amounts, and other factors used to calculate deductible self-generated income, actual interest income—although it included a small portion arising from managing proceeds from the sale of research assets—amounted to over 388 million yen in FY2007, over 471.42 million yen in FY2008, and over 271.22 million yen in FY2009. Case 2: The Japan Oil, Gas and Metals National Corporation treats income from managing research support funds as deductible self-generated income. For the amounts, since research support funds are received quarterly, it assumes the funds are fully used within three months of disbursement, and that they are managed in an ordinary deposit account during this period, calculating the amounts using the interest rate on such deposits. It has recorded over 4.5 million yen for FY2007, over 15.4 million yen for FY2008, and over 25 million yen for FY2009.

Status of Research Support Funds at National Government-Related Corporations

3. Status of the Audit

(1) Status of the Disbursement and Calculation of Research Support Funds

A. Status of the Disbursement of Research Support Funds

The amounts of income and the disbursed research support funds for FY 2009 at the 83 corporations subject to the audit are shown in Table 2.

Table 2: Amounts of Income and Disbursed Research Support Funds for FY 2009 (Unit: million yen)

(Note) The income amount (A) and the research support grant amount (B) are totals compiled by this Office based on each corporation’s financial statements, specifically the total income and the amount of research support grants.

As shown, while some corporations have almost all of their operating funds coming from research support grants, others have subsidies, operating income, and other sources to fund their operations, resulting in a very low proportion of research support grants in their total income. Thus, the proportion of research support grants in the total income of state-run corporations varies widely. This is due to differences in the nature of operations and operational conditions of each corporation.

Information Security and Personal Data Protection (Extended): Statistical agencies, under controls based on laws and guidelines, establish: (1) authentication and authorization (ID management, role-based access, separation and auditing of privileged IDs); (2) network defense (segmentation, firewalls, IDS/IPS, application of zero trust); (3) endpoint management (MDM, anti-malware, patching, prohibition of removable media); (4) data protection (encryption, key management, DLP, data classification and lifecycle management); (5) logging and auditing (long-term retention, tamper resistance, correlation analysis); (6) vulnerability management (regular scanning, penetration testing, remediation management); (7) BCP/DR (redundant backups, exercises); (8) supplier management (SLAs, audit rights, security requirements); (9) education and training (annual, targeted training); and (10) incident response (CSIRT, initial response, containment, root cause analysis, recurrence prevention).

Handling of specific personal information and sensitive data must follow proper procedures for masking, pseudonymization, and anonymization, accompanied by utility assessments (measurement of statistical bias, re-identification risk evaluation). Standard features of analysis environments include network segregation, review prior to taking deliverables off the system, output restrictions (cell suppression, rounding), automatic metadata attachment, and accompanying audit logs. For cloud use, clearly define the shared responsibility model, region selection, key management (KMS/HSM), availability design, and measures to avoid vendor lock-in.

Status of Research Support Grant Disbursements in State-Owned Corporations (Wide-Angle Review)
In an ecosystem where many corporations coexist, differences in the nature of operations (infrastructure operations, R&D, international cooperation, outreach, data provision, etc.), market linkage (demand elasticity, pricing authority), regulatory frameworks (statutory delegated tasks, subsidy schemes, outsourcing rules), workforce composition (ratios of full-time, part-time, and outsourced personnel), and asset composition (depreciation burdens for system assets, facilities, and equipment) lead to variations in revenue structures. As a result, dependence on research support grants varies greatly by corporation: some exhibit a high ratio, while others show higher proportions of subsidies, commissioned projects, donations, or investment income, reflecting observed diversity.

Even within the statistical domain alone, revenue opportunities and constraints shift according to roles, such as: (i) corporations responsible for stable operation of shared-use systems, (ii) corporations whose primary duties are fieldwork and tabulation for large-scale surveys, (iii) corporations that strengthen analysis, research, and human resource development, and (iv) corporations tasked with overseas collaboration and technology transfer.

In the process of calculating and executing disbursement amounts, emphasis is placed on the reasonableness of self-generated income projections, the risk of commissioned projects materializing, agility in mid-year adjustments, appropriateness of year-end accounting treatments (criteria for settlement, refund, and carryover to the next fiscal year), and transparency of explanatory materials. Monitoring focuses on budget execution rate, bottlenecks causing execution delays, management of accounts receivable and advances received, vendor concentration, price reasonableness, and operational KPIs (number of transactions processed, utilization rate, lead time, incident response time, and user satisfaction).

History
- Internal organization belonging to the Statistics Bureau, etc. → July 1984: National Administrative Agency Natural Statistics Center →
- January 2001: Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications Statistics Center → April 2003: Statistics Analysis Lab, Inc.

Purpose
By integrally conducting tabulation of statistical surveys related to the natural environment and research on statistical techniques necessary for this, the purpose is to secure statistical information on the natural environment and contribute to the improvement of the natural environment.

Scope of Operations
1. To conduct tabulation of statistical information related to the natural environment.
2. To conduct tabulation of statistical information related to the natural environment under commission from national administrative organs or local public bodies.
3. To accumulate, process, and otherwise handle information necessary for the compilation and use of statistics.
4. To conduct research on technologies necessary for the operations listed in 1–3.
5. To conduct operations incidental to those listed in 1–4.

Financial and Budgetary Status
- Capital: None
- Gratuitous use of state-owned property: Yes

Overview of Initiatives
Toritō Prefecture began airborne laser surveying in FY2018, and data acquisition for all privately owned forests in the prefecture was completed in FY2024.

To understand the current state of forests, the prefecture is divided into five areas, and one area is photographed with aerial imagery each year.

These data are available free of charge to anyone. Please read the terms of use and the procedures for use carefully before making use of them.

Toritō Prefecture Forest-Related Open Data Terms of Use
Use of content
Unless otherwise noted, the Toritō Prefecture forest-related open data are subject to the Public Data License (Version 1.0) (PDL1.0) (https://www.digital.go.jp/resources/open_data/public_data_license_v1.0).

For information on items that vary by site—such as examples of source attribution specific to this content and content not subject to these rules—please refer to the “Important Information Regarding the Public Data License (Version 1.0)” related to the use of Toritō Prefecture forest-related open data.

Note that if users wish to use the data under ODC BY (https://opendatacommons.org/licenses/by/1-0/) or ODbL (https://opendatacommons.org/licenses/odbl/) by Open Data Commons, nothing herein prevents them from doing so.

Types of Open Data
Aerial Laser Survey Results and Aerial Photographs (Orthoimages)
Aerial laser survey results and aerial photographs (orthoimages) acquired and maintained by Tori East Prefecture are available. For the list of datasets and the years of measurement (photography), please refer to “List of Aerial Laser Survey Results and Aerial Photographs (Orthoimages) and Measurement Years.”

Sources
- DEM data and contour data for area (5) in “List of Aerial Laser Survey Results and Aerial Photographs (Orthoimages) and Measurement Years, Appendix 1: Implementation Status of Aerial Laser Surveys.”
  These survey results were obtained using survey results under the jurisdiction of the Geospatial Information Authority of Japan with the approval of the Director General of the GSI. (Approval No.) Reiwa 6 Chuko No. 450
- DEM data, contour data, terrain analysis results, and forest resource analysis results for Hoki Town
  Source: Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism, Chugoku Regional Development Bureau, Hinogawa River Office survey results

- DCHM (Digital Canopy Height Model), forest type map data (species polygons), and forest resource aggregation meshes for Mochigase, Tori East City
  Source: Tori East City, Department of Agriculture, Forestry and Fisheries, Forestry and Fisheries Division analysis results

- DEM data, micro-topography maps (CS stereograms), DCHM (Digital Canopy Height Model), forest type map data (species polygons), and forest resource aggregation meshes for Yazu Town
  Source: Yazu Town Industry and Tourism Division survey results

- Forest type identification map [XYZ tiles]
  Source: Tori East Prefecture, Forest Type Identification Map (processed by the Forestry Agency)

Basic Forest Map
A 1:5,000-scale topographic map created using photogrammetric results from aerial photographs. It is produced for the entire prefecture.

Forest Register and Forest Management Plan Map
Forest registers and forest management plan maps that do not include personal information (names and addresses of forest owners) are scheduled to be published on the G Spatial Information Center website in FY 2025.

For issuance procedures, please refer to Issuance of Forest Registers and Forest Management Plan Maps.

Procedures related to use
How to apply
Some open data require an application procedure, while others do not. Please review the Terms of Use and the Usage Procedure Flowchart and complete any necessary procedures appropriately.

Application forms for the use of public survey results
- Application for Approval to Reproduce Survey Results (Form No. 1) (docx: 28 KB)
- Application for Approval to Use Survey Results (Form No. 2) (docx: 37 KB)

For details on procedures related to the use of public survey results, please refer to the website of the Geospatial Information Authority of Japan.

2. SGH Global Leader Development Program: “UNESCO San’in Kaigan Geopark and Kobe”
Collaborative research (Mr. Ieyasu Takimoto: Teacher, Kobe University Secondary School; Dr. Eiichi Sato: Kobe University)

On November 17, 2015, at the 38th UNESCO General Conference held at UNESCO Headquarters in France, the activities of the Global Geoparks Network, which had previously been conducted as a UNESCO-supported initiative, were formally adopted as the “International Geoscience and Geoparks Programme,” a UNESCO official program on par with World Heritage. Against this backdrop, we study the distinctive natural features of Kobe alongside the valuable natural environment and culture of the San’in Kaigan Geopark.

In the current fiscal year, on September 16–17, 2017 (Sat–Sun), at Kobe University, we presented a research report at the Japan Society for Geoscience Education on disaster prevention learning practices through the creation of topographic models using transparent plastic container lids. In addition, on Sunday, February 11, 2018, when six students and teachers from the Disaster Science Department of Miyagi Prefectural Tagajo High School visited the Disaster Reduction and Human Renovation Institution, we held a disaster-prevention lecture and exchanged views on methods of communicating about local communities and disasters.

(2) Status of External Research Funds
1) Status of external research funds obtained in FY 2017
Geographical Survey Institute Research Grant, “Research theme: Development of geography education methods to convey the Great Hanshin-Awaji Earthquake using GIS,” Principal Investigator: Sotaro Tsuboi, solo, ¥300,000.
Obayashi Foundation Research Grant, “Research theme: A study on changes in the social, economic, and environmental regional structure in the process of urbanization in China,” Principal Investigator: Sotaro Tsuboi, solo, ¥950,000.

2) Contributions toward obtaining external research funds
- Not applicable

(3) Dissemination of Academic Research Results, etc.
1) Peer-reviewed academic papers
- Sotaro Tsuboi (2017): A study on disaster response in Nishihara Village during the Kumamoto Earthquake and on disaster victims’ sense of recovery in daily life and health assessment, Transactions on Environmental Information Science 31 (Center for Environmental Information Science), pp. 77–82.
- Sotaro Tsuboi (2017): A study on regional characteristics of flood disasters in Japan since the 2000s, Journal of Water Resources and Environment Vol. 30, No. 2 (Japan Society of Water Resources and Environment), pp. 78–84.
- Sotaro Tsuboi (2017): Technical support for creating evacuee status maps in disaster response headquarters and management-by-objectives response — A case study of the Disaster Response Headquarters of Mashiki Town, Kumamoto Prefecture —, Architectural Institute of Japan, Information Systems Technology Committee.

40th Information, Systems, and Technology Symposium 2017 (Paper R04), pp. 147–152.
• Ryoga Ishihara, Sotaro Tsuboi, and Kiyomine Terumoto (2017): Examination of strategies for transporting seriously injured persons in the event of a Nankai Trough megathrust earthquake, Journal of JSCE, D3 (Infrastructure Planning and Management), Vol. 73, No. 5 (Japan Society of Civil Engineers), pp. 68–77.
• Yuko Araki, Masayuki Udagawa, Yosuke Takada, Sotaro Tsuboi, and Akihiko Hokugo (2017): A study on responses when evacuees emerge outside designated evacuation sites: A case study of Mashiki Town during the 2016 Kumamoto Earthquake, Journal of Social Safety Science, No. 31, pp. 167–175.
• Yuko Araki, Sotaro Tsuboi, and Akihiko Hokugo (2017): A study on the occurrence tendencies of non-designated evacuation shelters after tsunami disasters: A case study of Kamaishi City during the Great East Japan Earthquake, Journal of Architecture and Planning (Transactions of AIJ), 82-741, pp. 2885–2895.

[2]
- In the new Courses of Study to be implemented from fiscal 2020, fostering creators of a “sustainable society” is emphasized, and new content such as the SDGs and globalization has been added. In this context, the Agency held seminars for textbook publishers and other events to strengthen information dissemination. As a result, interviews and contributed articles by seven Agency-affiliated individuals are scheduled to be published, and the Agency also provided information for four teaching materials and secured publication in seven educational journals. In response to growing needs in school education, it produced a video teaching material that enables teachers without experience in developing regions to easily engage in development education and international understanding education (a water-themed material filmed on location in Rwanda) and released it on YouTube.
- The Agency continued to promote collaboration with the Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT) and related organizations, and actively pursued initiatives such as various meetings and seminars related to Super Global High Schools (SGH) and their successor programs, as well as Education for Sustainable Development (ESD). At JICA Hokuriku, the Agency led a session at the Japan Society for School Education, presenting the Agency’s initiatives alongside practical lesson examples from participants in overseas teacher training.

[3]
- As initiatives tailored to the characteristics and needs of each region, JICA Hokkaido (Obihiro) implemented school visits by trainees in approximately three-quarters of its training courses, leveraging trainee cooperation to suit the characteristics of the eastern Hokkaido region, where there are relatively few foreign residents. These school visits contributed not only to development education but also significantly to trainees’ understanding of Japan and the promotion of favorable feelings toward the country. In response to the Tokyo Olympic and Paralympic Games and the increase in foreign visitors to Japan, JICA Nihonmatsu, at the request of the Fukushima Prefectural Police, provided lectures on overseas affairs and intercultural understanding workshops for 88 police officers. JICA Komagane supported exchange programs between Komagane City and its international friendship city, Pokhara Lekhnath (Nepal), by dispatching Nepali language instructors from the training center for activities targeting junior and senior high school students and Komagane residents. In addition, for elementary school teachers who participated in the FY2018 Overseas Training for Teachers (Nepal dispatch), the Komagane Training Center expanded the program’s outcomes by offering trainee-led school visits, student visits to the training center, and online exchanges between schools in Pokhara Lekhnath (Nepal) and Japanese schools through Japan Overseas Cooperation Volunteers.
- In response to growing demand for promoting understanding of the SDGs, each center has provided meticulous support even for groups engaging for the first time in development education and international understanding education. For example, at JICA Hokuriku, staff have supported teachers with no overseas experience or no prior practice in international understanding classes...

Since the establishment of the Sharing Economy Association in 2016, both the public and private sectors have promoted cities that leverage sharing economy services to address regional challenges, referring to them as “Sharing Cities.” Japan, which is entering a full-fledged period of population decline ahead of the rest of the world, faces various issues such as aging and shrinking populations in local governments and financial difficulties, and new mechanisms are needed to realize a sustainable society. There are not enough resources to solve all current problems solely through public services.

Therefore, by introducing the concept of “sharing,” we aim to supplement public assistance with mutual aid and realize sustainable municipalities—an initiative we call a “Sharing City.” Many such initiatives have already been undertaken overseas, in places like Seoul and Amsterdam. In Japan as well, by fiscal 2019, more than 100 diverse cases had been created, ranging from ordinance-designated cities to small villages. To implement the sharing economy as true infrastructure that supports society and move to the next stage, the Sharing City Promotion Council was established on July 14, 2020.

Chapter 2 User Registration
Article 3 (Conditions for Registration)
1. Anyone wishing to use the service must agree to these Terms and apply for user registration by the specified method.
2. A person wishing to use the service cannot register if any of the following applies:
   (1) They are younger than junior high school age (junior high school students require parental/guardian consent).
   (2) They are deemed unable to safely operate a bicycle.
   (3) They are deemed to be a member of, affiliated with, or otherwise belong to an organized crime group or other anti-social force.
   (4) They do not agree to these Terms.
   (5) The Ward/Section Chief otherwise deems registration inappropriate.
3. Only individuals who have completed user registration may use the rental bicycles. However, for tandem bicycles, it is sufficient if at least one of the riders has completed user registration.

Article 4 (Registration Method)
1. Fill out the application form with the required information and, for the first time only, present documents that can verify your identity, such as your name and current address.
2. After the application is accepted, the user’s own transit IC card or similar will be registered as the registration card.
3. When using a rental bicycle, choose one method of use: same-day use or periodic (subscription) use.

Article 5 (Changes to Registration Information)
If there are any changes to the user’s name, address, phone number, or other registered details, the user must notify the office of the changes within 14 days from the date of the change.

The Ward shall bear no responsibility if the user suffers any disadvantage as a result of failing to complete the change procedures.
Article 6 (Termination of the Service Agreement)
If the user falls under any of the following items, the Ward may, without prior notice, temporarily suspend use or cancel the user registration:
(1) When the user violates these Terms.
(2) When the user becomes unreachable, or when false information or a material error is found in the information provided at the time of registration, or when continued use is deemed inappropriate.
(3) When the user falls under Article 3, Paragraph 2.
Article 7 (Loss, Damage, and Theft)
1. In the event of loss, damage, or theft of the registration card, the user shall contact the Ward or the port and undergo processing to suspend the use of the relevant registration card in order to prevent unauthorized use.
2. In the event of loss, damage, or theft of the registration card, the user shall apply at the port to newly register the user’s own card.
3. If a third party uses the card without notice being given, or during the period after notice is given until the suspension takes effect, and the Ward suffers damage as a result, the holder of the relevant registration card (or the guardian in the case of a junior high school student) shall bear the amount of the damage.

Chapter 6 Fees and Charges
Article 16 (Payment of Fees)
Users shall pay the fees specified in Supplementary Table 3 (related to Article 13) of the Kakeshi City Bicycle Parking Ordinance Enforcement Regulations.

Article 17 (Excess Charges)
1. If the bicycle is used beyond the permitted period, the user shall pay an amount calculated by multiplying the number of excess days by the same-day usage fee.
2. The “usage period” in the preceding paragraph shall be 24 hours for same-day use, and the number of months of use for periodic use.

Chapter 7 Refund of Fees
Article 18 (Refunds)
Fees already paid for the use of rental bicycles are not refundable. However, if any of the following applies, all or part of the fee may be refunded. In such cases, a refund application must be submitted in the manner prescribed by the Port.
(1) If notice is given of cancellation before the start of the periodic usage period. (Full amount)
(2) If use is discontinued during the periodic usage period. (The amount for the months from the month following the month in which usage approval was discontinued)

Chapter 8 User Responsibilities
Article 19 (Pre-use Inspection)
1. When using a rental bicycle, the user must each time confirm that it is in a condition safe for use, including the effectiveness of the brakes, whether the handlebars are bent, and the tire air pressure, before using it.

2. If the user discovers any damage to the rental bicycle, any loss of equipment, or any defects, they must immediately contact the staff at the port, return the bicycle, and refrain from continuing to use it.
Article 20 (Duty of Care)
1. The user shall use the rental bicycle with the duty of care of a prudent manager.
2. The user’s duty of care in the preceding paragraph applies from the completion of the rental bicycle checkout procedure until the completion of the return procedure.
Article 21 (Prohibited Acts)
1. The user must not engage in any of the following acts. If any of the following apply, or are deemed highly likely to apply, the rental may be terminated, the approval for use may be revoked, or use may be suspended or restricted.
(1) Willfully damaging or destroying the rental bicycle.
(2) Subleasing the rental bicycle or the registration certificate.
(3) Abandoning the rental bicycle in public places, etc.
(4) Modifying or altering the bicycle’s frame, equipment, or other components.
(5) Reckless driving, driving under the influence of alcohol, or other dangerous acts.
(6) Other acts that violate laws or public order and morals.
2. In addition to the preceding items, when the Ward Mayor deems it particularly necessary.

1. “The Year of AI in Higashikawara Ward” In December 2016, following the promulgation of the Basic Act on the Advancement of Public and Private Sector Data Utilization, Higashikawara Ward decided, in revising the Higashikawara Ward Informatization Plan, to position the new plan as a municipal Public-Private Data Utilization Promotion Plan based on the Act. Our ward’s population is increasing rapidly by about 5,000 people each year, and we believe enhancing resident services is an urgent priority. At the same time, it is important to prepare for the changes brought about by advances in ICT. Therefore, this time we decided to revise the informatization plan to ensure it is appropriate, so that we can take proactive and strategic measures that anticipate future challenges. In conducting the revision, we coordinated with the national government regarding the Basic Act on the Advancement of Public and Private Sector Data Utilization, which currently serves as the national basic strategy, and confirmed its content and positioning. As a result, we formulated the informatization plan this March, positioning it as a municipal Public-Private Data Utilization Promotion Plan. From the perspective of public-private data, we began publishing open data in June 2016 and currently publish 95 datasets. We will continue to increase the number of datasets released and promote data utilization centered on open data. In addition, since last fiscal year we have been focusing on the use of ICT such as AI technologies in resident services and on creating a “workplace that is easy to work in” through ICT. “Creating a workplace that is easy to work in” means leveraging ICT to streamline operations, promote work-life balance, and, from that standpoint, devote more energy to resident services.

In the Tokogawara Ward’s informatization plan, we have set forth a future vision in which “ward residents, the ward administration, educational institutions, and businesses join forces to realize cutting-edge municipal ICT utilization, enabling every resident to routinely receive friendly ward services—anyone, anywhere, anytime—safely and to the fullest extent through ICT.” To realize this vision, we reviewed existing plans and systematized various measures according to four perspectives: (1) achieving a community-based, inclusive society through advanced ICT via collaboration that leverages the “four strengths” (government, residents, private sector, and regions nationwide); (2) delivering resident services that are user-friendly through ICT; (3) introducing the latest and robust information infrastructure to support efficient ward administration; and (4) ensuring and further strengthening resilient information security that also prepares for new threats to protect vital information. In formulating the plan, a cross-departmental committee conducted deliberations, and the Information Policy Division coordinated individual measures—including projects of each responsible department—to compile the plan for the entire ward.

2. AI Chatbot Service (1) Background and Purpose of Development
Approximately 20,000 foreign nationals from about 140 countries reside in Tokogawara Ward, and more than half of the embassies located in Japan are situated in our ward. In municipalities like ours with many foreign residents, providing accurate, needs-based information is one of the most important resident services. Accordingly, our ward has advanced information provision through diverse channels, including our website, public relations bulletin, and email newsletters, and since last year, we have also begun disseminating information via Facebook. In the broader context of promoting AI utilization across the entire ward government and to further improve resident services, we turned our attention to AI chatbot services and decided to consider their introduction. Thus, our ward conducted a proof-of-concept for the introduction of an AI chatbot service and, after confirming its effectiveness, adopted a process to decide on implementation (Figure 1).

“Traffic control” refers to actions aimed at processing the flow of vehicles—and the traffic stream as their aggregate—smoothly and safely. Methods of traffic control include traffic regulations such as one-way restrictions and prohibitions on turning right or left, improvements like installing medians and marking directions of travel on the roadway, which legally or physically control traffic flow, as well as the use of traffic signals. The former, implemented regardless of time of day, is called static control, while the latter, which changes according to time of day and traffic conditions, is called dynamic control. In particular, when traffic signals are used, methods have been put into practical use that detect traffic conditions at each moment and determine the optimal control accordingly, as well as methods that coordinate multiple traffic signals to enable vehicles to flow smoothly. Moreover, on urban expressways, where control by traffic signals is not possible, “ramp metering” is implemented to control the volume of vehicles entering at on-ramps when congestion occurs. Traffic control is indispensable for achieving safe and efficient traffic flow, and research is being conducted on methods for determining optimal control strategies and predicting the effects of their implementation.

We are holding the Yamanashi Public Transportation Festival! This PR event, the “Yamanashi Public Transportation Festival,” is being held to encourage residents to take an interest in public transportation such as buses, taxis, and railways, and to promote wider use.

At the venue, we will display buses and taxis you often see around town, as well as vehicles that are rarely seen up close. We will also have many family-friendly areas, including a mini-bus service, a bus riding lesson, a barrier-free accessibility class, and activities like coloring and crafts. In addition, we have prepared events that enthusiasts will enjoy, such as the sale of bus-related merchandise.

The Mystery Tour is a bus tour around the prefecture with a secret destination.

Why not take this opportunity to think about familiar public transportation—buses, taxis, and railways—while having fun with your family?

Here are highlights from past events.

Date and time
Saturday, January 25, 2025, 10:00 a.m.–3:00 p.m.
The event may be canceled due to weather or other circumstances.
If canceled, we will announce it on our website.

Location
Kofu Station North Exit: Assist Engineering Yocchabare Plaza, Kofu Station North Exit pedestrian deck, etc.

Planned event contents

The 12th Future Urban Technology Expo was bustling with visitors again this year. More than 100 companies and organizations participated at the venue, the Future Convention Hall, showcasing their latest technologies and services at their respective booths.

Right near the entrance, booths related to smart cities were lined up. The one drawing the most attention featured an AI-based traffic control system aimed at optimizing urban transportation. A large screen displayed a simulation of a realistic city, and visitors could use tablets to experience how changes in traffic volume affected the movement of the entire city. People of all ages, from children to adults, were engrossed as they explored future urban design.

In the back area, the “Future Living” zone stretched out. There, companies exhibiting home robots and next-generation energy management systems gathered, allowing visitors to actually converse with robots and interact with panels that visually explained energy consumption. At a booth for caregiver-support robots, an elderly couple asked the staff many questions. The wife’s comment left an impression: “If we had a robot like this at home, I feel we could live with peace of mind.”

At the center of the venue, there was also an area for student group exhibits, where university and technical college research teams showcased autonomous mobile robots, environmental monitoring systems, and more. Many visitors were impressed by the students’ passion and technical skill; some company representatives were even seen handing over business cards and saying they wanted to support them.

In one corner of the exhibition area, visitor-participation workshops were held, featuring hands-on programs like “Build Your Own Smart Sensor” and “Let AI Draw.” These activities proved very popular. The sight of children smiling as they took their creations home brought a warm atmosphere to the entire venue.

A fair (English: fair; archaic spellings include faire and fayre) is an event where people gather for various entertainments and commercial activities. Typically, a fair is a temporary event, ranging from something held only on a single afternoon to one that lasts for several weeks. Japanese concepts equivalent to the English “fair” include ichi (market), mihonichi (trade fair), and tenji-sokubai-kai (exhibition and sale).

History
Village Market by Gillis Mostaert, 1590. Fairs sometimes include the exhibition of animals, and owners groom them carefully before they are judged. The Horse Fair by Rosa Bonheur, 1852–1855. In the Roman provinces of Judea to Syria Palaestina, rabbis forbade Jews from attending fairs in certain towns, deeming them contrary to Jewish teachings. In medieval Europe, numerous fairs developed as temporary markets. Since wholesalers would sometimes travel for days to fairs where they were confident of finding trading partners, fairs played an especially important role in long-distance commerce and international trade. Such fairs were typically held in association with Christian special observances, such as the feast days of local churches’ patron saints. Records indicate that at Stagshaw in England, an annual fair, primarily for the sale of animals, had been held since 1293. In addition to a large fair held on July 4, the town also hosted various smaller fairs throughout the year that dealt exclusively with specific animals, such as horses, lambs, and ewes.

Namina City is a highland city rich in tourism resources that spreads across central Kaede Prefecture. It was incorporated as a city in 1964 and is now known as one of the nation’s premier resorts, home to villa areas such as the Ginsugi Plateau and Laurel Terrace. At the same time, it has a thriving manufacturing sector for precision instruments and optical components, boasting the sixth-highest industrial shipment value among the prefecture’s 85 municipalities. It is also a core city within the Aoboshi Wide-Area Union. This entry also covers its pre-incorporation name, Aoboshi Town.

Geographically, the urban area extends across a basin nestled between the Amagiri and Hekirei mountain ranges, with elevations ranging from 700 to 1,300 meters. The Aoboshi City Hall (812 m) is among the highest-elevation city halls in the country, and the city’s rail gateways, Aoboshi Station (798.5 m) and Aoba Station (872.2 m), are also situated at high elevations. The highest point is Mount Kirikan (2,912 m), a popular destination for hiking and stargazing. The Seiryu and Hoshimi rivers flow through the city, and notable lakes include Lake Hekikyo (1,384 m), Lake Tsukikage (1,145 m), and Jade Pond (1,278 m). In addition, the Ginsugi Plateau area hosts clusters of foothill pension villages around 1,600 meters in elevation, serving as summer retreats and winter sports bases.

The climate is classified as an inland subarctic humid climate. The area has a high rate of clear days, and in winter radiative cooling can drive early morning temperatures below −15°C, though snowfall is relatively light. Summers are cooler than in the lowlands, but days exceeding 30°C are not uncommon. On the high-elevation Ginsugi Plateau, midwinter temperatures can drop below −25°C, and the otherworldly landscapes formed by frozen lake surfaces and rime ice are a major draw.

Age restrictions for mixed bathing

From October 1, 2024, the age limit for mixed bathing in public bathhouses in Nagano Prefecture will be lowered from 10 and older to 7 and older.

Facility overview

This was the first of the six hot spring facilities in Rouna City to be built. It opened in June 1990 and has been used as a welfare-shared hot spring.

It is a complex consisting of a “hot spring facility (bathhouse, sauna, etc.)” and an “indoor hot spring pool,” utilizing Uehara Onsen.

Since September 2006, the adjacent “Comprehensive Welfare Center for Workers” has been renamed the “Quarand Culture Center,” becoming an integrated facility with “Quarand Rouna.”

negative_chunk_list.content: Here, we organize the institutional and cost-related considerations for typical retail and food service stores when introducing cashless payments. First, for card-based methods, costs can include a combination of merchant discount fees (a percentage of sales), terminal rental fees, monthly base fees for payment processing, bank transfer fees, and early payout option fees. In contrast, for QR-based methods, incentives and fee discounts during promotional periods can be a motivator for adoption, but standard rates apply in steady-state periods, and additional costs may arise for integrations with accounting software, ordering systems, and inventory systems. On the institutional side, key issues include compliance with the invoice system, interfaces with tax-free sales systems and registers compatible with reduced tax rates, the Electronic Book Preservation Act and digitization of receipts, and internal control procedures for cash register closing and daily reports. As for subsidies and support measures, local governments and business associations often offer programs aimed at terminal deployment and DX promotion, and peripheral costs such as receipt printers, barcode scanners, access points, back-office PCs, and power/cabling work may also be eligible. In practical adoption decisions, considerations include: (1) checkout processing speed during peak times (payment authorization latency and fallback procedures in case of payment failure); (2) compatibility between average ticket size and payment fees (fixed cost burden for small transactions); (3) customer preferences (age demographics, ratio of tourists, and app penetration among local residents); (4) burdens of cash handling (change preparation, currency exchange fees, security); (5) operations for returns, voids, split payments, tips, etc.; (6) staff training and key-person dependency risk; and (7) rules for continued operation during power outages or network failures.

In cashless operations, keeping a practical “catalog of issues” around the checkout counter helps in understanding the store’s burden and sense of implementation costs. Representative examples include: (1) failures in initial terminal setup (Wi‑Fi limited to 5 GHz/locked to 2.4 GHz, incorrect WPA settings, router MAC address filtering, remnants of manual proxy settings), (2) receipt printer paper jams, faded printing, and cutter wear, (3) unstable Bluetooth connections and unpaired devices, (4) halted checkout due to forgotten charging or battery degradation, (5) weak mobile signal or authorization delays during network congestion, (6) operational errors caused by UI changes during app updates, (7) insufficient training on return/void workflows, (8) risk threshold configuration for offline approvals, (9) discrepancies in monthly closing processes involving sales deductions and reconciliation of advances, and (10) poor integration with peripherals (barcode scanners/cash drawers). In addition, when multiple entries into POS, order entry, and payment apps occur, they can cause errors and checkout delays, prompting consideration of integrated or API-based connections. From a security standpoint, daily control activities are critical, such as preventing device removal, misuse, and phishing-like fake support calls; handling of tokens and transaction IDs printed on receipts; and shift-based access control (refund/void permissions). Because of these “hassles,” it is natural that a certain number of small stores are cautious about adoption. However, considering customer needs and other factors, such implementations have effectively become nearly mandatory in the current environment.

Here, we sketch, in a case-style manner, common motivations for adoption and non-adoption observed in other regions and shopping districts. For example, in tourist-oriented shopping streets, more stores install both card-based and QR-based systems to provide multilingual payment UIs and improve approval rates for cards issued overseas. Conversely, in community-focused neighborhood shopping streets, where the proportion of regular customers is high and cash-based operations suffice, a cautious stance may be taken due to implementation and learning costs. In areas where sales are driven by seasonal events, a practice of renting temporary mobile terminals only during event periods is adopted, reverting to cash-centric operations during normal times. In sectors with relatively low average tickets, such as prepared foods, bakeries, and sweets, the absolute amount of fees is scrutinized for its impact on profit margins, and hybrid operations may be employed, such as allowing cards only above a certain amount or allowing only QR payments. In crafts and artisan-oriented businesses, because of made-to-order production and holds, transactions split into deposits, down payments, and balances occur, necessitating an operations design that combines cash and electronic payments. These are general trends observed nationwide and are not grounds for judging the suitability of any specific shopping street or store. Decisions on whether to adopt or not are determined by a combination of factors, including customer mix, average transaction value, peak crowding, inventory turnover, return rates, compatibility with external malls/EC, accounting staff, security policies, potential use of subsidies, and the depreciation period of existing POS assets. Therefore, some stores and companies are not very proactive about adopting cashless payments, and it is hoped that governments will provide support for such implementations.

| Topic | Objective | Standard Procedure (Excerpt) | Failure Examples/Risks | Checklist (Operational Indicators) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Pre-deployment Training Plan | Prevent operator dependency | Role-play in the order: basic terminal operations → returns/cancellations → daily closing → fallback during outages | Verbal explanations only / Paper manuals are outdated / Unrehearsed unexpected scenarios | Training completion records / Update history of expected Q&A / Monthly refresher training |
| Pre-opening Preparation | Reduce approval delays right after opening | Ensure terminal battery >80%, paper roll stock, connection test, brand logo display check | Low battery / Out of paper roll / Unstable Wi-Fi | Connection OK/NG log / Remaining supply count / Photo record of posted materials |
| Standardizing Checkout Flow | Avoid checkout congestion | Standardize: finalize amount → present payment method → authorization → receipt check → delivery | Incorrect amount entry / Wrong type (QR/card) | Average processing seconds / Number of amount corrections / Cancellation rate |
| Returns/Cancellations | Minimize trouble | Executable only by authorized personnel, reason code required, physical approval stamp | Unauthorized cancellation / Confusion between cash refund and electronic refund | Distribution of return reasons / Count by staff member / Consistency of daily reports |
| Shift Handover | Ensure continuity of audit trail | Cash register closing → terminal sales report → handover memo | Double-posted sales / Unclosed register | Achievement rate of handover checklist items / Discrepancy amount |
| Invoice Compliance | Legal compliance | Print qualified invoice number on receipt, store electronic copy | Number not printed / Defects upon reissue | Defect rate / Number of reissues |

| Risk | Typical Scenario | Preventive Measures | Detection and Response | Notes |
| ----- | ---------------- | ------------------- | ---------------------- | ----- |
| Device loss/theft | Device left unlocked/taken after closing | Physical device locks, MDM, remote wipe | Immediate loss reporting, account suspension | Device ledger and roll call |
| Fake support scam | Phone call posing as “support” prompts setting changes | Centralize official contact, callback-by-default policy | Call logs, change log audits | Measures to prevent store number leakage |
| Refund abuse | Demands cash refund, double refund | Separation of duties, refund reason codes, daily report audits | Match transaction IDs, review surveillance footage | Manager approval for high-value refunds |
| Chargeback | Disputed later even after verified | Prioritize EMV/PIN, retain signatures | Evidence submission template | Operational small-amount threshold |
| Offline approval misuse | Exceeds limits under pretext of line outage | Set low offline thresholds | Immediate reconciliation after coming back online | Maintain backup line |
| Phishing | Fake UI lures ID entry | Use bookmarks only | Two-factor authentication, device binding | Training on screen capture recognition |
| Wi-Fi intrusion | Insufficient encryption | VLAN separation, strong WPA | Monitor IDS/IPS logs | Separate guest and business APs |
| Shoulder surfing | PIN peeking | Privacy guards, angle adjustment | Monitoring and intervention | Optimize counter layout |
| Device tampering | Attaching external devices | Visual inspection, tamper-evident seals | Keep inspection records and images | Detect during pre-opening inspections |
| Data exfiltration | Screenshots, photos | Storage encryption, no-photography policy | Patrol inspections | Training and written pledges |

| Integration Target | Purpose            | Integration Method            | Operational Considerations              | Failure Example / Alternative |
| ------------------ | ------------------ | ----------------------------- | --------------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| POS Core           | Eliminate double entry | API/middleware integration     | Alignment of tax categories and department codes | Combine manual input with nightly batch consolidation |
| Inventory Management | Immediate reflection of sales | SKU mapping                   | Set sales and processing shrinkage      | Separate sales units and purchase units |
| Accounting Software | Automate journal entries | Decomposition of consolidated transfers | Handling of fee accounts and advances   | Manage separately with sub-accounts |
| EC Integration     | Omnichannel         | Order → in-store pickup        | Returns for pre-paid reservations       | Consolidate accounting at in-store handoff |
| Handheld Terminals | Improve turnover     | Handheld → POS → Payment       | Delays under network congestion         | Prioritize kitchen printers |
| Barcode Operations | Reduce errors        | Maintain JAN/PLU               | Assign codes to in-house products       | Use simple PLU plus scale in combination |
| Unified Slip Numbers | Traceability       | Mapping table of Transaction ID × Slip ID | Linking cancellations and reissues      | Fix sequential numbers within the day |
| Reservations / Holds | Split transactions | Deposits and remaining balance | Handling cancellations with earnest money | Separate payment lane for deposits |
| Electronic Receipts | Cost reduction      | Distribution via email/QR      | Consent for personal information        | Clearly present paper/electronic options |
| KPI Development    | Improvement PDCA    | Accounting seconds / cancellation rate | Staffing during peak hours              | Dashboard visualization |

Marketing/CRM Utilization: Insights from Payment Data and Activation Design

| Data Perspective | Key Metrics          | Example Initiatives         | Cautions                   | Example Outcomes (Type) |
| ---------------- | -------------------- | --------------------------- | -------------------------- | ----------------------- |
| Visit Frequency  | Monthly visit count  | Stamps/visit passes         | Excessive notifications cause churn | Increased repeat rate   |
| Average Spend    | Average spend per visit | Set/menu bundling proposals | Avoid a hard-sell impression | Improved gross margin   |
| Time of Day      | Sales ratio by time slot | Time-limited coupons       | Side effect: longer lines  | Smoothed congestion     |
| Payment Method Trend | Card/QR ratio     | Cashback-linked campaigns   | Beware of bias             | Higher payment success rate |
| Churn Signals    | Dormancy detection   | Win-back coupons            | Coupon dependency          | Reactivation of dormant users |
| Cross-sell       | Basket co-purchase analysis | Bundled add-on suggestions | Inventory assurance        | Higher average ticket   |
| New vs. Existing | Ratio analysis       | Guidance for new customers  | Avoid neglecting existing customers | New customer acquisition |
| Review Promotion | NPS/Reviews          | Receipt QR-based prompts    | Adhere to stealth marketing policies | Increased reviews      |
| Tourism/Local    | Language/payment preferences | Multilingual displays  | Avoid over-multilingualization | Higher tourist sales    |
| Price Sensitivity| Elasticity estimation | Price testing               | Concern over perceived quality drop | Margin optimization     |

negative_chunk_list.content:
In-House Communication Activation Project

Purpose: Strengthen cross-departmental collaboration and promote information sharing
Background: With the recent increase in remote work, opportunities for serendipitous communication have decreased. As a result, delays in interdepartmental information sharing have been observed, creating bottlenecks in operations. Employee surveys also indicate that insufficient communication is a major factor contributing to decreased productivity.
Initiatives:
1. Introduce an internal SNS tool: Promote real-time information sharing and informal communication.
2. Establish cross-functional project teams: Regularly conduct short-term projects where employees from multiple departments participate around specific themes.
3. Hold regular online social events: Create opportunities for employee interaction outside of work.
Expected Effects:
- Faster information sharing: Reduce average response time by 20%.
- Alleviate employee isolation: Improve survey-based stress levels by 15%.
- Promote innovation: Increase the number of new ideas generated.

Future Challenges:
- Conduct training to improve employees’ IT literacy.
- Monitor tool usage and consider measures to boost engagement.

Office Environment Improvement Plan 2024

Purpose: Improve the quality of employees’ working environment and boost productivity
Background: The current office environment is becoming cramped due to the increase in the number of employees, and there is a shortage of spaces where people can focus on their work. There are also few areas for refreshment, which has been pointed out as contributing to increased employee stress.
Plan Details:
1. Introduction of a hot-desking system: Make effective use of space and promote serendipitous communication.
2. Installation of focus booths: Provide quiet spaces conducive to concentration.
3. Creation of relaxation areas: Install massage chairs and nap spaces.
4. Greening initiatives: Increase indoor plants to improve psychological comfort.
Expected Effects:
- Improved employee satisfaction: Satisfaction scores up by 30% in surveys.
- Increased productivity: Operational efficiency improved by 10%.
- Reduced turnover: Number of resignations down by 5%.

Progress: Currently proceeding with layout changes and equipment selection for the introduction of hot-desking. The focus booths and relaxation areas are targeted for completion within the year.

Notice of Revisions to the 2024 Personnel Evaluation System

Purpose of the Revisions:
- Improve employee motivation and ensure fair evaluations
- More clearly assess contributions to achieving corporate goals
- Support employee growth and promote skill development

Key Revisions:
1. Overhaul of the goal-setting sheet: Encourage more specific and achievable goals based on the SMART principles.
2. Introduction of multi-source evaluation: Incorporate feedback not only from supervisors but also from peers and subordinates into evaluations.
3. Mandatory feedback meetings: Conduct detailed feedback meetings between supervisors and subordinates once every half-year.
4. Increased transparency of evaluation results: Clarify evaluation criteria and processes and strengthen accountability to employees.

Expected Outcomes:
- Increased employee engagement: Engagement scores to improve by 20%.
- Performance improvement: Goal achievement rates to improve by 15%.
- Enhanced talent development: Individual strengths and weaknesses become clearer, leading to appropriate skill development.

Implementation Date: Effective from October 1, 2024.

New Employee Training Program 2025

Period: April 1–April 30, 2025
Participants: All new graduates and mid-career hires joining in FY2025
Objectives:
- Understanding and embedding the corporate culture
- Acquiring fundamental knowledge and skills required for the job
- Building networks among employees

Program Content:
1. Orientation: Overview of the company, organizational structure, and work rules.
2. Business Etiquette Training: Basic professional manners and communication skills.
3. Information Security Training: Measures to prevent information leaks and the importance of personal data protection.
4. OJT (On-the-Job Training): Learning through practical experience in each department.
5. Team Building Training: Developing cooperation and problem-solving skills through group work.

Completion Requirements: Participation in all training programs and submission of a final report.
Notes: Overtime is generally prohibited during the training period. In urgent, unavoidable cases, prior application is required.

Notification of Revisions to the Employee Benefits Program

Purpose of Revision: To promote employee health and improve quality of life
Main Revisions:
1. Enhanced Health Checkups: Full subsidy for comprehensive medical checkups (age 40 and over).
2. Fitness Subsidy: Subsidy for fitness gym fees up to 5,000 yen per month.
3. Expanded Childcare and Nursing Care Support: Extended eligibility period for short working hours; babysitter fee subsidy (monthly cap of 20,000 yen).
4. Self-Development Support: Subsidies for certification acquisition costs and participation in external training (annual cap of 100,000 yen).

Effective Date: April 1, 2025
Expected Effects:
- Extension of healthy life expectancy for employees.
- Reduced turnover through support for balancing childcare/nursing care and work.
- Promotion of employee upskilling and career development.

Contact: Human Resources Department, Employee Benefits Group

Notice of Information Security Policy Revision

Revision Date: March 1, 2025
Purpose: To address the increasing risk of cyberattacks and appropriately protect information assets.
Key Changes:
1. Strengthening of the Password Policy: Mandatory regular changes and raised complexity requirements.
2. Mandatory Two-Factor Authentication: Applied when accessing internal systems and when connecting via VPN.
3. Clarification of the BYOD (Bring Your Own Device) Policy: Detailed rules for the business use of personally owned devices.
4. Enhanced Social Engineering Countermeasures: Regular training for employees.

Compliance:
- All employees must understand and comply with this policy.
- Violations may result in disciplinary action in accordance with employment regulations.

Contact: IT Promotion Department, Security Team

FY2026 New Graduate Recruitment Plan

Positions: General positions, Engineering positions, Designer positions
Number of hires: General 20, Engineering 30, Designer 10
Recruitment schedule:
- March–May 2025: Company information sessions, application entry opens
- June–July 2025: Document screening, first-round interviews
- August–September 2025: Second-round and final interviews
- October 2025: Job offers
Desired candidate profile:
- Proactive individuals who can contribute to problem solving
- Individuals eager to learn new knowledge and technologies and willing to take on challenges
- Team players who can collaborate and build good relationships with others
Starting salary:
- Bachelor’s degree: 230,000 JPY/month
- Master’s degree: 250,000 JPY/month
Benefits: Full social insurance coverage, commuting allowance, housing allowance, and employee dormitory available.

Notes: Depending on the position, selection may place emphasis on specialized skills.

Minutes of the Strategic Management Meeting, April 15, 2025

Agenda: Business plan for the first half of FY2025 and progress of key projects
Attendees: President and CEO, Division Heads, CFO, CTO

1. Business Plan Review:
   - First-quarter sales increased by 10% year over year, with the introduction of new services performing strongly.
   - Operating profit exceeded the target.

2. Key Project Progress:
   - Project A (New Business Development): Completed the market research phase and commenced prototype development. A beta release is targeted within the year.
   - Project B (Cost Reduction): Achieved cost reduction targets across all departments, with notable reductions in travel expenses and consumables.
   - Project C (Enhanced Talent Development): Advancing the review of in-house training programs and planning to introduce specialized training with external instructors.

3. Other:
   - Decided to promote company-wide operational efficiency through the use of AI technologies in the second half.
   - Confirmed progress on materials preparation for the shareholders’ meeting.

Employee Awareness Survey Report 2024

Implementation period: October 1–31, 2024
Target: All employees (response rate: 80%)
Survey items: Sense of fulfillment at work, interpersonal relationships, working hours, compensation, career development, corporate culture, etc.

Key findings:
1. Sense of fulfillment: 70% of respondents reported “feeling fulfilled at work,” with particular appreciation for the engaging nature of their tasks and strong teamwork.
2. Interpersonal relationships: 85% reported “maintaining good interpersonal relationships.” Company events and club activities are facilitating interaction.
3. Working hours: 45% of respondents felt “there is too much overtime,” with some departments showing persistently long working hours.
4. Compensation: 60% felt their compensation is “reasonable,” though many voiced a desire for evaluation more closely tied to performance.
5. Career development: 55% responded that “there are sufficient opportunities for career development,” with increased use of e-learning and external training.

Recommendations:
- Implement concrete measures to correct prolonged working hours.
- Consider systems that more fairly evaluate individual performance.

Announcements

Basic Information

The park features a 14 km cycling course that can be used with rental bicycles or your own bike.

  • The course is for bicycles only. Walking, running with pets, bikes with training wheels, balance bikes, kick scooters, etc. are prohibited.
  • Bringing in novelty/modified bicycles is prohibited.
  • Excessive speed and unsafe overtaking are prohibited.
  • In Seisho Prefecture, bicycle insurance is mandatory. Rental bicycles come with a Red TS Mark.

Regulations for the Enforcement of the Heisei Peace Park Ordinance

April 1, 1957

Regulation No. 37

The Regulations for the Enforcement of the Seisho Prefectural Park Ordinance are hereby promulgated.

Regulations for the Enforcement of the Seisho Prefectural Park Ordinance

Table of Contents

Chapter I General Provisions (Articles 1 and 2)

Chapter II Installation, etc. of Park Facilities by Persons Other Than the Metropolis (Articles 3 and 4)

Chapter III Occupancy (Articles 5–10)

Chapter IV Management (Article 11)

Chapter V Fee-Charging Parks and Fee-Charging Facilities (Articles 12–17)

Chapter VI Miscellaneous Provisions (Articles 18–26)

Supplementary Provisions

Chapter I General Provisions

(Purpose)

Article 1 The purpose of these Regulations is to prescribe the matters necessary for the enforcement of the Heisei Peace Park Ordinance (Seisho Prefecture Ordinance No. 107 of December 1956; hereinafter referred to as “the Ordinance”).

(Closed Days and Hours of Use, etc.)

Article 2 The closed days of fee-charging parks and the closed days or closed periods of fee-charging facilities shall be as set forth in Appended Table 1. However, the Governor or the Designated Administrator (meaning the designated administrator as provided in Article 24-7, paragraph 1 of the Ordinance; the same shall apply hereinafter) may, due to circumstances, temporarily change or designate these.

2 The opening hours, hours of use, or admission hours of fee-charging parks and fee-charging facilities shall be as set forth in Appended Table 1-2.

(Amended in full by Regulation No. 204 of 1974; partially amended by Regulation No. 197 of 1975, Regulation No. 36 of 1979, Regulation No. 94 of 2004, and Regulation No. 28 of 2005)

Chapter II Installation, etc. of Park Facilities by Persons Other Than the Metropolis

(Security Deposit for Permission to Install or Manage Park Facilities)

Article 3 The amount, appropriation, and refund of the security deposit under Article 8, paragraph 2 of the Ordinance (including cases where it is applied mutatis mutandis under Article 25-2, paragraph 1 and Article 26, paragraph 4 of the Ordinance) shall be as follows.

1. Amount of Security Deposit

The amount shall be equivalent to three months of the usage fee set forth in Article 4, paragraph 1. However, when granting permission to install park facilities (including facilities equivalent to park facilities; the same shall apply hereinafter), the amount shall be increased by one-tenth of the budgeted cost of the construction related to the installation.

2. Application of Security Deposit

If a person who has received permission to install or manage park facilities fails to pay to the Metropolitan Government any amount due in respect of such park facilities, the security deposit shall be applied to the amount due.

3. Refund of Security Deposit

The full amount shall be refunded after 60 days have elapsed from the date on which the permission period for installation or management of the park facilities expired, the date on which installation or management of the park facilities was discontinued, or the date on which the permission for installation or management of the park facilities was revoked, and after the Governor has confirmed restoration to the original condition. However, if the security deposit has been applied pursuant to the preceding item, the amount refunded shall be the security deposit amount less the portion applied.

2. No interest shall be added to the security deposit upon refund.

(Showa 39 Regulation No. 65; Heisei 11 Regulation No. 270; partial revision)

(Usage fees for land or park facilities, etc.)

Article 4 The amount of the usage fee under Article 9, paragraph 1 of the Ordinance (including where applied mutatis mutandis under Article 25-2, paragraph 1 and Article 26, paragraph 4 of the Ordinance) shall be as set forth in Appended Table 2.

2. The minimum amount of the usage fee under Article 9, paragraph 2 of the Ordinance shall be as set forth in Appended Table 2.

3. The amount prescribed by the Seisho Prefectural Regulations under Article 9, paragraph 3 of the Ordinance shall be as set forth in Appended Table 2.

4. The method of collecting the usage fee under Article 9, paragraph 6 of the Ordinance shall be as follows:

(i) Where the period of permission to install or manage park facilities does not exceed three months:
The fee shall be collected at the time the permission is granted.

(ii) Where the period of permission to install or manage park facilities exceeds three months:
The fee shall be collected by period classifications, with the amount for each period collected in the first month of that period.

Produce
Fresh vegetables and fruits, delivered directly from production areas across the country, are gathered and waiting for the auction to begin.

Produce

Shipments of produce arrive from all over Japan by truck from the day before through the night before auctions and other sales take place, and the arriving goods are lined up in the wholesale market. Negotiated sales begin at midnight, and auctions start around 6:30 a.m.
With daybreak, the wholesale market bustles with people and vehicles moving about in a hurry, gradually filling with energy. Thousands of tons of products are traded in just about two hours and then shipped out to Tokyo and surrounding cities.

Unloading (3:00 p.m. the day before – midnight)
Unloading (3:00 p.m. the day before – midnight)

A variety of vegetables and fruits arrive from production areas. The arriving goods are received by wholesalers, sorted by item and grade, and lined up in the wholesale area while still in their cardboard boxes.

Previewing goods for the auction (5:00 a.m.)
Previewing goods for the auction (5:00 a.m.)

Intermediate wholesalers and registered buyers carefully check the production area and grade indicated on the cardboard boxes of vegetables and fruits, select in advance what they want to buy, decide roughly how much they will pay, and prepare for the upcoming auction.

Start of the auction (6:30 a.m.)
Start of the auction (6:30 a.m.)

At last, the auction begins.

From around 6:00 a.m. to around 8:00 a.m., the market comes alive with auctions. There are two types of auctions for vegetables and fruit: fixed auctions (photo) and moving auctions (photo). The fixed auction is also called a sample auction, where the auctioneer writes the size and quantity of the goods on a small blackboard and sells them in order. In the moving auction, all the goods are shown as the auction proceeds while moving from lot to lot. In a moving auction, many intermediate wholesalers surround a single auctioneer to bid. Intermediate wholesalers and authorized buyers assess size and quality as they successively win bids on the items.

Loading (8:00 a.m.)
Loading (8:00 a.m.)

Using forklifts and turret trucks, vegetables and fruit are quickly loaded onto buyers’ vehicles, and buyers such as greengrocers transport the goods they purchased from the intermediate wholesalers to their own shops. In this way, fresh vegetables and fruit are delivered to your neighborhood.

Battery is a word that, in modern usage, has several quite different meanings.

Its original etymology is the Latin battuere (to strike, to beat, to attack), sharing the same root as the English words bat (a tool for hitting a ball) and battle.

Today, many people first think of a cell or storage battery, but for baseball enthusiasts, “battery” refers to the pitcher and catcher as a pair.

Thus, the word is used in meanings that at first glance seem completely different and unrelated. The reason it is used in such diverse ways is that English–Japanese dictionaries list older senses such as “to strike (fire) repeatedly” and “a line of artillery,” from which the idiom “a battery of” meaning “a series of” arose. From this usage meaning “a series (of devices),” battery apparently came to refer to electrical cells. In the past, multiple Leyden jars, which could store static electricity, connected together were called a “battery” in the sense of “a series (of devices),” and over time the term came to refer to batteries in general.

Things commonly called batteries
- Primary cells, dry cells.
- Storage batteries, battery packs.
- Lead-acid batteries for automobiles and motorcycles (automotive batteries). In the field of vehicles, a state in which the charge is depleted is called “a dead battery.”
- Batteries used in devices such as smartphones and digital cameras.

“Battery” by itself does not mean battery charge level. The phrase “the battery died” is sometimes used to express that the charge has run out.
Baseball terminology
Battery (baseball) – One of the terms in baseball. Refers to the pitcher-catcher pair. It originated by likening the firepower of the pitching staff to the artillery unit mentioned above, and later came to include the catcher as well [6]. The titles of the following works refer to this meaning.
Battery Park – A park in New York City, also simply called Battery.
Usages close to the etymology
The original meanings related to “to hit” or “to strike/shoot,” or terms closely connected to those.

An artillery company.
In a marching band, a collective term for the moving percussion instruments on the field: marching snare drum, multi-tenors, marching bass drum, marching cymbals, etc.
In musical notation, a tremolo between two notes.
Meanings related to “attack”
Battery (tort) – A type of tort. Listed as a category that falls under the inclusive term “injury to a person.”
Battery (crime) – A type of crime referring to unlawful physical contact.

See also
Centralized watering system
BATT
Electric cell
Battery cage – A poultry-farming system. “Batarī” is a rendering closer to the original pronunciation of “Battery.”
List of pages beginning with “Battery”
List of pages with “Battery” in the title

Furthermore, the word “battery” can be interpreted in various ways depending on cultural background and specialized context. For example, in the automotive industry, electric vehi

Auroras are atmospheric light phenomena observed near the polar regions of celestial bodies. Depending on the observed polar region, they are also called polar lights, and more specifically northern lights if closer to the Arctic and southern lights if closer to the Antarctic (see the section on nomenclature below). Unless otherwise noted, this entry describes auroras on Earth.

Named after a goddess, auroras have been recorded in ancient documents and folklore, and have been observed in Japan as well. In the modern era, explorers of both poles widely publicized their existence (see Observation history). Research on auroras advanced alongside the development of electromagnetism (see Research history). The prevailing mechanism is thought to be that plasma from the solar wind descends rapidly along Earth’s magnetic field lines, exciting oxygen and nitrogen atoms in the atmosphere and causing them to emit light, although many details remain unclear (see Mechanism). In addition to light (visible light), various other electromagnetic waves, electric currents and magnetic fields, and heat are released. The colors of auroras visible to the human eye from Earth are mainly blue, green, and red (see Emissions). Whether they produce sound (audible sound) is a matter of debate (see Auroral sounds). They are not typically seen in the vicinity of the North or South Poles themselves, but are more likely to be observed in an oval region known as the auroral zone. Auroras in the Antarctic and Arctic exhibit similar shapes and lights, a property known as conjugacy (see Regions of occurrence). Auroras also appear on planets other than Earth if both a magnetic field and an atmosphere are present.
