The Origins and Cultural Background of Italian Cuisine

Italian food culture traces its roots to ancient Rome, and through a fusion with traditions nurtured along medieval trade routes and within each region, it has evolved into today’s diverse culinary styles. The cooking techniques and ways of bringing out the best in ingredients that have been passed down across the country are like living history books, producing flavors and atmospheres that differ from region to region. For instance, in southern Italy, the warm climate supports thriving olive cultivation bathed in abundant sunlight, while inland and northern areas offer rich, mountain-influenced sauces and dishes that feature vegetables varying with the seasons. These backgrounds cannot be conveyed through a single recipe alone; rather, a multitude of elements—natural environments and historical trajectories cultivated in each region, as well as social bonds—intertwine to underpin the depth and diversity of Italian cuisine.

Moreover, Italy’s traditional dishes are the crystallization of wisdom handed down through gatherings in the home and within local communities. The preparation of meals, the cooking process, and each dish laid out on the table vividly reflect the lifestyles of their times, conveying the natural bounty and passion embedded in the ingredients themselves. From ancient banquets and medieval village festivals to the grand feasts of Renaissance courts, the foods enlivened by traditions unique to each region continue, even today, to inspire the modern table.

In addition, Italian cuisine is not merely a set of cooking techniques, but also resonates with visual arts, music, and even poetic expression, and...

On Chef Giorgio Lorenza’s Innovative Cooking Techniques

Chef Giorgio Lorenza is garnering attention as a figure who, while deeply rooted in Italian tradition, brings innovation to contemporary food culture. His culinary philosophy lies in fusing ancient wisdom with cutting-edge technology to realize unprecedented approaches to cooking.

Formative Childhood Experiences and the Shaping of His Culinary Vision

The warm meals he enjoyed at home during childhood and the experience of his grandmother’s home cooking became the wellspring of his creative drive. Building on a foundation of understanding traditional techniques, he has developed his own culinary style.

Fusion of Science and Tradition

In his kitchen, the latest technologies—such as low-temperature cooking and rapid chilling—are employed to maximize the inherent flavors, aromas, and textures of ingredients. By fine-tuning temperature, time, and heat with precision down to the smallest increments, he practices cooking that respects the individuality of each ingredient.

Artistry That Engages Sight, Smell, and Touch

Lorenza believes that cuisine is a comprehensive art form that mobilizes not only taste, but also sight, smell, and touch. He is deeply particular about plating and the selection of tableware, presenting dishes that merge traditional beauty with modern aesthetics.

Global Recognition and Ripple Effects

His cuisine has received high praise from food writers and critics, and its artistic quality and technical depth have influenced chefs around the world. Many culinary professionals learn new modes of expression from Lorenza’s techniques and incorporate them into their own styles.

Passing the Torch to the Future

While preserving tradition, he…

Diversity and Development of Food Culture in the Mediterranean Region

The countries spanning the Mediterranean coast have nurtured a rich diversity of food cultures thanks to their warm climate and abundant natural environment. The region’s distinctive conditions—blessed by both sea and land—have enabled the cultivation of ingredients such as olives, wine, and herbs. Combined with unique cooking techniques in each locale, these ingredients have been elevated into dishes with rich flavors. Throughout history, influenced by trade routes and cultural exchange, from ancient civilizations through the Middle Ages and into modern times, Mediterranean food culture has continually evolved by incorporating new elements.

In this region, traditional recipes are passed down within families, while at the same time being reinterpreted in various countries under the influence of growing health consciousness and globalization. The fusion of age-old traditions with modern lifestyles brings layered deliciousness and vibrancy to the table, creating unforgettable experiences for many people. Moreover, each nation’s geopolitical background and ethnic character impart distinctive individuality and historical depth to its cuisine, allowing visitors not only to enjoy meals but also to engage with the stories embedded within them.

Additionally, the Mediterranean region’s distinctive food culture is internationally recognized for its significant health benefits and nutritional balance, and has become the subject of extensive academic research. For example, studies have shown that combinations of ingredients used in traditional dishes contribute to the prevention of heart disease and lifestyle-related illnesses—findings that demonstrate how ancient wisdom aligns remarkably well with contemporary health science. As a result, the Mediterranean diet is…

The Art of Temperature Control in Cooking and Its Practical Significance

In the cooking process, temperature control has long been studied and practiced as a key factor that greatly influences the flavor, aroma, and texture of ingredients. From intuitive methods rooted in ancient wisdom to today’s precise temperature control technologies, temperature management has evolved beyond a mere technical task into a realm that can be described as an art. Each chef, drawing on personal experience and sensibility, finely adjusts heat levels, cooking times, and even the materials of the cookware used, striving to bring out the inherent qualities of each ingredient.

For example, low-temperature cooking requires maintaining a constant temperature over an extended period, making it essential to devise ways to deliver heat evenly throughout the interior of the food. Moreover, by combining techniques such as rapid cooling and partial heating, it becomes possible to create diverse textures—crisp on the outside and juicy on the inside. These cooking processes transcend the framework of purely physical temperature management and, accompanied by a rhythm and harmony akin to music, imbue the entire dish with a sense of vitality that moves the diner.

In the modern kitchen, the introduction of cutting-edge digital controllers and temperature sensors has transformed methods that once relied on intuition into management based on scientific data. This shift has enabled control over delicate temperature changes that were difficult to achieve with traditional approaches, allowing ongoing efforts to maximize the subtle umami of ingredients. Improvements in the precision of temperature control contribute not only to cooking efficiency but also to the quality of the meal served.

Cultural Backgrounds and Their Influence as Seen in the Interconnectedness of Cuisine and Music

Cuisine and music have long formed a deep relationship as forms of artistic expression that engage the human senses and enrich emotions and memories.

Unity in Traditional Culture

In traditional food cultures, music performed at banquets and festivals has fused with cuisine to create moving experiences. The rhythms and melodies of music resonate with the rich flavors and textures of dishes, delivering emotional satisfaction through hearing. For this reason, music has been considered indispensable at the dining table in many cultures.

Collaborations in the Modern Era

In contemporary restaurants and gastronomy events, collaborations between music and cuisine are receiving increasing attention.

- Beyond mere background music, music plays the role of a “staging” that enhances flavor
- Some studies indicate that the tempo and melody of music influence the progression and atmosphere of a meal
- Growing evidence suggests that rhythm affects chewing and taste experiences

Interconnectedness Rooted in History and Culture

This interconnectedness is portrayed in numerous cultural and artistic records and has been valued as a foundation for conversation, shared emotions, and memorable experiences at the table.

- Traditional rituals and family gatherings
- International gastronomic events
- Course meals staged with performance, among others

By synchronizing music with cuisine, productions that evoke deeper emotions are achieved.

Dining as a Holistic Experience

Trends in Dietary Reform Based on Contemporary Health Consciousness and Their Philosophical Reflections

In modern society, alongside a rising health consciousness, new perspectives are being added to traditional food cultures, and dietary reforms are unfolding that go beyond mere nutritional intake to influence entire lifestyles. By blending traditional culinary techniques with the latest nutritional insights, cooking methods are being explored that maximize ingredients’ inherent flavors and health benefits. As a result, meals are being reappraised as a vital element for balancing mind and body. The use of organic ingredients, low-carb and high-fiber recipes, and an emphasis on fresh, locally sourced produce, among other trends, have made contemporary health consciousness a catalyst for diverse reforms.

These developments can also be seen as a process in which wisdom inherited as a traditional legacy is being reconstructed to suit modern technologies and lifestyles. Health-conscious cuisine provides reassurance and trust by conveying not only flavor without compromise and visual beauty and color, but also the stories behind the origins of ingredients and their methods of production. Such a backdrop expands beyond the notion of simply being good for the body into a philosophical approach to eating itself, becoming a theme keenly discussed by many experts and enthusiasts.

Within the context of wellness, contemporary healthy eating has also garnered attention as a countermeasure to the stresses of modern life, positioned as a symbol of a new lifestyle aimed at harmonizing mind and body. Furthermore, these trends in dietary reform are deeply connected to environmental protection and the promotion of sustainable agriculture, serving as an important impetus for transforming values across society.

# Changes in Cooking Environments Through the History and Technological Advances of Kitchen Tools

Kitchen tools are more than mere cooking instruments; they embody the very history of human food culture and technological evolution. From ancient times to the present, the development of tools has progressed alongside the diversification of cuisine, symbolizing shifts in the cooking environment.

## Historical Transitions and the Evolution of Materials

- Ancient civilizations: Tools made from natural materials such as stone and pottery were used, with open-fire cooking as the norm. In some regions, fire handling was tied to cultural and religious rituals.
- Medieval Europe and Asia: Metal cookware and blades made of bronze and iron appeared. The prototypes of the wok, kamado stoves, and ovens began to take shape in this era.
- Industrial Revolution: Cast iron, stainless steel, and enameled products rose to prominence, greatly improving reproducibility and efficiency in cooking.
- Since the 20th century: New materials such as plastics, silicone, and heat-resistant glass spread into households, promoting diversification and weight reduction of cookware.

## Introduction of Modern Technologies

- Electronic control systems: IH cooktops, microwave ovens, and steam ovens have made precise control of temperature and time possible.
- Integration with smart appliances: Integration with IoT technology is underway, including app-controlled rice cookers and robots that automatically reproduce recipes.
- Improved hygiene and safety: The introduction of antimicrobial materials and features such as automatic shutoff help prevent fires and burns, creating a safer environment.

## Fusion of Tradition and High-Tech

The Evolution of Social Culture Seen in Traditional Italian Dinners and Its Impact

Traditional Italian dinners have a long history as more than just a setting for meals; they have served as a vital social element that deepens family and community bonds. Since ancient times, dinner has marked the end of the day, and gathering around the table has facilitated intergenerational transmission of wisdom and deep conversation among people. The unique rituals and manners passed down in each family or village vividly reflect the cultural characteristics of each region. As a place for sharing warmth within the home and a sense of solidarity in the community, the traditional Italian dinner has not lost its value even today.

Although the role of dinner as a social practice has changed with the times, the importance of communication born from gathering at the table remains strongly present in the modern era. In addition to conventional formats, new aspects have emerged to suit today’s busy lifestyles, such as shorter conversations and information sharing through social media. As a result, the psychological and social effects of meals are being reassessed from multiple angles.

Moreover, the traditional Italian dinner constitutes a cultural heritage imbued with family recipes and innovations passed down through generations, as well as stories of region-specific ingredients. Each dish on the table is a precious presence that evokes past glories and the wisdom of predecessors. Consequently, the meal itself goes beyond a mere means of nourishment to become a cherished moment or memory in individual lives, playing a part in fostering a sense of solidarity across society.

Reevaluating Pasta Dishes Through Regional Characteristics and Ingredient Identity: Cultural Significance

Pasta dishes are recognized as a highly distinctive culinary category shaped within the dynamic culture and history of Italy. The unique climate and traditions of each region influence the types of wheat used, pasta-making techniques, and even cooking methods, elevating pasta into something more than mere food. Recipes passed down through generations reflect an interplay of factors such as local climate conditions, soil characteristics, and traditional events, giving rise to unique flavors and tastes.

In particular, cooking methods developed in differing natural environments—such as mountainous areas and coastal regions—highlight the individuality of pasta dishes in homes and restaurants alike, and are celebrated as sources of regional pride. These backgrounds often extend beyond technique and taste, deeply tied to the lifestyles of local residents and historical episodes, making the story contained in each plate of pasta an essential element that enriches the dining table.

Furthermore, contemporary chefs, while honoring traditional pasta-making techniques, are beginning to explore new expressions by incorporating scientific insights and modern technologies. As part of these innovative efforts, attempts are being made to fuse ancient recipes with contemporary food processing methods, resulting in new styles of pasta that harmonize tradition and modernity. Consequently, pasta dishes are being reevaluated by gourmets around the world, while also illuminating the diversity and depth of regional food cultures.

New Food Philosophies by Culinary Researchers and Their Practical Applications: Prospects for the Future

Contemporary culinary researchers continue to propose new food philosophies unbound by conventional fixed ideas, driven by free thinking and innovative perspectives. While maximizing the natural flavors of ingredients, they also take on cooking methods that realize modern health consciousness, environmental protection, and a sustainable food culture, seeking to rediscover hidden values. A wide range of practices are underway—from reinterpreting traditional recipes to developing new preservation techniques and creating inventive dishes that highlight local ingredients—and these endeavors are sparking significant innovation and expectations for the dining tables of the future.

Rather than viewing meals as mere acts of nutritional intake, these researchers position food as a comprehensive art form that stimulates the five senses—sight, smell, taste, touch—and even affects the mind. Their approach transcends the bounds of conventional home cooking and shapes a new concept that encompasses ecology, health, and even social ethics. As a result, many experts anticipate that future food culture will evolve in a more diverse and sustainable direction.

Moreover, through lectures, workshops, and documentary films, their philosophies and practices are being widely disseminated, gradually building mechanisms by which these new food philosophies permeate society and influence consumers, producers, and even policy levels. This movement goes beyond the traditional framework of “meals” and can be described as a revolution that renews culture as a whole, shaping the future...

negative_chunk_list.content: In new graduate recruitment at tech companies, coding interviews are generally a key selection factor. Candidates are evaluated on their understanding of algorithms and data structures, problem-solving ability, and the readability and efficiency of their code. Interview formats vary widely, including whiteboard coding, online judge systems, pair programming, and system design interviews. In major tech companies, particularly GAFA (Google, Apple, Facebook, Amazon), LeetCode-style problems frequently appear, and optimization of time and space complexity is required. Mastery of fundamental algorithmic patterns—such as dynamic programming, graph algorithms, tree manipulations, string processing, and array operations—is essential. In system design interviews, candidates are assessed on their ability to architect large-scale systems and their consideration of scalability, reliability, and performance. Recommended preparation includes daily coding practice, mock interviews, writing technical blogs, and contributing to open-source projects. Common language choices include Python, Java, C++, and JavaScript, and communication skills with interviewers, logical thinking, and stress tolerance are also important evaluation criteria. In recent years, remote interviews have become mainstream, and skills in using screen sharing and collaboration tools have also become necessary.

The onboarding process for new graduate engineers depends heavily on a company’s tech stack and development culture. The typical training period lasts 3–6 months and is built on three pillars: technical training, business training, and a mentorship program. Technical training includes learning the programming languages, frameworks, and toolchains used in-house; understanding the code review process; practicing test-driven development (TDD); and mastering the Git/GitHub workflow. Business training aims to deepen understanding of the product, customers, and industry, as well as instill the company’s mission and values. In the mentorship program, a senior engineer provides one-on-one technical guidance, career advice, and support with day-to-day tasks. Participation in real projects is phased: it starts with small bug fixes and minor feature additions and gradually progresses to more complex tasks. It’s also important to set up the development environment, learn how to use internal tools, and master communication methods (e.g., Slack, Zoom, Confluence). Evaluations are conducted through regular 1-on-1 meetings, technical skill checks, contributions to projects, teamwork, and initiative. Some companies also offer robust internal events such as hackathons, study sessions, and lightning talks (LTs), providing opportunities to build cross-team connections.

In the tech industry, compensation structures are generally composed of three elements: base salary, variable pay, and benefits. The base salary is a fixed remuneration determined by years of experience, technical level, and job responsibilities. Variable pay includes performance bonuses, project bonuses, and equity compensation (stock options and RSUs), and is tied to the performance of the individual, team, and company. Startups in particular place a heavier emphasis on equity compensation, with expectations for future capital gains. Benefits typically include health insurance, dental insurance, life insurance, defined contribution retirement plans, paid vacation, sick leave, parental leave, remote work stipends, equipment purchase support, subsidies for learning and training expenses, gym subsidies, and meal allowances. Engineer-specific benefits may include support for purchasing technical books, conference attendance subsidies, certification support, sabbatical leave, and permission for side jobs. In salary negotiations, it is important to understand your market value, compare offers with competitors, demonstrate technical skills, and present past achievements. Raises are conducted on an annual or biannual review cycle, evaluating factors such as technical expertise, leadership, innovation, and contributions to the team. In recent years, job rotation and career ladder systems have provided multiple career paths in both technical and management tracks.

The technical skill evaluation framework for engineers is conducted along four axes: programming ability, system design capability, problem-solving ability, and communication skills. Programming ability is measured by proficiency in multiple languages, understanding of algorithms and data structures, code quality, and development speed. Language proficiency is assessed on four levels: beginner (understanding of basic syntax), intermediate (ability to utilize frameworks), advanced (ability to design architectures), and expert (ability to contribute to language design). System design capability encompasses understanding of architectural patterns, consideration of scalability, security design, and performance optimization. Domain-specific expertise in areas such as microservices, API design, database design, cloud architecture, and DevOps practices is also important. Problem-solving ability is evaluated based on requirements analysis, technology selection, trade-off decision-making, debugging skills, and a mindset of continuous improvement. Communication skills emphasize the ability to explain technical content, collaborate within a team, provide constructive feedback in code reviews, and mentor others. Evaluation methods include regular technical interviews, assessment of project achievements, 360-degree feedback, technical presentations, and contributions to open-source software.

The organizational culture of technology companies is characterized by an emphasis on innovation, data-driven decision-making, agile development, and open communication. In an innovation-focused culture, practices include taking on challenges without fear of failure, encouraging experimental projects, adopting the 20% rule (allocating a portion of work hours to independent research), and holding regular hackathons. Data-driven decision-making prioritizes A/B testing, metrics analysis, KPI management, and hypothesis-testing cycles. In agile development, Scrum, Kanban, continuous integration/deployment (CI/CD), and a DevOps culture are deeply embedded. Ritual elements such as sprint planning, daily stand-ups, and retrospectives are also deeply rooted in organizational operations. Open communication emphasizes transparency, flat organizational structures, and ensuring psychological safety. Companies leverage communication tools like Slack, Discord, and Microsoft Teams to balance asynchronous and synchronous communication. Diversity and inclusion are also core values, with active promotion of diversity in gender, race, age, and background, and efforts toward bias mitigation, creating inclusive environments, and providing equal opportunities.

In the new graduate recruitment process, a five-stage selection flow—document screening, aptitude tests, technical interviews, HR interviews, and executive interviews—is standard. In the document screening, resumes, academic transcripts, statements of purpose, and portfolios (for technical roles) are evaluated. Portfolios emphasize personal projects, team development, outcomes from internships, code on GitHub, and technical blogs. Aptitude testing includes ability assessments such as SPI, Tamatebako, TG-WEB, and CAB, as well as personality tests. Technical interviews assess programming skills, system design ability, technical knowledge, and problem-solving approaches, and may include live coding sessions, technical discussions, and in-depth questions about past projects. HR interviews evaluate motivation for applying, career vision, cultural fit, and communication skills. Executive interviews make the final judgment based on alignment with corporate philosophy, leadership potential, future prospects, and potential contributions to the company. The selection period typically lasts 2–6 weeks, and it is expected that feedback be provided at each stage and that transparency regarding selection status be ensured. After an offer is made, companies also provide follow-up for prospective hires, pre-boarding training, and opportunities to connect with peers.

In career development within engineering organizations, programs are offered along two axes: technical growth and leadership development. For technical growth, offerings include in-house technical training, participation in external conferences, support for obtaining certifications, subsidies for purchasing technical books, and access to online learning platforms (such as Coursera, Udemy, and Pluralsight). Learning paths are designed by specialty (frontend, backend, infrastructure, data science, security, etc.), with clearly defined proficiency targets for each level. Practical learning opportunities include pair programming, code reviews, tech sharing sessions, internal hackathons, and study groups. For leadership development, opportunities are provided such as project lead experience, mentoring, serving as an instructor for new hire training, participating as a hiring interviewer, and writing technical blog posts. For those inclined toward management, training is available in management fundamentals, 1-on-1 skills, performance management, team building, and strategic planning. Career support includes regular career discussions, career coaching, internal mobility programs, and permission for side jobs or concurrent roles. Regarding promotion and advancement, established processes include objective evaluations of technical levels, 360-degree feedback, promotion interviews, and reviews by a promotion committee.

In engineering work within a remote work environment, three key challenges are maintaining productivity, streamlining communication, and ensuring work-life balance. For maintaining productivity, it is necessary to set up a home office environment conducive to focus, provide high-performance PCs and monitors, secure high-speed internet connections, and ensure stable VPN connectivity. In terms of the development environment, a portable setup is built using cloud-based IDEs, remote desktops, virtual machines, and container technologies. From a security perspective, multi-factor authentication, VPNs, endpoint protection, data encryption, and adherence to security policies are required. For communication efficiency, it is important to leverage asynchronous communication tools (Slack, Microsoft Teams), project management tools (Jira, Trello, Asana), and document sharing tools (Confluence, Notion, GitHub Wiki). Synchronous collaboration is also ensured through regular video meetings, virtual stand-ups, online pair programming, and screen-sharing sessions. For work-life balance, effective self-management of working hours, taking appropriate breaks, switching between work and personal time, and maintaining mental health are crucial. On the company side, support measures such as remote work allowances, equipment purchase assistance, mental health support, and online social events are provided.

Semiconductor Manufacturing Equipment Industry Technology Trend Analysis Report 2024

Technological innovation in leading-edge semiconductor manufacturing is accelerating, with particularly notable improvements in the performance of extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography systems. For mass production at 5 nm and below, new systems have been introduced that deliver a 30% increase in throughput and a 20% reduction in power consumption compared with previous models. Domestic manufacturers are engaged in a race to develop next-generation equipment, and total annual capital investment across the industry has risen 45% year over year. Manufacturing process optimization technologies that leverage AI and machine learning have entered practical use, with confirmed improvements in yield. Looking ahead, accommodating the fabrication of specialized devices for quantum computing presents a new technical challenge, necessitating further enhancements in the precision of manufacturing equipment.

Electronic Components Trading Company Market Trend Survey Report (Q3 2024)

The domestic electronic components market continues to expand, with particularly strong demand for processor-related components. On the pricing front, average unit prices have risen 7% year-on-year due to higher raw material costs; however, profitability has improved thanks to added value from technological innovation. On the supply side, capacity expansions by major manufacturers are showing signs of stabilizing the supply-demand balance. By customer industry, demand is highest in automobiles (33%), industrial equipment (28%), and telecommunications equipment (24%), with especially notable growth in power semiconductors for electric vehicles. Looking ahead, the spread of 5G and the increase in IoT devices are expected to drive greater demand for communications-related semiconductors.

Alfitech Industries Co., Ltd. R&D Division Technology Roadmap 2025–2030

As a development strategy for next-generation semiconductor technologies, we have set three priority areas. First, we aim to establish process technologies at 3 nm and below by pursuing new materials and innovating manufacturing processes. Second, we will enhance the performance of AI-dedicated processors (NPUs) to expand our share in the edge computing market. Third, we will establish foundational technologies for quantum computing devices to prepare for future market entry. We will maintain R&D investment at over 15% of annual sales and continue to secure top-tier researchers and introduce the latest equipment. We will also actively promote industry–academia collaboration, with plans to conduct more than 10 joint research projects with leading universities in Japan and overseas.

Manufacturing DX Promotion Case Studies (Alfitec Industries Co., Ltd. Case)

Our company is actively driving digital transformation on the factory floor, achieving higher production efficiency and stabilized quality. We implemented an always-on equipment condition monitoring system using IoT sensors, raising operational availability to 98.5% through preventive maintenance. Our visual inspection system leveraging AI image recognition has reduced inspection time by 60% compared to conventional methods, while achieving a detection accuracy of 99.7%. With production planning optimization AI, we have built a flexible production setup that adapts to demand fluctuations, achieving both inventory reduction and shorter lead times. As a data analytics foundation, all devices in the plant are network-connected, enabling visualization through a real-time production monitoring dashboard. Going forward, we plan to strengthen data collaboration with partner companies to optimize the entire supply chain.

negative_chunk_list.content: In Zenith Corporation’s Q3 2024 earnings presentation, the core Enterprise Software segment recorded sales of 720 million yen (up 15.2% year over year) with an operating margin of 22.1%. The company’s CRM solutions saw expanding adoption, particularly in the financial sector, with new contract value rising 40% year over year to 180 million yen. Driven by growing demand for cloud migration services, the Professional Services segment also performed well, posting sales of 410 million yen and operating profit of 85 million yen. The Digital Marketing business struggled amid intensifying competition, with sales at 280 million yen (down 5.3% year over year) and operating profit limited to 30 million yen. Going forward, the company plans to review its product portfolio and concentrate management resources on high-profit businesses. R&D investment will be maintained at 8.5% of sales, with a focus on developing next-generation AI capabilities.

In Apex Systems Ltd.’s Q3 2024 results, the Systems Integration business recorded net sales of 1240 million yen, achieving year-over-year growth of +18.9%. DX solutions for the manufacturing sector performed strongly, with the IoT platform “ApexConnect” posting sales of 320 million yen, up +45% year-over-year. Operating income was 248 million yen, with an operating margin of 20.0%. Meanwhile, the Legacy System Maintenance business saw a decline in revenue to 580 million yen (−3.1% year-over-year), with operating income of 75 million yen (operating margin of 12.9%). Although competition has intensified in Cloud Migration Support services due to new entrants, the company differentiated itself with proprietary automation tools, securing net sales of 190 million yen and operating income of 42 million yen.

In Stellar Technologies Inc.’s performance highlights, the data analytics platform business achieved significant growth with revenue of 890 million yen (up 28.7% year over year), operating income of 187 million yen, and an operating margin of 21.0%. Enterprise adoption of the flagship product, “StellarAnalytics Pro,” accelerated, and monthly recurring revenue (MRR) grew 55% year over year. The cybersecurity business also performed well, posting revenue of 610 million yen and operating income of 110 million yen (an operating margin of 18.0%). The mobile application development business for emerging markets is in an investment phase, recording revenue of 200 million yen and an operating loss of 15 million yen, but the expanding user base indicates promising prospects. Company-wide R&D expenses totaled 240 million yen, equivalent to 14.2% of revenue.

According to the Phoenix Digital Group Q3 2024 report, the digital marketing support business achieved net sales of 950 million yen (up 22.1% year over year), with operating income of 171 million yen and a profit margin of 18.0%. The number of companies adopting the automated ad management tool “PhoenixAds AI” surpassed 1,500, leading to a substantial increase in platform fee revenue. In the e-commerce development support business, demand was strong for packages aimed at small and medium-sized enterprises, recording net sales of 420 million yen and operating income of 76 million yen (profit margin of 18.1%). Meanwhile, the traditional web production business saw a decline due to price competition, with net sales of 310 million yen (down 8.2% year over year) and operating income of 28 million yen (profit margin of 9.0%). In the fourth quarter, the company plans to fully enter the AI chatbot business, aiming to establish a new revenue stream.

According to Quantum Solutions Corp.’s financial statements, investment in the quantum computing R&D business has accelerated, with net sales reaching 450 million yen (up 8.5% year over year). Operating profit was a modest 35 million yen (an operating margin of 7.8%), due to upfront investments to build a future technological foundation. The traditional IT consulting business remained stable, with net sales of 780 million yen and operating profit of 156 million yen (an operating margin of 20.0%). Corporate security audit services saw increased demand driven by strengthened regulations, posting net sales of 330 million yen and operating profit of 59 million yen (an operating margin of 17.9%). The company aims to commercialize quantum technologies within the next five years and plans to continue annual R&D investments of around 200 million yen. It is also actively advancing technology development through partnerships.

In Meridian Tech Solutions’ Q3 2024 results, the cloud infrastructure management business saw rapid growth with sales of 1150 million yen (up 31.2% year on year), operating income of 253 million yen, and a profit margin of 22.0%. As corporate cloud-first strategies take hold, demand for infrastructure build-out and managed operations services is surging. The DevOps tools development business also performed well, recording sales of 340 million yen and operating income of 58 million yen (a profit margin of 17.1%). The traditional on-premises software licensing business is trending downward with sales of 290 million yen (down 12.4% year on year), but it continues to maintain high profitability with operating income of 87 million yen (a profit margin of 30.0%). Going forward, the company plans to accelerate investment in cloud-native technologies to expand its market share.

In Horizon Business Systems Ltd.’s performance summary, the ERP solutions business achieved sales of 820 million yen, operating profit of 131 million yen, and a profit margin of 16.0%. Customized offerings for mid-sized manufacturers were well received, with new implementation projects increasing by 25% year over year. In the BI tool development and sales business, sales reached 480 million yen and operating profit was 82 million yen (a profit margin of 17.1%), continuing steady growth amid rising demand for data utilization. Meanwhile, the systems maintenance business saw a slight decline due to a shortage of engineers, with sales of 390 million yen (down 2.8% year over year) and operating profit of 54 million yen (a profit margin of 13.8%). To secure talent and enhance technical capabilities, the company increased its education and training budget by 40% year over year to support engineers’ skill development.

In Cosmic Technologies Group’s Q3 2024 results, the AI platform business achieved substantial growth with revenue of 10.80 billion yen (up 42.3% year over year), operating profit of 2.16 billion yen, and a margin of 20.0%. Usage of the natural language processing API service “CosmicNLP” has surged, with monthly API calls surpassing 100 million. The image recognition solutions business also performed well, generating revenue of 6.50 billion yen and operating profit of 1.17 billion yen (an 18.0% margin). The robotics business remains in the development stage, posting revenue of 1.60 billion yen and an operating loss of 450 million yen, but inquiries from manufacturers are increasing, and the company expects to turn profitable in 2025. Company-wide R&D expenses totaled 3.20 billion yen, representing 16.8% of revenue, to accelerate the development of next-generation AI technologies.
